TOPIC: Error_codes.htm
========================================
Export Status Codes
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Export Status Codes
 
Export status codes are reported in the optional status variable that is part of the Export configuration.
 
 
 
 
 

 Code
 Status
 
 

 0
 Successful
 
 

 1
 Running
 
 

 2
 Not generated by this server 
 
 

 3
 Canceled
 
 

 10
 General failure
 
 

 11
 Failure - Bad configuration
 
 

 12
 Failure - Failed historical request
 
 

 13
 Canceled - Export file already exists
 
 

 14
 Failure - In demo mode


========================================
TOPIC: Error_handling.htm
========================================
Error handling in VBA
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Error handling in VBA
 
Why is error handling important?
 
If no provision is taken in the code, when a VBA application encounters an error, script execution is stopped and an error box is displayed providing direct access to the VBA editor for debug. Show picture

 
With PcVue, if you have a Runtime license, this error box is disabled as it would give the user access to the VBA editor. Therefore, if there is an error, and no error handling is implemented, the script will stop and the user may be unaware. To avoid this you should add an error handler to all but the simplest of scripts.
 
How to add an error handler to your scripts
 
There are two parts to adding an error handler to a script.
 
 
You must tell the VBA engine that you have included an error handler.
 
You must provide the error handling code.
 
 
To tell VBA engine that you have included an error handler you use the On Error statement. The On Error statement points to a block of code to which the runtime will jump when it encounters an error. A simple example is shown below.
 
Sub OnErrorStatementDemo()
 
 On Error GoTo ErrorHandler &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; ' Enable error-handling routine. 
 
 Open "TESTFILE" For Output As #1 ' Open file for output.
 
 Kill "TESTFILE" &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; ' Attempt to delete an open file to force error.
 
 Exit Sub &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; ' Exit to avoid handler.
 
&#160;
 
 ErrorHandler: &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; ' Error-handling routine.
 
 &#160;Select Case Err.Number &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; ' Evaluate error number.
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;Case 55 &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;' "File already open" error.
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;Close #1 &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;' Close open file
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;Case Else
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;' Handle other situations here...
 
&#160;&#160;End Select
 
&#160;Resume &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;' Resume execution at same line that caused the error.
End Sub

 

 
A full explanation of error handling in VBA is beyond the scope of this help. For more information, see Microsoft's help for VBA or one of the many available resources.


========================================
TOPIC: EVENT_Example.htm
========================================
EVENT example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
EVENT Examples
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example 1
 
This example exercises several modes of the EVENT command. 
 
 
It calls modules 'test' and 'test2' of the Beep.svs program.
 
'Variables used:
 
'@STATE01 of type STATE
 
'@AUTHORISATION of type STATE
 
&#160;
 
'Mode ADDPROG or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB eventaddprog()
 
'Declare variable
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
intResult = EVENT("ADDPROG","@STATE01","ALL>ALL","beep.svs","","test" 
 
&#160;&#160;,"1000,100" ,"@AUTHORISATION" );
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DEL or Mode 5 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB eventdel()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = EVENT("DEL","@STATE01","ALL>ALL","beep.svs","","test");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DELALL or Mode 6 (syntax 3)
 
&#160;
 
SUB eventdelall()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = EVENT("DELALL");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode ADDPROGS or Mode 11 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB eventaddprogs()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = EVENT("ADDPROGS","@STATE01","ALL>ALL","beep.svs","","test2" 
 
&#160;&#160;,"1000,100" ,"@AUTHORISATION" );
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DELPROG or Mode 21 (syntax 4)
 
&#160;
 
SUB eventdelprog()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = EVENT("DELPROG","@STATE01","ALL>ALL","beep.svs","","test");
 
END SUB 
 
This program (Beep.svs) is called by the one above.
 
SUB test()
 
DIM dblfrequency as double; 'in Hz
 
DIM dblduration as double; ' in ms
 
dblfrequency =DVAL(GETARG("ARG1"));
 
dblduration = DVAL(GETARG("ARG2"));
 
BEEP (dblfrequency,dblduration);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB test2()
 
PRINT("starting program test2");
 
END SUB 
 
Example 2 
 
When the routine DEF_EVN is executed in this example, 
 the change of the bit 'DoorState' triggers the execution of the functions 
 TRANS_0 and TRANS_1.
 
'Program "TEST_TR" contains two functions.
SUB TRANS_1( )
PRINT("Open The Door");
END SUB
 

 
SUB TRANS_0( )
PRINT("Shut That Door!");
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Program "DEF_EVN" contains :
SUB MAIN( )
IF (PROGRAM("IS_LOADED","TEST_TR","")==0) THEN
PROGRAM("PRELOAD","TEST_TR","");
ENDIF
EVENT("ADDPROG","DoorState",0,"TEST_TR","","TRANS_0");
EVENT("ADDPROG","DoorState",1,"TEST_TR","","TRANS_1");
END SUB

TOPIC: Excel_templates.htm
========================================
Using Excel workbook templates with Data Export 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Using Excel workbook Templates with Data Export 
 
The workbook generated by the Data export has, by default, no formatting. To automatically format the output you can specify an Excel workbook template in the Export Configuration dialog. The template is applied to the workbook when it is generated. Show picture

 

 
The name of the worksheet or worksheets in the workbook template must match those being generated by the Export. This implies that you must use either fixed text as the worksheet name or an expression whose output does not change each time the worksheet is generated.
 
 

 
Using a template will clear data from any existing sheet even if the Clear if sheet exists property is not set.

TOPIC: EXCELTOBUF_Example.htm
========================================
EXCELTOBUF example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
EXCELTOBUF Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example
 
'-------- Simple example of the use of ExcelToBuf
 
Sub ExcelToBuf1()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lHandle As Long;
 
&#160;&#160;Dim cLineSepa As Str, cColSepa As Str;
 
&#160;&#160;Dim iFirstRow As Integer, iFirstCol As Integer, iLastRow As Integer, iLastCol As Integer;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;cLineSepa = ";";' Separators can only be a single char
 
&#160;&#160;cColSepa = ",";
 
&#160;&#160;iFirstCol = 1;' First and last column and row are optional
 
&#160;&#160;iFirstRow = 1;
 
&#160;&#160;iLastCol = 10;
 
&#160;&#160;iLastRow = 2;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lHandle = ExcelToBuf("Book1.xlsx", "Sheet1", cLineSepa, cColSepa, iFirstRow, iFirstCol, iLastRow, iLastCol);
 
&#160;&#160;BufToFile(lHandle, "Output.txt");	'Dump to text file to check result
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;Print("Buffer size was ", Seq_Buffer("LEN", lHandle));
 
&#160;&#160;Free_Buffer(lHandle);End Sub
 
End Sub


========================================
TOPIC: Export (2).htm
========================================
How to export a configuration subset
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to export a configuration subset
 
The Export feature of the Application Architect makes it possible to export a subset of the configuration elements you have modeled. It facilitates the reuse of application functions in other projects, reinventing therefore, the way projects are configured. This feature also enables the creation of large projects out of smaller ones, thus improving the production and generation of projects.
 
The configuration subset you export can contains a combination of various elements such as parameters, templates, or instances from one or more libraries.
 
 

 
From the PcVue menu, go to Configure.Application Architect and select the tab containing the elements to export. You can export instances, templates or parameters.
 
 

 
Right-click the elements you want to export then click Add to exported items.
 
 

 
Select the Export tab. All added elements are listed. You can also check, uncheck and remove the elements from the list using buttons in the toolbar. The list is saved in the CurrentExportList.xml file in the TP directory of the project and is loaded when opening the Application Architect. Show picture
 

 
 
 

 
If your engineering workflow includes re-iterating exports for different subsets of configuration, you have what it takes to manage several custom export lists:
 
 

 
You can save the export list using the Save button. This will create a file in a folder and under a name of your choice that can later be reused.
 
 

 
You can also reload a previously saved list by clicking the Load button. This will replace the current list of elements chosen for export.
 
 
 
 
 

 
Select the file path and enter the file name in which your configuration will be exported. An empty path exports the file in the TP folder.
 
 

 
From the Dependencies box, choose whether to export all related dependencies or none. Dependencies are elements that are needed to create elements you have selected for export such as:
 
 

 
A parameter used in an expression defined in the property of an element you want to export.
 
 

 
A parameter and/ or a template included in another template you want to export.
 
 

 
A parameter and/ or a template in an instance you want to export.
 
 
 

 
The elements you have selected and their children elements are exported in all cases.

 
 

 
Select None if the elements you want to export are only dependent on elements that are already in the configuration where you will import. For example, if you export instances with the goal of importing them in a project where the necessary templates and parameters are already defined, you will eventually select None.
 
 

 
Select All dependencies if you want to export the definition of all the elements necessary to re-compose the elements you have selected for export. For example, if you export a template, the definition of any parameter or included template referenced in the exported template will only be exported if you select All dependencies.
 
 
 
 
 

 
Click Export to export the list of elements you have selected. The exported file can then be used as input to the Import feature in another PcVue project.
 
 
 

 
It is important to save your environment regularly by clicking File in upper-left menu then Save.


========================================
TOPIC: Exporting_strings_from_single_mimic_template_or_symbol.htm
========================================
Exporting strings from a single mimic, mimic template or symbol
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Exporting Strings from a Single Mimic, Mimic Template
 or Symbol
 
This process exports the strings from a single element
 (mimic, mimic template or symbol). Before you use Import/Export for a
 single item, you must first configure the Import/Export Tool to select
 the destination import/export file and export language. See the topic Configuring the Import/Export tool.
 

 
If the Import/Export options are grayed out in the main
 menu or pop-up, ensure that the type of process (mimic, mimic template
 or symbol) is enabled and that the import/export file names are configured
 in the Translation Import/Export Tool dialog.
 
 
How to export the strings from a single mimic or mimic
 template
 
 
Open the mimic or
 mimic template and select Design mode. If the mimic was already open, make
 sure that any changes that have been made are saved.
 
Right-click on the
 mimic and, from the pop-up menu, select Mimic Translations.Export. 
 
 
The export process proceeds without further interaction
 with the user unless any errors are encountered.
 

 
You can also select the Export
 command from the menu using Window.Mimic Translations.Export.
 
 
How to export the strings from a single symbol
 
 
Open any mimic or
 mimic template that contains an instance of the symbol.
 
Right-click on the
 symbol. From the pop-up menu, select Symbol Translations.Export. 
 
 
The export process proceeds without further interaction
 with the user unless any errors are encountered.
 

 
You can also select the export
 command from the menu using Edit.Symbol Translations.Export.


========================================
TOPIC: Exporting_the_strings_for_an_entire_library.htm
========================================
Exporting the strings for an entire library
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Exporting the Strings for an Entire Library
 
This process exports the strings from an entire library 
 (local, common or custom).
 
How to export the strings from one or more libraries
 
 
Open the Import/Export 
 Tool using the menu command Display.Translation Import/Export.
 
If you have not already 
 done so, configure the file paths and export languages. See the topic
Configuring the Import/Export tool.
 
Select the library 
 or libraries for export. You can select more than one library by clicking 
 while the Ctrl key is held down.
 
Start the export 
 process using either the Export-NEW or Export-UPDATE button. 
 
The export 
function processes all the mimic, template and/or symbol files in the selected libraries. For large projects, this may take several seconds to run.
 
 

 
If any mimics are open at the 
 time of the export the process will display a warning dialog then automatically 
 close them.
 
 

 
Any changes you make to the 
 Import/Export Tool configuration are only temporary unless you click the 
 Save button.
 
 
Canceling the export process
 
You can cancel the export process at any time during 
 its execution by clicking the Export button again (which is renamed Stop 
 during the export process). The export will stop but changes made to the 
 text files prior to clicking Stop are not undone.
 
What is the difference between Export-NEW and Export-UPDATE?
 
 
Export-NEW - Creates 
 new import/export files. If a file of the same name already exists you 
 will be asked whether you want to overwrite it or cancel the export
 
Export-UPDATE - Updates 
 entries in existing import/export files and adds any new information. 
 Items that have been deleted from the project since the previous export 
 are not deleted from the text files.
 
 
Checking the Status Log
 
When the export process has completed, you should check 
 the status log in the lower pane of the Import Export Tool dialog for 
 any errors. Show picture 
 
If you want 
 a permanent record of the process or want to search for something in particular, you can copy the contents of the status log by using Ctrl+A and Ctrl+C 
 and then pasting it into a text editor such as Windows Notepad.


========================================
TOPIC: Express_mode_Command_line_options.htm
========================================
Command line options
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Command Line Arguments
 
When using Express mode of Database Import you add several arguments to the command line used to start the Database Import tool. The arguments replace information that would have been supplied by the wizard. Some of the arguments also require a parameter string. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 String
 Mandatory
 Meaning
 
 
 
 

 /F
 <filename>
 
 Yes
 
 
This causes the import to start in Command Line mode rather than starting the wizard.
 
<filename> identifies the configuration file to use. See Configuration file format.
 
 
 

 /C
 -
 No
 Automatically closes the Import dialog when the import process is complete.
 
 

 /S
 <start_time>
 
 Yes
 The starting date and time for selecting data to import. See information on data and time format below.
 
 

 /E
 <end-time>
 
 Yes
 The ending date and time for selecting data to import. See information on the date and time format below.
 
 

 /L
 <language>
 No
 To display the dialog using a particular language. If this is omitted, the default language is used.
 
 

 /R
 <suffix>
 
 No
 To specify and add a suffix to the log list name.
 
 

 /?
 -
 No
 To display a Help pop-up for the syntax of the command line.
 
 
 
 

 
Any string containing a space must be enclosed in quotation marks, for example: "19-01-2009 12:00:00"
 
 
Example
 
DBImport.exe /S="01/02/2009 00:00:00" /E="02/02/2009 00:00:00" /L=FR /F=C:\conf.xml /C
 
Date and time format
 
The string representing the date and time can take the following forms. 
 
 
 A string with a date and a time component.

Example: 25/01/2009 17:35:00 
 
A string with a date but no time component.
Example: 17/12/2008
 
A string that includes time zone information and conforms to the ISO 8601 time format. 
Example: 2008-11-01T19:35:00.0000000Z
 
 
A string that includes the GMT designator and conforms to the RFC 1123 time format. 
Example:

 Sat, 01 Nov 2008 19:35:00 GMT
 
A string that includes the date and time along with time zone offset information.
Example:

 03/01/2009 05:42:00 -5:00
 
 

 
The time and date can be in any format supported by the .Net Framework class library method DateTime.Parse.
 
 
Returned error codes
 
The following error codes are returned which can be useful if running DBImport.exe from a batch file.
 
 0 = Success.
 
1 = Success with duplicated lines (if one or more imported records were already in the destination table).
 
2 = Failed or canceled.


========================================
TOPIC: Express_mode_Configuration_file_format.htm
========================================
Configuration file format
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Configuration File Format
 
The file used with the Database Import Tool when used in Express mode is in XML format. For those familiar with XML and with access to an XML editor the Schema is provided below. For those not familiar with XML it is possible to take the example shown below, copy and paste it into a text editor, carefully make the changes required and save it as a file with a .XML extension.
 
The configuration file defines an import task in the following terms.
 
 
Source directory - The folder containing the files to be imported.
 
Destination connection string - The SQL connection string including the SQL Server name, database name and table name.
 
Purge task - To purge or not purge the table before import.
 
 

 
A single configuration file can define one import task or several.
 
 
Example of a Configuration file
 
This example defines the source folder, the destination connection string and table. A purge of the destination table precedes the import.
 
<?xml version="1.0" ?>
<!-- DB Import : Express mode configuration file -->
<ExpressModeConfiguration>
<ImportElements>
<ImportElement>
<Source Directory = "C:\Program Files\SV 8.2\Databases\Export\DB_IMPORT_DB1\TRENDTABLE1"/>
<Destination ConnectionString="Data Source=.\SQLEXPRESS;
 Initial Catalog=DB_IMPORT_DB1;
 Integrated Security=True;
 Persist Security Info=False"
 TableName="TRENDTABLE2" />
<Tasks Purge="True" />
 </ImportElement>
</ImportElements>
</ExpressModeConfiguration>
 
Schema
 
If you want to generate the configuration file using an XML editor it must comply with the following schema: Show picture

TOPIC: EXPRESSION_Example.htm
========================================
EXPRESSION example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
EXPRESSION Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises several modes of the EXPRESSION 
 command. 
 
'Variables used:
 
'@STATE01 of type STATE
 
'@STATE02 of type STATE
 
'@AUTHORISATION of type STATE
 
'@REGISTER01 of type REGISTER
 
'@REGISTER02 of type REGISTER
 
&#160;
 
'contents of a file model:
 
'EXPRM,"MODEL 01","STATE01 or STATE02",,,0,0,"expression model no01"
 
'EXPRM,"MODEL 02","REGISTER01 + REGISTER02",,,0,0,"expression model no02"
 
'contents of a file model:
 
'EXPRV,@STATE02,NOT @STATE01 ,,,0,0,comment expression01
 
'EXPRV,@REGISTER02,@REGISTER01 + 21,,,0,0, register variables 
 
&#160;
 
'Mode IMPORT_FILE_TEMP or Mode 0 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB expressionimportfiletemp()
 
'Declare variables
 
&#160;&#160;DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM strString01 as STR;
 
&#160;&#160;strString01 = "file model expressions.txt";
 
&#160;&#160;intResult = EXPRESSION("IMPORT_FILE_TEMP",strString01);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode IMPORT_BUFFER_TEMP or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB expressionimportbuffertemp()
 
'Declare variables
 
&#160;&#160;DIM lngBuffer as long;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM strString01 as STR;
 
' Format for version 10 onwards
 
&#160;&#160;strString01 = "EXPRESSIONTEMPLATE,Expression01,,0,0,,,1,,@Bit01 OR @Bit02";
 
&#160;&#160;lngBuffer = ALLOC_BUFFER(1000);
 
&#160;
 
'Put the strString01 into the buffer
 
&#160;&#160;PUT_BUFFER(lngbuffer,0,strString01);
 
&#160;&#160;intResult = EXPRESSION("IMPORT_BUFFER_TEMP",lngBuffer);
 
&#160;&#160;FREE_BUFFER(lngBuffer);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode IMPORT_FILE_ONVAR or Mode 2 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB expressionimportfileonvar()
 
'Declare variables
 
&#160;&#160;DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM strString01 as STR;
 
&#160;&#160;strString01 = "file expressions.txt";
 
&#160;&#160;intResult = EXPRESSION("IMPORT_FILE_ONVAR",strString01);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode IMPORT_BUFFER_ONVAR or Mode 3 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB expressionimportbufferonvar()
 
'Declare variables
 
&#160;&#160;DIM lngBuffer as long;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM strString01 as STR;
 
' Format for version 10.0 onwards
 
&#160;&#160;strString01 = "EXPRESSIONONVAR,Expression02,,0,0,,,1,,Register01,,@Register02 + @Register03";
 
&#160;&#160;lngBuffer = ALLOC_BUFFER(1000);
 
&#160;
 
'Put the string strString01 into the buffer
 
&#160;&#160;PUT_BUFFER(lngbuffer,0,strString01);
 
&#160;&#160;intResult = EXPRESSION("IMPORT_BUFFER_ONVAR",lngBuffer);
 
&#160;&#160;FREE_BUFFER(lngBuffer);
 
END SUB

TOPIC: Expressions_overview.htm
========================================
Expression engine overview
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Expression Engine Overview
 
The Expression Engine provides the capability for PcVue to calculate a value, using variables, functions and operators, without the need to use one of the programming languages. For example:
 
EXP(LOG(INLET.PRESSURE)/2)
 
Expressions can be used in the following contexts.
 
 
To compute the value of a variable based on a combination of constant values and the value of other variables. Known as Expression On Variable.
 
To compute a value for display in a mimic. Either by entering an expression directly in the variable field of an animation, or by creating an Expression Template.
 
In the Application Architect for the purpose of differentiation by computing property values at generation time.
 
In an add-on developed using the SvMgr toolkit. For example, the MQTT add-on makes extensive use of the Json function for message payload deserialization and mapping to variable properties (VTQ).
 
In Data Export for computing Folder, Workbook, Template and Sheet names.
 
In the mapping of Sql variables to define how variable properties and fields returned by Sql requests are linked together.
 
 
For ease of use, an Expression Editor is provided when configuring Expression On Variable, Expression Templates, in the Application Architect, in the Sql variables mapping and in the Data Export. The Expression Editor includes features such as keyword auto-completion. The available variables, functions and operators depend on the context in which the Expression Editor is used. See Using the expression editor for more information. Show picture
 

 

 

 
 

 
 
 

 
When entering an expression directly in an animation, or for configuring an add-on developed with the SV Manager SDK, the expression must be entered directly by typing in the string.

TOPIC: Extraction_of_Two_Drawing_Elements.htm
========================================
Extraction of two drawing elements
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Extraction of Two Drawing Elements
 
The Extraction tool creates a polygon 
 that is equivalent subtracting the overlapping area of one drawing element 
 from the other.
 

 
 
 
How to use the extraction tool
 
 
Select the first 
 drawing element by clicking with the left mouse button.
 
Select the second 
 drawing element holding the Shift button so that both drawing elements 
 are selected.
 
Select the Extraction 
 tool from the Arrange toolbar. A new polygon is created taking the color 
 from the second shape.
 
Click and drag the 
 polygon to its location. The original drawing elements remain unchanged.


========================================
TOPIC: Failed_login_attempts.htm
========================================
Managing failed login attempts
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Managing Failed Login Attempts
 

 
The following behavior is only exhibited when the advanced security strategy is enabled.
 
 
A failed login attempt is registered when a user attempts 
 to login with any of the following errors.
 
 
The user name is 
 misspelt.
 
The password is misspelt.
 
The user account 
 has been deleted.
 
The user account 
 is deactivated.
 
 
After the maximum number of failed login attempts configured in the User Account Settings (default 3) is reached, the User Account is 
 locked and can only be unlocked by a user with a superior profile level. 
 Locked accounts are indicated in the Application Explorer with a 
 padlock symbol adjacent to the user name. If you attempt to login 
 using a locked account a dialog will be displayed requesting an administrator 
 to unlock it. To avoid the possibility of the system becoming completely 
 inaccessible, a user with a profile level of 0 is never locked. 
 
How an administrator unlocks a locked user account
 
The following explanation assumes the user following 
 the procedure has administration rights.
 
 
Display the Logon 
 dialog (F2) and enter the locked user's name. Click the OK button. A dialog 
 will appear informing you that the account is locked and that an administrator 
 is required to unlock it.
 
Click the Unlock 
 button, select your name and enter your password. Click the Unlock button 
 again. A further dialog will appear displaying the locked user's properties.
 
Enter a new temporary 
 password for the locked account in both the Password and Password verification 
 fields. Click the OK button.
 
Click Cancel in the 
 Login dialog to close it. 
 
 
Show picture
 
 

 

 
 
 
 

 

 
A User Account that has been unlocked is treated the same 
 as a new user account. That is, the first time a user logs in he/she will 
 have to first enter the temporary password allocated by the administrator, then enter and confirm a new password that only he/she will know.
 
 

 
System variables are available for tracing failed login attempts.


========================================
TOPIC: FAQs.htm
========================================
Network applications FAQ
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Networked Applications - Frequently Asked Questions
 
What is a station?
 
A station is an instance of the PcVue software main process running on a given computer.
 
What is a node?
 
A node defines the network access of a station. The node defines the connection type (TCP/IP or NetBios), 
 the connection properties and if the station is to behave as a client 
 or server or both.
 
Can I configure more than one node per station?
 
It is possible to configure up to four nodes for each 
 station. Additional nodes can be used for network redundancy or if a station requires access various sub-networks.
 
What protocol can I use?
 

 &#160;PcVue's networking can use either NetBios 
 or TCP/IP (recommended).
 
Can I mix NetBios and TCP/IP nodes on the same station?
 
Yes, it is possible to mix NetBios and TCP/IP nodes on 
 the same station.
 
How is a station identified?
 
Each station is identified by a unique station name and number.
 
 
 If you are using TCP/IP a station's name and number is linked to its TCP/IP address or Computer Name. 
If you are using NetBios a station�s name and number is linked to a LANA number.
 
What is a server station?
 
A server station is a station that sends changes in value 
 of one or more of its variables to other (client) stations. The 
 origin of the value could be one of PcVue's Data Acquisition sources or internally generated (Expression, SCADA Basic etc.)
 
The mechanism of sending changes in value is known as 
 producing. In general, the value of a variable can only be produced on one station 
 at any one time.
 
What is a client station?
 
A client station is a station where the value of one 
 or more of its variables is obtained from another (server) station. 
 The mechanism of obtaining values in this way is known as consuming. The 
 value of a variable can be consumed on many stations.
 
Can a station be both client and server?
 
A station can be both client and server producing some 
 variable values and consuming others.
 
Can the value of a variable be changed on a client 
 station?
 
The value of a variable can be changed on a client station 
 if the variable's control attribute is enabled. The value can be changed 
 by either a send type animation or a program. The client station sends 
 the server station a request to change the value. The server station changes 
 the value in its variables tree and then distributes it as it would any other 
 value change. The client station does not change the value directly.
 
How is a link between a server station and a client 
 station established?
 
The client is responsible for establishing the link to 
 the server. The server has no knowledge of the client until it makes a 
 request for connection.
 
How does a station know that it is to produce or consume 
 the value of a particular variable?
 
When a variable is created, it is allocated to a client 
 list and a server list. 
 
 
If the name of the 
 local station appears in a variable's server list, the variable's value 
 is to be produced on that station.
 
If the name of the 
 local station appears in a variable's client list, the variable's value 
 is to be consumed on that station.
 
If the name of the 
 local station does not appear in either the variable's client or server 
 list, the variable is not loaded and is not available on that station.
 
If the server list and the client list of a variable are not empty (not defined), this variable will produced and consumed locally on every station. Its value will not be shared among stations.
 
 
What is an Association?
 
An Association is a group of two or more servers operating 
 together to provide redundant operation. As far as the client is concerned, 
 an Association is treated in the same way as if it were just a single 
 server station.
 
What are the station lists?
 
A station list contains the name of one or more stations. 
 Station lists may contain the names of only server stations, only client 
 stations or both.
 

 
It is essential to understand the use of the station lists 
 as they are used to control the behavior of a number of aspects of PcVue's so that the same project can be used on all 
 stations in a multi-station application.
 
 
How do I develop the projects for each station?
 
Projects are developed in the normal way. Careful use 
 of the client and server station lists will allow an identical project 
 to be used for all stations in the majority of cases. 
 
Does the network configuration have to be the same 
 on all stations in the network?
 
Yes, the network configuration should be the same on each station, except in some very specific cases.

========================================
TOPIC: Filter_Expressions.htm
========================================
Native filter expressions
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Native Filter Expressions
 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
The PcVue native filter expressions can be used with the following instructions:
 
 
ALARMDISPLAY
 
LOGDISPLAY
 
SVALA
 
SVLOG
 
SVTREND
 
SVBRANCH
 
 
A filter starts with an equals sign and is followed by one or more expressions combined with the logical operators. Each expression comprises an attribute, an operator and a value all enclosed in brackets.
 
For example, =(#t BEG ALM) would include all variable names (#t) beginning (BEG) with the text ALM.
 
The following symbols are supported:
 
 
 
 
 

 Symbol
 Description
 
 

 #t
 The variable's name
 
 

 #A1 to #A16
 The variable's extended text attributes. #A1 and #A2 are Domain and Nature respectively
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 #T
 Variable label (description).
 
 

 
 
#B
 
 
 
Boolean attribute (as a word).
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 Operators
 Description
 
 

 ==
 Equal to
 
 

 >
 Greater than
 
 

 <
 Less than
 
 

 >=
 Greater than or equal to
 
 

 <=
 Less than or equal to.
 
 

 ^ or BEG
 Beginning with "string"
 
 

 $ or INC
 Including "string"
 
 

 [ or END
 Ending with string
 
 

 &&
 Logical AND used between expressions
 
 

 ||
 Logical OR used between expressions
 
 
 
 
Examples of filter expressions
 
 
 
 
 

 Filter
 Description
 
 

 =(#t BEG FACTORY)&&((#A1 == DOM1)||(#A1 == DOM2))
 The name of the variable starts with FACTORY and the Domain is either DOM1 or DOM2.
 
 

 =(#A1 == DOM1)||(#A2 == NAT1)
 The Domain is DOM1 or the Nature is NAT1.
 
 

 =(#A5 >=10)&&(#A5 < 35)
 The attribute A5 is greater than or equal to 10 and less than 35.
 
 
 
 

 
Note the use of brackets in the first example to control the order in which the expressions are evaluated.
 
 

 
It is recommended to enclose text values with quotation marks, and it is mandatory for the expression to work as expected if the value contains a special character such as a space, a comma or a quotation mark. For example: (#T BEG "Urgent alarm").

TOPIC: Filtering_and_scheduling.htm
========================================
Filtering and Scheduling the Replication
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Filtering and Scheduling the Replication
 
This continues with the Replication Setup Wizard from 
 the previous topic. The process of configuring the filtering and scheduling 
 is as follows. Show flowchart
 

 

 
 
 
 
If at any interim stage you wish to change your choices, 
 select the Back button and repeat the previous steps.
 
The Running Options dialog enables you to filter the 
 data and to schedule the replication. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
On demand execution
 
 
In the Running Options 
 dialog, select one of the options in the On Demand section.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Option
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Time-based absolute filter
 
 
 
Select data between two dates.
 
 
 

 
 
Time-based filter
 
 
 
Select data in a duration.
 
 
 

 
 
All data
 
 
 
No filter - all data are passed.
 
 
 

 Delete pending temporary records before starting
 Force deletion of records of a previously generated TempTimeTable table to prevent resuming steps of a previous, but failed, replication.
 
 
 
 
Click on Next.
 
 
If you chose All Data, you are asked to 
 confirm the configuration.
 
If you chose Time based absolute filter, 
 the dialog Data to Replicate opens. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
If you chose Time based filter, the dialog 
 Data to Replicate opens. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
 
Use the drop-down 
 boxes to set a start date and time and an end date and time. Click on 
 Next.
 
 
Scheduled execution
 

 
These options only apply if Microsoft SQL Server is used. 
 In Microsoft SQL Server Express Edition, only the On Demand options 
 are available.
 
 
 
Select one of the 
 options.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Option
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Time based filter
 
 
 
Select data in a duration.
 
 
 

 
 
All data
 
 
 
No filter - all data are passed.
 
 
 
 
 
Click on Next. If 
 you selected All Data, this takes you to the dialog Schedule Your Application, 
 below Step 3. If you chose Select Time Based Filter, the dialog Time Based Filter 
 opens. Show picture

 
 
 
The dialog Schedule 
 Your Application opens. Show picture

 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Section
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
General 
 
 
 
Set a start date and time.
 
 
 

 
 
Repeat 
 
 
 
Set the period (in hours/days/weeks/months/years - or 
 None).
 
 
 

 
 
End 
 
 
 
Optionally set an ending date and time.
 
 
 
 
 
Configure these options 
 then click on Next.
 
 
Confirming the configuration
 
 
After the dialogs 
 for Scheduled or On Demand Execution, a confirmation dialog opens. Click 
 on Finish to complete the configuration. Show picture

 
 
The configured replication process appears in the Database 
 Manager. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Where the configuration is stored
 

 
At this stage, a configuration file has been created, but 
 the replication process has not been sent to the SQL server nor launched, 
 even for the option Run Now.
 
 
The configuration file is named REPLICATIONCONFIGS.XML 
 and is stored in the BIN folder of the software.
 

 
The periodic execution is not launched automatically on 
 SQL Server Express Edition.
 
 
SQL Server Express Edition does not include the Scheduler 
 (an SQL Agent) for doing so. If you are using SQL Server Express Edition 
 and you wish to use a Scheduled replication, please use the command line 
 tool as described in the topic Using 
 the Command Line Tool.

========================================
TOPIC: FOR_NEXT.htm
========================================
FOR ... NEXT
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 FOR ... NEXT
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Repeat a block of instructions a specified number of 
 times in a loop.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
FOR(expression1;expression2; [expression3])
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;[block of instructions]
 
NEXT
 
There is no return type.
 
Execution
 
Expression1 
 is evaluated when the Execution of the loop starts, to provide the initial 
 state of the loop.
 
Expression2 
 is the loop test. The loop will repeat whilst the condition is true.
 
Expression3 
 provides the update or increment for the loop.
 
Example
 
SUB Main()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM i as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
'Integer i increases from 0 to 7 by steps of 2
 
For (i=0;i<7;i=i+2)
 
&#160;&#160;Print(i); 
 
Next 
 
END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: FORMATX_Example.htm
========================================
FORMAT example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FORMAT Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example shows how the FORMAT command displays various text strings.
 
SUB MAIN ()
 
&#160;&#160;DIM count as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM i as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM ch97 as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM ch122 as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM s as str;
 
&#160;&#160;DIM fp as double;
 
&#160;&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;count = -9234;
 
&#160;&#160;i = 1;
 
&#160;&#160;ch97 = 97;
 
&#160;&#160;ch122 = 122;
 
&#160;&#160;s="Hello world!";
 
&#160;&#160;fp = 251.7366;
 
&#160;&#160;
 
'-------- Display integer
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Display integer");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Decimal: %d", count)); 'Decimal: -9234
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Decimal: %i", count)); 'Decimal: -9234
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Justified: %.6d", count)); 'Justified: -009234
 
 &#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Unsigned: %u", count)); ' Unsigned: 4294958062
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(Format("Short integer: %.2d", i)); 'Decimal: 01
 
&#160;
 
'-------- Display hexadecimal
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Display hexadecimal");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Hex: %Xh", count)); 'Hex: FFFFDBEEh
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("hex: 0x%x", count)); 'hex: 0xffffdbee
 
 
 
'-------- Display octal
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Display octal");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Octal: %o", count)); 'Octal: 37777755756
 
 
 
'-------- Display characters
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Display characters");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Character code 97: %c", ch97)); 'a
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Character code 97: %C", ch97)); 'a
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("Character code 122: %c", ch122)); 'z
 
 
 
'-------- Display string / text
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Display string / text");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%s", s)); 'Hello world!
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%.9s", s)); 'Hello wor
 
&#160;&#160;
 
'-------- Display real number
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Real numbers:");
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%f", fp)); '251.736600
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%.2f ",fp)); '251.74
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%e", fp)); '2.517366e+002
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(FORMAT("%E", fp)); '2.517366E+002
 
END SUB

TOPIC: FORMULA_Example.htm
========================================
FORMULA example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FORMULA Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example exercises modes of the FORMULA command.
 
It needs a text file "importFormula.txt" to
 be in the TP folder, formatted like FORMULA.DAT.
 

 
For a full path use "\\" instead of "\",
 e.g. "C:\\SV\\util.txt".
 
Always put a return character at the end of each line
 in FORMULA.DAT, else the formula is ignored.
 
 
'Variables used in the formulas:
 
'@BRCH1.REGISTER01 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH2.REGISTER01 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH3.REGISTER01 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH1.REGISTER02 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH2.REGISTER02 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH3.REGISTER02 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH1.REGISTER03 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH2.REGISTER03 of type REGISTER
 
'@BRCH3.REGISTER03 of type REGISTER
 
&#160;
 
'Mode ADD or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formulaadd()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngbuffer1 as long;
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
DIM strFilename as Str;
 
strFilename = "Formula.txt";
 
lngbuffer1 = filetobuf(strFilename);
 
intResult = FORMULA("ADD",lngbuffer1);
 
FREE_BUFFER(lngbuffer1);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode ENABLE or Mode 2 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formulaenable()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = FORMULA("ENABLE", "formula01", "BRCH1");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DISABLE or Mode 3 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formuladisable()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = FORMULA("DISABLE", "formula01", "BRCH1");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DEL or Mode 4 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formuladel()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = FORMULA("DEL", "formula01", "BRCH1");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DELALL or Mode 4 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formuladelall()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = FORMULA("DELALL");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode DELALL or Mode 4 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB formuladelall2()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
'intResult = FORMULA("DELALL",3); 'Delete computational formulas
 
intResult = FORMULA("DELALL",14);
 
END SUB

TOPIC: FPUTC_Example.htm
========================================
FPUTC example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FPUTC Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises modes of the FPUTC command.
 

 
For a full path use "\\" instead of "\", 
 e.g. "C:\\SV\\tool.txt".
 
 
To run, it needs a text file "tool.txt" to 
 be stored in the TP folder of the project.
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM intResult as INTEGER;
 
DIM intEOF as INTEGER;
 
DIM StrAccess as STR;
 
DIM StrFilename as STR;
 
DIM StrChar1 as STR;
 
&#160;
 
StrFilename = "doc1.txt"; 'name of the file to open in the TP folder
 
StrAccess = "w+"; ' opening in Write mode 
 
&#160;
 
' opening of the file 
 
intResult = FOPEN(StrFilename , StrAccess);
 
If (intResult ==1) Then
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Opening the file ", StrFilename," succeeded");
 
End If
 
StrChar1= "A";
 
intResult = FPUTC(StrFilename,StrChar1);
 
If (intResult ==1) Then
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Writing succeeded.");
 
End If
 
&#160;
 
'Closing the file 
 
intResult = FCLOSE(StrFilename);
 
If (intResult ==1) Then
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Closing of the file ", StrFilename," succeeded");
 
End If
 
END SUB

TOPIC: FREE_BUFFER.htm
========================================
FREE_BUFFER
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FREE_BUFFER
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Release a memory area reserved by an ALLOC_BUFFER.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
FREE_BUFFER(Handle);
 
There is no return type.
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning 
 
 
 

 
 
Handle
 
 
 
The location of the memory buffer as returned by ALLOC_BUFFER.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
The memory buffer allocated by ALLOC_BUFFER and referenced 
 by Handle is released.
 
Example
 
SUB Main()
 
'Declare the return code
 
DIM lngPointer as long;
 
DIM intValue as integer;
 
&#160;
 
intValue = 50;
 
'intValue <=> intValue*4 bytes
 
lngPointer = ALLOC_BUFFER (intvalue);
 
PRINT("pointer =", lngPointer );
 
&#160;
 
'The memory area from ALLOC_BUFFER must always be released after use
 
FREE_BUFFER (lngPointer);
 
END SUB

TOPIC: Free_substitution.htm
========================================
Defining a property by an input
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Defining a Property by an Input
 
When a property value is defined by an input no value, is entered. Instead, the value must be entered for each instance of the template. 
 
How to define a property by an input
 
 
Select the Templates tab.
 
Navigate the templates configuration tree and open the properties grid that contains the property to be defined.
 
Right click on the property name (left column) and, from the pop-up menu, select Define by and Input. The value of the property will change to {?} and it will be marked with a red icon . Show picture 

 
 
How to enter a value for an input (at instantiation)
 
 
Select the Instances tab.
 
Navigate the instances configuration tree and select a template instance. The input are listed in the lower right pane.
 
Select an input and enter the value. Show picture

TOPIC: FSEEK_Example.htm

FSEEK example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FSEEK Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises of modes of the FSEEK command.
 

 
For a full path use "\\" instead of "\", 
 e.g. "C:\\SV\\tool.txt".
 
 
To run, it needs a text file "tool.txt" to 
 be stored in the TP folder of the project.
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM intResult as INTEGER;
 
DIM intEOF as INTEGER;
 
DIM StrAccess as STR;
 
DIM StrFilename as STR;
 
DIM STRLine as STR;
 
&#160;
 
StrFilename = "tool.txt"; 'name of file to open in TP folder
 
StrAccess = "r"; ' opening in Read mode
 
&#160;
 
' opening the file 
 
intResult = FOPEN(StrFilename , StrAccess);
 
If (intResult ==1) Then
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Opening of the file ", StrFilename," succeeded");
 
End If
 
PRINT(FGETC (StrFilename,2));
 
FSEEK(StrFilename,3,0);
 
PRINT(FGETC (StrFilename,2));
 
&#160;
 
' closing the file 
 
intResult = FCLOSE(StrFilename);
 
If (intResult ==1) Then
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Closing of file ", StrFilename," succeeded");
 
End If
 
END SUB

TOPIC: FTP_Example.htm
========================================
FTP example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
FTP Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This program copies a file from and then to a file transfer site.
 
Sub Down ()
 
 Dim cFTPsource As Str, cLocalDestn As Str;
 
 Dim cName As Str, cPass As Str, cStatusVar As Str;
 
 Dim iReturn As Integer;
 
&#160;
 
 cFTPsource = "ftp://ftp.xxxxxxxx.com/test.txt";
 
 cLocalDestn = "test.txt";
 
 cName = "xyz123";
 
 cPass = "abc890";
 
 cStatusVar = "FTPSTATUS";
 
&#160;
 
 iReturn = FTP("DOWNLOAD", cFTPsource, cLocalDestn, 1, cName, cPass, cStatusVar);
 
 Print ("Return from FTP download is ", iReturn);
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub Up ()
 
 Dim cFTPdestn As Str, cLocalSource As Str;
 
 Dim cName As Str, cPass As Str, cStatusVar As Str;
 
 Dim iReturn As Integer;
 
&#160;
 
 cFTPdestn = "ftp://ftp.xxxxxxxx.com/test.txt";
 
 cLocalSource = "test.txt";
 
 cName = "xyz123";
 
 cPass = "abc890";
 
 cStatusVar = "FTPSTATUS";
 
&#160;
 
 iReturn = FTP("UPLOAD", cLocalSource, cFTPdestn, 1, cName, cPass, cStatusVar);
 
 Print ("Return from FTP upload is ", iReturn);
 
End Sub


========================================
TOPIC: Functions.htm
========================================
Functions
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Functions
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
A function is composed of an optional declarations part 
 and a sequence of instructions. The declaration part always comes before 
 the instruction sequence.
 
ExecutionA function may receive up to 12 parameters on entry, with the exception 
 of the main function that receives none. Parameters are passed by value 
 not reference, i.e. a parameter's value is passed, not its name. 

The structure of a function is as follows:
SUB proc name ([parameter list])
 [declaration 1];
 [declaration 2];
 .
 .
 [declaration N];
 [instruction 1];
[instruction 2];
 .
 .
 [instruction N];
END SUB

Instructions and declarations always end with a semicolon.
In some of the examples of code in this Help, an instruction 
 is split across lines. The symbol is used to indicate where that occurs. This is only 
 due to formatting restrictions in this manual. In a real program, a complete instruction 
 must be contained on a single line.

Each program runs in a single thread. This means that 
 two functions from the same program cannot be executed at the same time. 
 Thus, two functions launched with EVENT, CYCLIC, CRONTAB, KEY or SELECTOR 
 cannot run at the same time.
To avoid this situation, the DELAY instruction must be 
 used with extreme caution in event-triggered functions.
Asynchronous calling
The effects of a function call do not occur in a fixed 
 sequence (see the example below).
Example
In this example, program P1 calls program P2 with one 
 argument.
Program P1 :
SUB main()
PROGRAM("FUNCTION","P2","", "message1");
PRINT("message 2");
END SUB
Program P2 :
SUB message1()
PRINT("message1");
END SUB
When program P1 is run, it may display: 
message1
message2
or:
message2
message1


========================================
TOPIC: General_formatting_characters.htm
========================================
Common formatting characters
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Formatting Characters
 
 
I - Used for input 
 formatting only.
 
O - Used for output 
 formatting only.
 
I/O - Used for both 
 input and output formatting.
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
Character
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
""
 
&#160;
 
 
 
Display the string inside the double quotation marks.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
\
 
 
 
Display the following character literally. Do not interpret 
 it as a formatting character.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[=n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is n.Example 
 [=15]#.#
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[#n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is not n. Example [#15]h:n:s
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[<n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is less than n. Example [<15]#.#
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[<=n] or [=<n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is less than or equal to n. Example [<=15]#.#
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[>n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is greater than n. Example [>15]#.#
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
[>=n] or [=>n]
 
 
 
Condition expression to use a format when the value of 
 the variable is greater than or equal to n. Example [>=15]#.#
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
;
 
&#160;
 
 
 
Condition separator - syntax 1. The characters on the 
 left of the semicolon are used to format the value if it is positive and 
 those to the right are used if it is negative. Example "Positive 
 Value";"Negative Value"
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
;
 
 
 
Condition separator - syntax 2. [Condition1]Format1;[Condtion2]Format2;Format3 
 Format1 is used when Condition1 is met, Format2 is used when Condition2 
 is met, else Format3 is used.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
0
 
 
 
Digit placeholder. Display a digit or a zero. If the expression 
 has a digit in the position where the 0 appears in the format string, 
 display it; otherwise, display a zero in that position. If the number 
 has fewer digits than there are zeros (on either side of the decimal) 
 in the format expression, display leading or trailing zeros.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
#
 
 
 
Digit placeholder. Display a digit or nothing. If the 
 expression has a digit in the position where the # appears in the format 
 string, display it; otherwise, display nothing in that position.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
.
 
 
 
Decimal placeholder. The decimal placeholder determines 
 how many digits are displayed to the left and right of the decimal separator.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
%
 
 
 
Percentage placeholder. The expression is multiplied by 
 100. The percent character % is inserted in the position where it appears 
 in the format string.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
~
 
 
 
Display the absolute value (unsigned).
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
,
 
 
 
Thousand separator. The thousand separator separates thousands 
 from hundreds within a number that has four or more places to the left 
 of the decimal separator.


========================================
TOPIC: Generation.htm
========================================
Generation
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Generation
 
Generation is the process of creating or updating the PcVue configuration elements from the Application Architect. Generation can be started from the menu using the Task.Generate command, or by using the keystroke F7. 
 
The Application Architect generates the configuration in PcVue by using the Smart Generator Generic Import (although the Smart Generator itself is not visible during the process). First, an XML file is produced based on the information that has been entered, then the Application Architect starts the synchronization phase of the Generic Import. During this phase, the Generic Import compares the XML file with the previous version and, depending on the differences, creates, modifies or deletes elements of the PcVue configuration.
 
The Generation Options dialog
 
The Generation Options dialog is displayed at the start of the generation process. Show picture

 
 
Check consistency before generation - Execute the consistency checks before generation as configured in the Application Architect Settings dialog. If the consistency checks fail, generation is abandoned.
 
Generate graphical elements - Generate any graphical elements defined in the Application Architect.
 
Fast synchronization - see below.
 
Full synchronization - see below.
 
 
Full synchronization
 
The XML import file created by the Application Architect is compared with the previous file that was imported and all new elements are created and those that no longer exist are deleted.
 
All properties that have been given a value in the Application Architect are generated in the PcVue application. Any of these properties that have been manually changed (using a tool other than the Application Architect) since the previous generation process will be overwritten. Properties that do not have a value or that do not exist in the Application Architect are not affected.
 
Full synchronization can take several minutes as, even if you have only changed a single element, all elements are regenerated.
 
Fast synchronization
 
Fast synchronization is similar to full synchronization except that ONLY properties that have been changed are updated. This is the normal mode that should be used for generation, as it is much quicker than full synchronization. 
 

 
The first time that you run generation, as there is no previous file to compare, all elements and properties are generated.
 
 

 
Properties left undefined in the Application Architect (e.g. left at their default value), are never affected by the generation process whatever their type. It is so possible to define certain properties via the Application Explorer or Smart Generators without having the Application Architect overwriting them.
 
 
Generation results
 
The generation results can be displayed by clicking the >> button in the generation dialog. Results can be sorted by clicking on the appropriate column header. Show picture

 Applies to
 
 
Example
 
This example uses each mode of the GETARG command.
 
'variables
 
'TEXT01 type text
 
'TEXT02 type TEXT
 
'TEXT03 type TEXT
 
'TEXT04 type TEXT
 
&#160;

'How the example works: a branch must be specified

END SUB

'Mode MAINBRANCH or Mode 0 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;

@text01 = strReturn;

END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: Global_Program.htm
========================================
The Global Program
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Global Program
 

 
 

 

 

 

 See also
 
 
The global declaration program is used to declare working 
 variables and functions that are used throughout a project. It does not 
 directly run functions itself. The global program can have any name and its source file is stored in the same folder (P) as the other programs.
 
An example of a simple global program follows.
 
' Declare global working variables 
DIM WatchDog AS SINGLE;
DIM RunTime AS SINGLE;
DIM WindowName AS STR
 
&#160;
 
' Global functions
 
&#160;
 
SUB setdog ( )
 
watchdog = 1;
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB cleardog ( )
 
watchdog = 0;
 
END SUB
 
Essential features to be understood about the global program
 
 
The global program must always be loaded before running any other programs. If you do not load a global program, the first other program that is loaded will be treated as the global program with corresponding differences in behavior.
 
 
Only one global declaration program may be loaded at 
 a time.
 
A global program does not have a MAIN function and can 
 only be loaded. It cannot be run.
 
Any working variable that has been declared in a global 
 program is known to all other programs and is referenced by name only. 
 It must not be declared again in any other program.
 
Any function that has been declared in the global program 
 is known to all other programs and is referenced by name only. It must 
 not be declared again in any other program.
 
The global program may be (re)loaded at any time from 
 the Program Management dialog box. However, re-loading the global program will cause all other programs to stop and be un-loaded.
 
 To load a global program automatically on project startup, add it to the Station Start-up configuration (Application Explorer.Settings.Stations Startup).


========================================
TOPIC: Glossary.htm
========================================
Glossary of 3D terms used in the HMI
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Glossary of 3D Terms Used in the HMI
 
3D model
 
In the HMI, a '3D model' means a DirectX (.x) file. You can use 3D packages and tools to convert models and drawings from other formats to DirectX.
 
Alpha channel
 
A means of blending a degree of transparency with colors, e.g. for water, glass, smoke/fog/mist and surface film effects.
 
Ambient color
 
Defines the color of the light reflected off a lighted object's surface.
 
Azimuth
 
Rotation angle of the camera around its Y axis. For example, if the camera were a person's head, the azimuth angle would be the rotation angle of the head to the left or right.

 
Backface culling
 
A technique to save system resources by only rendering the surfaces (polygons) that are facing toward the camera.

 
Built-in camera model

 
A 3D camera model provided by the HMI.

 
Camera

 
A point of view onto a 3D window.

 
Camera model

 
A 3D model that provides pre-configured parameters for a window camera.

 
Custom camera model

 
Any of the 3D camera models located in the project libraries.

 
Design material

 
A material used to render an object in a window in Design mode.
 
Diffuse color

 
In principle, this is the color of the light reflection on an object under pure white light. It is usually defined as the color of the object itself rather than a reflection.

 
Distance to target

 
Distance between the camera (or eye) and the target.

 
Emissive color

 
Similar to the ambient color except that the object's surface need not be lit.

 
Eye

 
Same as Camera.
 
Frame
 
The hierarchy of components of an object. This has nothing to do with 'frame' in the context of visual animation, e.g. as in an animated GIF file.

 
Gizmo

 
A tool for rotating and scaling a 3D model in Draw mode.
 
Indexed material
 
Material specified in the header of the model file.

 
Local material
 
Material that is part of a mesh's attributes.

 
Material

 
The visual representation of the surface of an object.
 
Mesh

 
The surface attributes of a 3D object.

 
Orthographic

 
Describes a 2D projection of a 3D model without perspective. Parallel lines look parallel and lines of the same length appear with the same length.

 
Pitch

 
Rotation angle of the camera around its X axis. For example, if the camera were a person's head, the pitch angle would be the rotation angle of the head looking up or down.

 
Pivot

 
The 'center' of an object, where the Gizmo tool originates.

 
Roll

 
Rotation angle of the camera around its Z axis.

 
Specular color
 
Defines the highlights on an object, so as to control an object's glossiness and shininess. It is usually set to a brighter shade of the diffuse color. See also: Specular level.

 
Specular level

 
Adjusts the sharpness of the highlights.

 
Surface geometry

 
A collection of faces (polygons) bound together in a particular manner to define the outside of a 3D object.

 
Target

 
The focus point of a camera in the 3D world. Each target axis is always parallel with the corresponding axis in the 3D world.
 
Yaw
 
Rotation angle of the camera around its Y axis.


========================================
TOPIC: Gradient_Register.htm
========================================
Understanding the Gradient Register animation
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Understanding the Gradient Register Animation
 
You can configure a Gradient Register animation as follows: 
 
 
 
Select the drawing 
 element whose color is to be made to vary.
 
In the main menu, 
 select Animate.Color.Gradient Register to open the Drawing Properties 
 dialog with a new tab for Color Gradient Register. Show picture

 
In the Register box, 
 select the variable whose values are to be used to determine the colors. 
 
 
In the Line (or Text) 
 section, with the Range box ticked, click on the Start color to select 
 a color to represent the lowest value of the variable for gradation. 
 See note on 'Value Limits' below.
 
Click on the End 
 color to select a color to represent the highest value of the variable 
 for gradation. See the note on value limits below.
 
If required, set the other properties as described 
 in the table below.
 
Click on OK to close the dialog and apply the 
 settings.
 
 

 
To apply the values in the Line section only, un-tick 
 the boxes in the Pattern section. To apply the values in the Pattern section 
 only, un-tick the boxes in the Line section.
 
If you apply Value Limits, the values outside of those 
 limits are represented by the static colors you specify for them. Otherwise, 
 the graded colors apply to the whole range of values of the register. 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Property
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Register
 
 
 
The variable whose values determine the colors of the 
 drawing element.
 
 
 

 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Value 
 Limits section
 
 
 

 
 
Minimum
 
 
 
The 
 lowest value for grading the colors.
 
 
 

 
 
Maximum
 
 
 
The 
 highest value for grading the colors.
 
 
 

 
 
&#160;
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Colors 
 section
 
 
 
(separately for Pattern and for Line or Text)
 
 
 

 
 
Range
 
 
 
Tick the box to use the Start and End colors.
 
 
 

 
 
Start
 
 
 
A color to represent the lowest permitted value for grading.
 
 
 

 
 
End 
 
 
 
A color to represent the highest permitted value for 
 grading.
 
 
 

 
 
Out of Min Limit
 
 
 
Tick the box and set a color to represent values below 
 the lower limit.
 
 
 

 
 
Out of Max Limit
 
 
 
Tick the box and set a color to represent values above 
 the upper limit.
 
 
 

 
 
Reg.Unavailable
 
 
 
Tick the box and set a color to represent invalid values 
 of the variable, or an invalid variable.
 
 
 
 
Example
 
For example, these settings cause the following Pattern 
 colors to be displayed: Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Value
 
 
 
Color
 
 
 

 
 
100 to 900
 
 
 
Shades of green, graded from light to dark.
 
 
 

 
 
Below 100
 
 
 
Light gray.
 
 
 

 
 
Over 900
 
 
 
Red.
 
 
 

 
 
Unavailable
 
 
 
Magenta.
 
 
 
 
The Line color is always black in this example.
 
Ranges of colors
 
The color for a value of the variable is determined by 
 the value's position in relation to the lower and higher value limits 
 that you configure. That proportion of the scale of values is applied 
 separately to the red, green and blue (RGB) color differences between 
 the Start and End colors. The result of those three components gives the 
 color to display.
 
A simple use of this tool is to show shades of brightness 
 for a particular color. In the example above, the gradation covers shades 
 between the lightest and darkest on the row of green colors on the Color 
 Palette. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
If you choose different colors for the Start and End 
 limits, the effect of gradation will not seem linear. The intermediate 
 colors may look quite different to either end of the range, or (for a 
 range of values) too similar to one of them. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
The dark gray is the middle color between the yellow and the blue.
 
 

 
You can only use plain colors for the Start and End colors 
 of a Gradient Register animation, not gradient or blinking colors.
 
You can configure the animation with a quality 
 sequence from the Sequence Manager.


========================================
TOPIC: Grid.htm
========================================
Drawing Grid
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Drawing Grid
 
The Drawing Grid is a tool to help you precisely position your 
 drawing elements. It is an imaginary matrix of co-ordinates within each 
 mimic. When you create drawing element, for example a rectangle, its size 
 and position will automatically adjust so that each of its corners is 
 located on the nearest grid point. When you move a drawing element, as 
 you release the mouse button, the top left hand corner will automatically 
 move to the nearest grid point.
 
You can modify the behavior of the grid as follows.
 
 
Change the color of the 
 grid from the mimic context menu or the mimic's Properties 
 dialog.
 
Toggle display of the grid on and off using the mimic context menu or the mimic's Properties 
 dialog. Even if the grid is not displayed, drawing elements are still aligned 
 to it.
 
Change the resolution of 
 the grid from the mimic's Properties dialog.
 
Turn the grid 
 off completely using the mimic context menu or the mimic's Properties dialog. 
 Drawing elements subsequently produced or modified will not be aligned 
 to the grid.
 
 
Changing the grid resolution
 
The standard grid resolution of 8 pixels is suitable 
 for nearly all applications. However, if you have a special requirement, you can change the grid resolution from the mimic's Properties dialog.
 
 
Display the Mimic 
 Properties dialog using the Mimic context menu.
 
Enter new values 
 for the grid resolution in pixels. The minimum recommended grid size is 
 4 pixels.
 
Select OK to confirm 
 the change.


========================================
TOPIC: Groups.htm
========================================
Drawing groups
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Drawing Groups
 
A drawing group is a collection of one or more drawing 
 elements that behave as if they were one. For example, when moving them 
 within the window their relative positions remain the same although as 
 a group they may be moved anywhere within the window. All the tools that
 operate on single drawing elements may also be used on a drawing group.
 

 
You cannot arrange or align individual drawing elements 
 in a group.
 
 
To create a group
 
 
Select one or more 
 drawing elements.
 
From the Drawing context menu select the Group command. 
 
Or
 
Select the Group tool from the 
 Arrange toolbar.
 
 
To ungroup
 
 
Select a drawing 
 group.
 
From the Drawing context menu select the Ungroup 
 command. 
Or
 
Select the Ungroup tool from 
 the Arrange toolbar.

TOPIC: HARDCOPY_Example.htm
========================================
HARDCOPY example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
HARDCOPY Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This program exercises several modes of the HARDCOPY 
 command.
 
'Variables:
 
'@ACTIVATION.STATE of Type STATE
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SCREEN or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB hardcopyscreen()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = HARDCOPY("SCREEN");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode OPTION (mode 2) (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB hardcopyoption()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",1,1); 'Fit to the page
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",1,2); 'Full use of page
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",1,3); 'Scale factor mode
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",2,2); 'Reduced by 50% X
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",3,2); 'Reduced by 50% Y
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",5,"Hardcopy color"); 'Box title
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",6,"Copying in progress"); 'Message
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("OPTION",9,"Hardcopy done"); 'Button text
 
'intResult = HARDCOPY("SCREEN");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode PREVIOUSWINDOW (mode 4) (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB hardcopypreviouswindow()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = HARDCOPY("PREVIOUSWINDOW");
 
END SUB
 

 
For mode 4, the window that has lost focus must not be 
 hidden or covered by a window with focus.


========================================
TOPIC: Hints_and_tips.htm
========================================
VBA hints and tips
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
VBA hints and tips
 
The following hints and tips are based on the feedback we revceived. They do not concern programming techniques but more the way in which you use VBA in PcVue.
 
Stopping a VBA Program
 
To stop a VBA program that cannot be stopped in any other way (for example, if it is in an infinite loop) you can press the keys Ctrl+Break.
 
Enabling events 
 
You must have the Dim WithEvents statement in a code module to enable an object's events to be intercepted by VBA procedures.
 
Events are fired only if the EnableEvents property of the underlying object is set to True. This happens automatically once the first event script has been associated with the object. For instance, you must set the EnableEvents property of a symbol to True to allow it to respond to events. For more details see the topics Dim WithEvents - Coding VBA Events dynamically and Embedding VBA scripts in symbols.
 
In Design mode, events are not executed.
 
The 'Me' keyword
 
'Me' refers to the current object - the specific instance of the class where the code is executing. For an example, see the Microsoft VBA topics 'Me <keyword>' and 'Invalid use of Me keyword'.
 
Access to Variables
 
You must subscribe a variable before you can use it in VBA, unless it is used in an animation in a mimic that is currently open and therefore already subscribed.
 
The [...] syntax provides an additional shortcut to access a variable directly. For example, [Main.Branch1.B1] is the equivalent of: Variables("Main.Branch1.B1").
 
Diagnostic tools
 
The HMI is always open when you are developing a VBA program. You can use animations in a mimic to monitor the values and properties of variables dynamically. You can also keep open the Properties List (for a selected element in the HMI) for reference as you develop the code. The contents of the Properties List change dynamically as you select HMI objects and alter their properties.
 
For diagnostic tools in the standard Microsoft VBA environment, see the Microsoft Help topic 'Ambiguous Selection' (Code/Module Window, Immediate Window/Pane, Locals Window/Pane, Object Browser and Watch Window/Pane).


========================================
TOPIC: Historic_Data.htm
========================================
Historic data instructions
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Historic Data Instructions
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Instruction

Generate historic log data using the functionality provided by Data Export.

Generate historic trend data using the functionality provided by Data Export.

Extract alarm information.

Produce a user configurable batch record.
 
 
 

 
 
SVLOG
 
 
 
 
Extraction of logged event records information.
 
 
 

 
 
SVTREND
 
 
 
 
Extract historic information for a group of variables.


========================================
TOPIC: Historical_assoc_with_HDS_and_SQL.htm
========================================
Using historical associations with the HDS and SQL server
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Using Historical Associations with the HDS and SQL Server
 

 &#160;PcVue allows considerable flexibility when using an association of two or more historical servers and the HDS to record data in an SQL server database. The following are all possible configurations although in practice the configuration using a single active server is the likely to be the preferred solutions for reasons of practicality and cost. 
 
Configuration 1 - Multiple active servers and multiple SQL servers
 
Each historical servers are active and records in its own dedicated SQL server. Show picture

 
 
Technical issues - A replication mechanism is advisable in case of server failure.
 
Configuration - Select Multiple active servers for the redundancy mode in the association configuration.
 
Advantage - The same project can be used on all servers.
 
Advantage - Each station records data in tables of the same name.
 
Disadvantage - High cost if using full versions of SQL Server as it requires one SQL server license for each server.
 
 
Configuration 2 - Multiple active servers and single SQL server with multiple tables
 
Each of historical servers are active and record in the same database in the same SQL server (on a dedicated computer) but in different tables. Show picture

 
 
Technical issues - A replication mechanism is advisable in case of server failure.
 
Configuration - Select Multiple active servers for the redundancy mode in the association configuration.
 
Advantage - Lower cost if using full version of SQL Server as only one license required.
 
Disadvantage - Different projects must be used on each server because of the different table names.
 
 
Configuration 3 - Single active server and single SQL server
 
Only one of historical server is active at any one time. All of the historical servers record in the same table of the same database of the same SQL server. Show picture

 
 
Technical issues - No replication mechanism required.
 
Technical issues - Double buffering in the HDS minimizes any loss of data during changeover of the active server.
 
Configuration - Select Single active server for the redundancy mode in the association configuration.
 
Advantage - The same project can be used on all of servers.
 
Advantage - Lower cost if using full version of SQL Server as only one license required.


========================================
TOPIC: HISTORY_Example.htm
========================================
HISTORY example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
HISTORY Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example 1
 
This program "EXTRACT1" extracts historic data 
 to a text file:
 
SUB Main ()
 DIM ret as long;
DIM hd as double;
 ' Loading of "EXTRACT2" if not loaded :
 If (Program ("Is_loaded", "EXTRACT2", "") == 0) Then
Program ("Preload", "EXTRACT2", "");
End if;
 
' Programing of execution of function "EndExtract"
' of the program "EXTRACT2" when bit switches to 1
' "BitExtract" :
Event ( "ADD", "BitExtract", 1, "EXTRACT2", "","EndExtract");
 
' Export of the trend variable "Valve1" in the
' file "C:\EXTRACT.TXT". The request for export
' is only valid for values archived in the current
' hour. The internal variables "BitExtract"
' (bit) and "ResultExtract" (register) must exist.
hd = DateTimeValue ();
ret = HISTORY ( "GetTrend", "Valve1", hd -3600000,hd, "C:\\EXTRACT.TXT", 0, 
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"#D/#M/###Y,#h:#m:#s.##l, #V", "BitExtract", "ResultExtract" );
If (ret == 0) Then
Print ("Error on GetTrend: ");
Event ("DEL", "EndExtract", 1);
Exit SUB;
End If
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Program "EXTRACT2"
SUB EndExtract ()
Print ("Export of variable Valve1 completed:");
If (ResultExtract < 0) Then
Print ("Error code = ", ResultExtract);
Else
Print (ResultExtract, "values exported.");
End If
' Cancellation of the release :
Event ("DEL", "BitExtract", 1);
END SUB
 
Example of file "C:\EXTRACT.TXT" after export:
 
23/03/1994, 14:01:03.130, ? 
23/03/1994, 14:04:45.520, 12.3 
23/03/1994, 14:07:11.890, 11.9 
23/03/1994, 14:12:52.460, 11.7 
23/03/1994, 14:19:02.780, 11.4 
23/03/1994, 14:25:59.340, 11 
23/03/1994, 14:40:44.520, 11.2 
23/03/1994, 14:56:31.200, 11.5
 

 
The maximum number of values that can be exported at one 
 time is 4,000. 
 
 
To export more than 4000 values the HISTORY instruction 
 can be repeated, thus appending the results to the file.
 
Example 2 
 
These programs import historic data:
 
'Program "IMPORT1"
' Set up event for change of the Count variable
SUB Main()
Event("DELALL");
Event("ADD", "IMPORT.COUNT.MV1", "ALL>S", "Import.pvb", "","CountMV1");
END SUB
 
' Import Example 1. FORMAT and VARNAME keywords are
' contained in the import file
SUB LoadMV1()
HISTORY("IMPORTTREND","MV1.DAT", "IMPORT.STATUS.MV1","IMPORT.COUNT.MV1");
END SUB
 
' Function to run when count variable changes
SUB CountMV1 ()
If (@IMPORT.COUNT.MV1 == -1) Then
@IMPORT.MESSAGE = "Starting import of MV1";
Else
If (@IMPORT.COUNT.MV1 == 0) Then
@IMPORT.MESSAGE = "No import - check error messages";
Else
@IMPORT.MESSAGE = Addstring("Import succesfull - number of records imported = ",
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;TOC(@IMPORT.COUNT.MV1));
End If
End If
END SUB
 
Import Example 2. 
 
The FORMAT and VARNAME keywords are contained in the 
 instruction.
 
SUB LoadMV2()
HISTORY("IMPORTTREND","MV2.DAT", "IMPORT.STATUS.MV2","IMPORT.COUNT.MV2", 
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"FORMAT,0,0,5", "VARNAME,MV2");
END SUB
 
Example of Import file for LoadMV1:
 
FORMAT,0,0,5
VARNAME,MV1

25/09/2000,00:00:00, 7.64
25/09/2000,00:10:00, 7.87
25/09/2000,00:20:00, 8.12
25/09/2000,00:30:00, 8.40
25/09/2000,00:40:00, 8.69
25/09/2000,00:50:00, 8.99
 
Example of Import file for LoadMV2:
 
The FORMAT and VARNAME keywords are contained in the 
 instruction.
 
25/09/2000,00:00:00, 7.64
25/09/2000,00:10:00, 7.87
25/09/2000,00:20:00, 8.12
25/09/2000,00:30:00, 8.40
25/09/2000,00:40:00, 8.69
25/09/2000,00:50:00, 8.99
25/09/2000,01:00:00, 9.30
25/09/2000,01:10:00, 9.62
