TOPIC: HowToRunAProgram.htm
========================================
How to run a SCADA Basic program
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Run a SCADA Basic Program
 
SCADA Basic programs may be started in a variety of ways depending on the project's requirements. Below is a comprehensive list. 
 
 
From an event action. Triggered by a change in value of a variable. See the help book The Application Explorer in Actions.Event actions for information on how to configure an event action.
 
From a cyclic action. Periodically every N seconds. See the help book The Application Explorer.Actions in Cyclic actions for information on how to configure a cyclic action.
 
From the Scheduler. According to a schedule. For example, on the 1st day of each month at 12 PM. See the help book The Application Explorer.Actions in .Schedules actions for information on how to configure a schedule.
 
When the user presses a key combination. See the help book The Application Explorer.Actions.Function keys for information on how to configure function keys.
 
When PcVue starts. Startup options can be configured to run programs before and / or after communications is started. See the help topic The Application Explorer.Project settings.Station startup for more information on how to configure the program to be run at startup.
 
By the User from a control zone on a mimic. See the topic Running a SCADA Basic Program in the book Developing the HMI.Animation.
 
When a User logs on and/or off. See the book The Application Explorer in User Accounts.Configuring profiles.
 
When a mimic opens or closes. See the topic Developing the HMI.Working with mimics.Window properties dialog.Programs for more information. 
 
From an Alarm Viewer using the associated action of an alarm variable. See the topic The Application Explorer.Variables.The variable tree.Configuring variables.Associating behavior with a variable.Configuring an associated action.
 
From another program.
 
 

 
You can run up to five lines of SCADA Basic using the Macro animation without having to write an actual program. See the topic Developing the HMI.Animation.Run.Starting a SCADA Basic Program.

Iconizing and restoring a mimic
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Iconizing and Restoring a Mimic
 
Mimics that have been iconized are displayed in a column 
 next to the right hand border of the Workspace. Iconized mimics in Run 
 mode continue to be updated in real time.
 
 
To iconize a mimic, 
 double-click in the mimic�s title bar or select the Minimize tool 
 if enabled.
 
To restore a mimic, 
 double-click anywhere in the iconized view.

TOPIC: IF_THEN_ELSE_END_IF.htm
========================================
IF...THEN...ELSE...END IF
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
IF...THEN...ELSE...END IF
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Conditional execution of instructions according to the 
 result of a logical expression.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Syntax
 
 
 

 
 IF...THEN...
 
 1
 
 
 

 
 IF...THEN...ELSE...END IF
 
 2
 
 
 
 
Syntax 1
 
IF (Condition) THEN
 
&#160;&#160;[instruction block 1]
 
END IF
 
There is no return type.
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Condition
 
 
 
A logical expression, for example: TankLevel > 25.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
If the condition is true then instruction block 1 is 
 executed after which the program continues at the line after the END IF.
 
Syntax 2
 
IF (Condition) THEN
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;[instruction 
 block 1]
 
ELSE
 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;[instruction 
 block 2]
 
END IF
 
There is no return type.
 
Execution
 
If the condition is true then instruction block 1 is 
 executed else instruction block 2 is executed after which the program 
 continues at the line after the END IF.
 
Example
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM i as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
i=0;
 
'or: i=1;
 
If (i==1)Then
 
&#160;&#160;Print("i=1");
 
Else
 
&#160;&#160;Print("i is not 1");
 
End If
 
END SUB

TOPIC: If_you_are_using_TCP_IP.htm
========================================
Checking the network setup of your PC when using TCP/IP
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Checking the Network Setup when Using TCP/IP
 
If using NetBios see the topic Checking the network setup when using NetBios.
 
Windows 7
 
 
Using the Start menu open the Control Panel and select View network status and tasks. The Network and Sharing Centre dialog opens.
 
From the task list in the left pane select Change adapter settings. The Network Connections dialog opens displaying the connections available on your PC. Show picture
 
Right click the icon representing the network connection that you will be using and, from the pop-up menu, select the Properties command. The Network Properties dialog is displayed. Show picture

 
Select the Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) from the item list and click the Properties button.
 
Make a note of the TCP/IP address for use in PcVue's configuration.
 
 
What if the IP address grayed out?
 
If the option Obtain 
 IP address automatically is selected then the network card is configured 
 to obtain its IP address dynamically from a DHCP server and the IP address 
 field will be grayed out. If this is the case, there are two options if 
 you want to use the TCP/IP protocol.
 
 
Change the configuration 
 so that the TCP/IP address is not obtained automatically, that is it is 
 configured in the Internet Protocol Properties dialog.
 
Use the host name 
 instead of the TCP/IP address in PcVue's network configuration. 
 If you use the host name, you are limited to one node per station.
 
 
Checking the host name
 
 
Right click on the 
 My 
 Computer icon on the desktop and, from the displayed pop-up menu, 
 select Properties. 
 The System Properties dialog is displayed.
 
The host name is displayed in the Full 
 computer name field.

TOPIC: IGET_BUFFER.htm
========================================
IGET_BUFFER
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
IGET_BUFFER
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Read an integer located in a memory area.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
IntVal = IGET_BUFFER(Handle, Offset);
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Handle
 
 
 
The location of the memory buffer as returned by ALLOC_BUFFER. 
 Type LONG.
 
 
 

 
 
Offset
 
 
 
The offset in bytes at which the integer is to be found. 
 Any numeric type.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 

 
 
Return:
 
 
 
An integer located in a memory area reserved by ALLOC_BUFFER 
 or FILETOBUF.
 
 
 
 
Example
 
i1 = IGET_BUFFER(handle,2);

TOPIC: Images_Overview.htm
========================================
Overview of images
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Overview of Images
 
The HMI supports the use of images in the following formats.
 
 
BMP
 
GIF and animated GIF
 
JPEG
 
PNG
 
WMF
 
EMF
 
 
It also supports multimedia files in AVI 
 format. 
 
The use of images can considerably enhance the appearance of your 
 application. However, large images or those with a large number of colors can consume significant system resources when they are rendered.
 
Before an image can be used in the HMI, you must move it to the 
 correct folder using the Windows Explorer or the Libraries Manager (Application Explorer). 
 The image must reside in either:
 
 
The Bitmap folder 
 of your project.
 
The Bitmap folder 
 of any Libraries exposed to the project.
 
 
An image is inserted as a reference. That is, the image 
 file name is inserted in the mimic definition file. This means that, even 
 when it has been inserted into a mimic, Microsoft Paint or any other drawing 
 package may still edit an image. Any changes made will be visible the 
 next time the mimic is opened.
 

 
There are many variations and options for the encoding of GIF, animated GIF, JPEG, PNG, WMF and EMF images. If an image does not render correctly it is likely that PcVue does not support the particular encoding variation of that image. Consider changing the encoding using a suitable 3rd party graphic editor before trying again.
 
 
How to insert an image
 
 
Click on the Image button in 
 the Draw toolbar. A dialog will be displayed containing a list of available 
 images and an area in which they may be previewed.
 
Select the Library 
 in which the image is located.
 
Select the name of 
 the required image from the list. If necessary, a lexical filter may be 
 entered to reduce the number of images displayed in the list.
 
Insert the image by either:
Clicking the Insert button. The 
 image will be placed at the top left hand corner of the mimic. Drag the 
 image to the desired location in the mimic. 
Click on the name of the image in the list and 
 drag it to the required location in the mimic.
Click on the thumbnail 
 of the selected image and drag it to the required location in the mimic.
 
 
Show picture
 

 
 
 
About the Insert Image dialog
 
The Insert Image dialog displays a list of images from the selected library. A thumbnail of the selected image is displayed at the top right corner.
 
The images list can be displayed in several formats selected by the tools above the list.
 
 
 
 
 

 Format
 Description
 
 

 Detail
 Displays the available images as a list of one per line including the size of the image.
 
 

 List
 Displays the available images as a tabular list. Only the image name is displayed.
 
 

 Small icon
 Displays the available images with a small icon representing each image.
 
 

 Medium icon
 Displays the available images with a medium thumbnail representing each image.
 
 

 Large icon
 Displays the available images with a large thumbnail representing each image.
 
 

 Refresh
 Used to refresh the list in the event that the content of the library has been modified while PcVue is running.
 
 
 
 

 
If you choose to display the images in as thumbnails there may be a small delay while the thumbnails are generated


========================================
TOPIC: Import.htm
========================================
How to import a configuration subset
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to import a configuration subset
 
The Import feature of the Application Architect makes it possible to import a subset of the configuration elements you have modeled and exported. It facilitates the reuse of application functions in other projects, reinventing therefore, the way projects are configured. This feature also enables the creation of large projects out of smaller ones, improving thus the production and generation of projects.
 

 
We recommend you make a backup of you configuration before carrying out the import process. Having a backup at hand helps manage any error that might occur.
The backup can be done using the Save button on the import task bar.
 
 
 

 
From the PcVue menu, go to Configure.Application Architect and select the Import tab.
 
 

 
Click on the button representing the type of element you want to import: Instances, templates or parameters. The Reference data pane on the left displays the list of elements of the selected type in the current configuration of the project. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 

 
In the right pane, select the file containing the configuration to import - A file created using the Export feature. The content of the file will appear in the Data to import pane. If needed, you can use the Reload file button to update the content of the pane.
 

 
An empty list of elements to import after you have selected and loaded a file to import is the sign that the selection of element type in the left pane does not match the type of elements you are trying to import.
 
For example, if you have selected Instances in the left pane and the file you are trying to import only contains templates, then the list of elements to import remains empty because there is no instance to import in the selected file.
 
 
 

 
(Optional) Right click in the Data to import pane to check the Advanced mode to see the properties modified and their values for configuration elements contained in templates. This mode is not available for parameters.
 
 

 
(Optional) Check Merge with existing to combine the imported elements with the ones in your current configuration. Without merging, the elements you import replace the current configuration.
 

 
For every element you import, if the element already exists in your current configuration, its properties will be overwritten by those defined for the element you import.
 
 
 

 
Drag and drop the elements from the Data to import pane to the Reference Data pane.
 
 

 
Click Check consistency to check if there is a configuration conflict.
 

 
Inconsistent configurations are due to various factors. Refer to Troubleshooting configuration import for more information.
 
 
 

 
Click the Save drop-down list and select a save mode. Standard location saves the configuration file in its default location (same as the File.Save menu item) and Export location saves it to the folder \TEMPLATES\EXPORT of the project. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 
 

 
If necessary, you can restore a previous configurations using the Restore drop-down: Restore from Standard location or from Export location.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 

 
Manually deleting a configuration element leads to cascading deletions due to dependencies between elements. These dependencies will not be recreated automatically by re-importing the deleted element.


========================================
TOPIC: Import_troubleshooting.htm
========================================
Troubleshooting configuration import
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Troubleshooting configuration import
 
The table below details the possible configuration inconsistency issues that might occur when importing a subset of modeled configuration into PcVue.
 

 
When a configuration inconsistency occurs with severe warnings, the save button is deactivated and the dialog box listing the inconsistencies is displayed. This is not true for warnings due to minor inconsistencies. You can configure the save button to be deactivated even with less severe warnings from the settings.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Problem description
 
 Possible causes
 Possible solutions
 Expected behaviors
 
 

 
 
Missing parameters on a template (or inherited template) or
 
Missing parameters as "Named parameters" on a template (or inherited template)
 
 Templates imported before dependencies. 
 Make sure you import the necessary parameters.
 
 
Configuration becomes consistent and saving is possible.
 
The parameter values, if they existed, will also be restored without re-importing the template or instance.
 
 
 

 Missing parameters on an instance (or in an element in an instance)
 Instances imported before dependencies.
 
 
 Make sure you import the necessary parameters.
 
Re-import the template if the parameter (with the exception of global and unitary) is not found during an import. This could be because it has been taken out of the template used to create the instance. Does not block saving. 
 
 
 
Configuration becomes consistent and saving is possible.
 
 
 

 Missing included template in a template
 Templates imported before dependencies.
 Make sure you import the necessary templates.
 
 
Configuration becomes consistent and saving is possible.
 
The included template is automatically updated as well as the concerned instances.
 
 
 

 Missing instance's configuration element in the original template.
 
 
The configuration element exists in an imported instance, but not in the corresponding template.
 
 
 
If the template is the source of the problem, the solution is to re-import the template with the configuration element in question as well as the instance.
 
If the instance is the source of the problem, the solution is to re-import the instance that is consistent with the template.
 
 
 
Configuration becomes consistent and saving is possible.
 
 
 

 
 
Missing instance's parameter in the original template.
 
 
 
The template structure is no longer supported, or the imported template is corrupted.
 
 
 
If the structure is no longer supported, the solution is to remove the parameter from the instance. Otherwise, re-importing the template should solve the problem.
 
 Configuration becomes consistent and if the parameter is still supported, references will automatically be restored between the instance parameter and that of the template without reloading the instance.
 
 
 
 
Other reasons for an inconsistent configuration are:
 
 

 
Importing a configuration into a library that is not its original library.
 
 

 
Using files from previous, more permissive PcVue versions.
 
 
 
Depending on the issue, restoring a previously saved configuration or continuing to import more element may help troubleshooting.

TOPIC: Importing_strings_for_an_entire_library.htm
========================================
Importing strings for an entire library
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Importing Strings for an Entire Library
 
This process imports the strings for an entire library
 (local, common or custom).
 
How to import the strings for an entire library
 
 
Open the Import/Export
 Tool using the menu command Display.Translation Import/Export.
 
If you have not already
 done so, configure the file paths and languages. See the topic
 Configuring the Import/Export tool for details.
 
Select the library
 or libraries for import. You can select more than one library by clicking
 while holding down the Ctrl key.
 
Start the import
 process using the Import button. 
 
The import
function processes only the mimic, template and/or symbol files that are referenced in the translation files. For large projects, this may take several seconds to run.
 
 

 
If any mimics are open at the
 time of import, the process will display a warning dialog then automatically
 close them.
 
Any changes you make to the
 Import/Export Tool configuration are only temporary unless you click the Save button.
 
 
Canceling the import process
 
You can cancel the import process at any time during
 its execution by clicking the Import button again (which is renamed Stop during the import process). The import will stop but changes made to the
 files prior to clicking Stop are not undone.
 
Important note about selecting the language for the
 import
 
An import/export file may contain strings for several
 languages. The Import/Export tool indicates the languages available by
 showing the entry in the language list boxes with bold styling. Appended (in square brackets)
 to the language name is a list of the types of strings that are available
 (mimics, mimic templates and symbols) Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 

 
If you select a language for
 which there is no information in the import/export file, the import will
 run to completion with no errors but no strings will be imported.
 
 
Checking the Status Log
 
When the export process has completed you should check
 the Status log in the lower pane of the Import Export Tool dialog for
 any errors. Show picture
 
If you want a permanent record of the process or you want to search for something
 in particular you can copy the contents of the Status log by using Ctrl+A
 and Ctrl+C and then paste it into a text editor such as Notepad and save it as a text file.

Importing strings for a single mimic, mimic template or symbol
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Importing Strings for a Single Mimic, Mimic Template 
 or Symbol
 
This process imports strings for a single mimic, mimic 
 template or symbol. Before you use Import/Export for a single item, you 
 must first configure the Import/Export Tool to select the source import/export 
 file and import language. See the topic Configuring the Import/Export tool.
 

 
If the Import/Export options are grayed out in the main 
 menu or pop-up, ensure that the type of process (mimic, mimic template 
 or symbol) is enabled and that the import/export file names are configured 
 in the dialog Translation Import/Export Tool.
 
 

 
If the file from which you are 
 importing contains no entries for the selected item, no warnings are given 
 and the item remains unchanged.
 
 
How to import the strings to a single mimic or mimic 
 template
 
 
Open the mimic or 
 mimic template and select Design mode.
 
Right-click on the 
 mimic and select Mimic Translations.Import from the pop-up menu. 
 
 
The import process proceeds without further interaction 
 with the user unless any errors are encountered.
 

 
You can also select the import 
 command from the menu using Window.Mimic Translations.Import.
 
 
How to import the strings to a single symbol
 
 
Open any mimic or 
 mimic template that contains an instance of the symbol.
 
Right-click on the 
 symbol and select Symbol Translations.Import from the pop-up menu. 
 
 
The import process runs without further interaction 
 with the user unless any errors are encountered.
 

 
You can also select the Import 
 command from the menu using Edit.Symbol Translations.Import.


========================================
TOPIC: Included_templates.htm
========================================
Included templates
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Included Templates
 
An included template is an instance of one template that has been included in another. One or several instances of a template may be included. For example, the template SMALLOFFICE includes two instances of the template LUM and once instance of the template AC. When the application is generated, any instantiation of SMALLOFFICE will generate not only its own configuration objects but also two instantiations of objects from template LUM and one instantiation of objects from template AC.
 
When to use and when not to use included templates
 
Included templates are most useful when the element represented by the template has a regular structure. For example, you have a building with three small offices and one big office. The three small offices are identical and each have two lights and an air conditioning unit. The big office has four lights and an air conditioning unit. You could create a template for a light (LUM) and one for an air conditioning unit (AC). Another template could be created (SMALLOFFICE) including two instantiations of LUM and one of AC, and a final template (BIGOFFICE) with four instantiations of LUM and one of AC. Three instantiations of SMALLOFFICE and one of BIGOFFICE would complete the configuration. Show picture

 
Included templates are not so useful if the application has an irregular structure. For example a building with four different offices - they have different numbers of lights and one does not have an air conditioning unit. In this case, you could not use multiple instantiations of a template that included the lights and air conditioning unit. Instead, it would be necessary to create a template (OTHEROFFICE) with no embedded templates. Then the correct number of lights and air conditioning units would have to be added to each instance of OTHEROFFICE during the instantiation process. Show picture

 
How to add an included template
 
 
In the right pane of the Application Architect, select the Templates tab.
 
Using the templates configuration tree select the template to which the embedded template will be added.
 
Using the secondary navigation tree select the Included templates folder.
 
Select Add an included template
 
Double click the template to be included. A new instance will appear in the Included Templates folder.
 
Select the newly added template. Its properties will appear in the properties grid. Show picture
 
Edit the Name of the new instance to one appropriate to the application. 
 
Edit the Branch of the new instance to one appropriate to the application.


========================================
TOPIC: Inherited_templates.htm
========================================
Inherited templates
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Inherited Templates
 
An inherited template is one that has another template as its parent. When the application is generated, an instantiation of the inherited template will generate not only its configuration elements, but also those of its parent from which it is derived.
 

 
Adding an inherited template does not affect the parent template in any way. When the application is generated, any instantiation of the parent template will only generate any configuration elements that it contains.
 
 

 
If the parent template includes symbols, an instance of the inherited template will include those as well as any of its own. This can be avoided by using tagging to control the symbol instantiation.
 
 
An example of an inherited template
 
The template LUM represents a simple light and contains one variable called ON. The template DIMMER is inherited from LUM and contains one variable called LEVEL. An instantiation of DIMMER will produce two variables, ON and LEVEL. An instantiation of LUM will produce one variable, ON. Show picture

 
How to add an inherited template
 
 
In the right pane of the Application Architect, select the Templates tab.
 
Using the templates configuration tree select the template to which the included template will be added.
 
Select Add an inherited template. A new template with a default name is created.
 
Select the new template and use the properties grid to change the Name to one appropriate to your application. You can also add an optional Description.
 
Configure the template in the same way as any other adding variables, parameters etc.


========================================
TOPIC: Input_Output.htm
========================================
Input-Output instructions
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Input-Output Instructions
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Instruction
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 EMAIL
 
 Manages simple email functions to send messages, with or without attachments.
 
 

 
 
FTP
 
 
 
 
Copies a file to or from an FTP server.
 
 
 

 
 
FTPS
 
 
 
 
Copies a file to or from an FTP server using FTPS.
 
 
 

 
 
HARDCOPY
 
 
 
 
Initiates a hard copy of the desktop client screen on the Windows default printer or clears its print jobs.
 
 
 

 
 
KEY
 
 
 
 
Program the keyboard function keys on the desktop client.
 
 
 

 
 
LPRINT
 
 
 
 
Send a message to a printer.
 
 
 

 
 
MULTIMEDIA
 
 
 
 
Provides access to the Window's Media Control Interface for the desktop client.
 
 
 

 
 
PRINTER
 
 
 
 
Manage the operation of a printer.
 
 
 

 SQL_COMMAND
 
 
 
Sends Sql commands using a pre-configured Sql connection.
 
 
 

 SQL_CONNECTION
 
 Manage PcVue runtime objects used to handle Sql connections.
 
 

 SQL_QUERY
 
 Used to execute a specific Sql Query (read or write)
 
 

 
 
SVKEY
 
 
 
 
Read the contents of the protection key on multi-station systems.
 
 
 

 
 
SVSQL
 
 
 
 
Execute an SQL command.


========================================
TOPIC: Input_parameters_value.htm
========================================
How the value of an input parameter is resolved
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How the Value of an Input Parameter is Resolved
 

 
The manner in which Global Input Parameters and Input Parameters are resolved is different.
 
 
A reminder of the three steps of using an Input Parameter
 
 
Define the parameter.
 
Add the property to the Parameters folder of a template to define at which level the parameter value is to be entered at instantiation time. A Global Input Parameter can also be added to a template instance or the tree root.
 
Use the parameter to define the value of one or more properties.
 
 

 
The same parameter can be assigned to several templates on one or more levels. A different input value can be entered for each use.
 
 
How the value of an Input Parameter is resolved
 
The value of an input parameter is resolved starting at the leaves of the instance tree moving through the hierarchy to the root.
 
If no value is found in the tree for the parameter (instances or parent templates), the default value of the parameter is used. A value is considered to be found if it was entered by the user and not left empty.
 
 In practice, the easiest way to explain and understand how a parameter is resolved is by example. Consider the following representation of an instance tree.
 
 
BUILDING1 - An instance of the template BUILDING.
FLOOR1 - An instance of the template FLOOR and subordinate to BUILDING1.
LIGHT1 - An instance of the template LIGHT and subordinate to FLOOR1.
CMD - A variable of the template LIGHT.
 
 
A parameter used in the variable CMD is resolved as follows: 
 
If a value was entered for the parameter in the variable CMD then use this value 
 
Else if a value entered for the parameter in instance LIGHT1 then use this value
 
Else If there is a default value for the parameter in template LIGHT then use this value 
 
Else if a value entered for the parameter in instance FLOOR1 then use this value 
 
Else if there is a default value for the parameter in template FLOOR then use this value
 
 Else if a value entered for the parameter in instance BUILDING1 then use this value 
 
Else if there is a default value for the parameter in template BUILDING then use this value
 
Else the value is unresolved.
 
How the value of a Global Input Parameter is resolved
 
For Global Input Parameters a value found in the tree is considered valid even if it is empty, and it replaces the previous value. If it is empty, the default value is used. This mechanism can be used to limit the propagation of the value of a global parameter (in conjunction with the parameter's Impacted Elements configuration).
 
In practice, the easiest way to explain and understand how a global parameter is resolved is by example. Consider the following representation of an instance tree.
 
 
INSTANCES ROOT.
Global parameter gParam positioned on INSTANCES ROOT with value of RootValue.
BUILDING1 - An instance of the template BUILDING.

FLOOR1 - An instance of the template FLOOR and subordinate to BUILDING1.

Global parameter gParam positioned on FLOOR1 with value of no input value.

LIGHT1 - An instance of the template LIGHT and subordinate to FLOOR1.

CMD - A variable of the template LIGHT.
 
 
The global parameter gParam is resolved as follows.
 
 
Objects of BUILDING1 will take the value RootValue for gParam.
 
 
Objects of FLOOR1 (and subsequent instances) will take the default value for gParam. The parameter gParam found at the instance FLOOR1 overwrites that defined at the BUILDING1 instance. If a value was entered at FLOOR1, that value becomes the value of the parameter. If no value is entered then the default value of the parameter is used.


========================================
TOPIC: Insert_Symbols.htm
========================================
How to insert a symbol into a window
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to insert a Symbol into a Window
 
Before you can insert a symbol into a window, the window 
 must be in Design mode. There are then a number of methods by which a 
 Symbol may be inserted into a window.
 
 
Using the Insert.Symbol 
 command.
 
Using the Insert.Symbols from instance command.
 
By dragging and dropping 
 the symbol from another window.
 
By using the standard 
 Windows commands for Copy (Ctrl+C) and Paste (Ctrl+V) to copy the symbol 
 either to the same window or a different window.
 
By using the Copy 
 tool from the Standard toolbar to copy the symbol either to the same window 
 or to a different window.
 
By dragging and dropping 
 it from the Library Browser.
 
 
How to insert a symbol using the Insert.Symbol command
 
 
Select the Insert.Symbol 
 command. A box containing a list of Symbols, referenced by name, will 
 be displayed. A lexical filter may be applied to reduce the number of 
 symbols displayed in the list.
 
If using multiple 
 Libraries select the relevant Library.
 
Select the symbol 
 name from the scrollable list. A visual preview of the symbol is displayed 
 adjacent to the list.
 
If the symbol uses 
 relative variables then enter a valid branch.
 
Insert the symbol 
 into the window by selecting the OK button.
 
 
How to insert a symbol using the Insert.Symbols from instance command
 
 
Select the Insert.Symbols from instance
 command. A dialog box containing the list of instances and the symbols they contain will 
 be displayed. A lexical filter may be applied to reduce the number of 
 instances displayed.
 
In the left pane, select the instance that contains the symbol you want to insert.
 
In the right pane, select the symbol by ticking its
 name from the list and click the Insert button. Double clicking a symbol in the visual preview also inserts it in the current window. The branch corresponding to the instance is added automatically. By using multi-selection, you can insert multiple symbols.
 
Repeat steps 2 and 3 if you need to insert other symbols.
 
Click the Close button to close the Instances selector.
 
 
The toolbar allows you to filter the list of instances displayed:
 
 
By entering a text-based filter on the instance name (Starts with, Contains, Ends with or a regular expression).
 
By selecting the name of a template, in which case only instances of this template will be displayed.
 
 
How to drag and drop a symbol from another window
 
 
Using the mouse click 
 on the symbol in the source window with the left mouse button. Hold down 
 the mouse button and drag the symbol to its new window - release the mouse 
 button when it is in the correct position.
 
When the mouse button 
 is released, a dialog will be displayed showing the names of any variables 
 and allowing a branch to be entered. Enter a branch (if required) and 
 select the OK button. The symbol has now been moved to the new window.
 
 
How to copy a symbol from another window
 
 
Using the mouse click 
 on the symbol in the source window with the left mouse button. Hold down 
 the mouse button and the Ctrl key on the keyboard. Drag the copy of the symbol to its new window - release the mouse button when it is 
 in the correct position.
 
When the mouse button 
 is released, a dialog will be displayed showing the names of any variables 
 and allowing a branch to be entered. Enter a branch (if required) and 
 select the OK button. The symbol has now been moved to the new window.
 
 
How to paste a symbol using Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V
 
 
Using the mouse select 
 the symbol in the source window by clicking with the left mouse button. 
 Use Ctrl+C to copy the symbol.
 
Move the mouse pointer 
 to destination window and click with the left button to select the window. 
 Use Ctrl+V to paste the symbol into the window.


========================================
TOPIC: Instanciating_templates.htm
========================================
Instantiating templates
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Instantiating Templates
 
The tree structure developed during template instantiation defines the final structure of the application, in particular the variables tree. Much of the time spent using the Application Architect will be instantiating templates and managing the template instances and so it is worth understanding in detail the mechanisms involved. 
 
There are two methods to instantiate templates. 
 
 
From the context menu or main toolbar using Add instance.
 
From the Templates toolbar in the left pane, using either drag and drop or double click.
 
 
How to instantiate a template using the Add instance task
 
 
In the right pane, select the Instances tab.
 
In the primary configuration tree, select the configuration root, instance or topology element under which the new instance is to be placed.
 
Select Add instance. An expanding menu of available templates is displayed. Select the template to be added. Show picture
 
An instance is inserted in the instances tree with default properties.

 
 
How to instantiate a template using the Add multiple instance task
 
The Add multiple instance task allows you to add several instances of a template in one action.
 
 
In the right pane, select the Instances tab.
 
In the primary configuration tree, select the configuration root, instance or topology element under which the new instance is to be placed.
 
Select Add multiple instances. The Multiple Instances dialog opens. Show picture
 
Expand the templates tree in the left pane and select the template to instantiate.
 
Using the right pane configure the instances. The properties will be initially populated by the Automatic naming properties of the template. 
 
Click the Instantiate button to start instantiation.

 
 
How to instantiate a template using the Templates toolbar (left pane)
 
 
In the right pane, select the Instances tab.
 
In the instances tree, select the root, instance or topology element under which the new instance is to be placed.
 
In the left pane select the Templates toolbar.
 
Expand the tree and locate the template to be instantiated. Double click the template.
 
A template instance is inserted in the instances tree with default properties.
 
 

 
This method permits inserting quickly a large amount of instances as soon as the default name fits.
 
 

 
You can also instantiate a template from the templates toolbar using drag and drop.
 
 
Displaying and changing the properties of a template instance
 
To display or change the properties of an instance, select it in the instances tree of the Instances tab. The properties are displayed in the properties grid.
 
 
Description - A description of the instance. Only used within the Application Architect and has no effect on the application itself. Optional
 
Name - The name of the instance. Only used within the Application Architect and has no effect on the application itself.
 
Branch - The branch that will be used during application generation for all configuration elements that the instance contains. 
 
 

 
The Branch property creates an explicit branch in the variables tree. If a Description is entered, it is used as the branch description.
 
 
Managing properties defined by an input, expression or parameter
 
The properties grid in the lower part of the right pane displays a list of the properties, of the selected instance, that have been defined by an input, expression or parameter. The grid is used to view and edit values. See the topic Managing properties defined by an input, expression or parameter for more information. 
 
Managing instances
 
The following tasks are available to help you manage instances.
 
 
You can move an instance and all elements subordinate to it using drag and drop.
 
You can copy and paste an instance and all elements subordinate to it using the standard keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl + C and Ctrl + V) or using Copy and Paste. Be careful when pasting that you first select the element under which it is to be pasted.
 
 You can delete an instance and all elements subordinate to it using the Delete key or Remove. A confirmation dialog is always displayed when deleting.


========================================
TOPIC: INSTANTMESSAGING_Example.htm
========================================
INSTANTMESSAGING example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
INSTANTMESSAGING Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises modes of the INSTANTMESSAGING command.
 
Sub LoadChats()
 
 &#160;InstantMessaging("LOADCHATS","loadChatsState");
 
'-------- Allows us to store all the chats we are using into memory.
 
end sub
 
&#160;
 
sub LoadMessages()
 
 &#160;InstantMessaging("LOADMESSAGES", @ChatName, "10/03/1997", "",100,"loadMessagesState");
 
'-------- Loads at most 100 messages since 10/03/1997 up to now.
 
end sub
 
&#160;
 
sub GetMessageInfo()
 
 &#160;@MessageId = InstantMessaging("GETMESSAGEID",@ChatName,@index);
 
&#160;
 
'-------- Once everything is loaded we can get all the message information depending on the index we chose
 
&#160;&#160;@sender = InstantMessaging("GETMESSAGESENDER", @MessageId);
 
&#160;&#160;@text = InstantMessaging("GETMESSAGETEXT", @MessageId);
 
&#160;&#160;@filepath = InstantMessaging("GETMESSAGEFILEPATH", @MessageId);
 
&#160;&#160;@extension = InstantMessaging("GETMESSAGEEXTENSION", @MessageId);
 
&#160;
 
'-------- Once we are done interacting with the messages from this chat we can clear the memory
 
 &#160;InstantMessaging("ClearMessagesCache",@ChatName);
 
End sub

TOPIC: IntelliMouse.htm
========================================
Intellimouse support
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Intellimouse support
 

 &#160;PcVue has built in support for the Intellimouse features. In addition to the main two buttons, the centrally mounted wheel that can be used to zoom, scroll and pan around the
 workspace and windows.
 
Mimic zoom - Default: Ctrl + wheel
 
Zooms the view of a mimic in and out.
 
Mimic manual panning - Default: Shift + middle button down + drag
 
 Pans a mimic within its container window. Only operational if the mimic is larger than the container window.
 
Mimic automatic panning - Default: Ctrl + middle button click + drag
 
Pans the mimic within its window (only operational if the mimic is larger than the container window).
 
Mimic and workspace scroll - Default vertical: Wheel, Default horizontal: Shift + wheel
 
Scrolls the mimic within its window. Only operational if the mimic is larger than the container window. Then, when the mimic is at its limits, scrolls the workspace within its window (only operational if the workspace is larger than the container window and scrollbars are enabled).
 
Workspace automatic panning - Default: Shift + middle button click + drag
 
Pans the workspace within its window (only operational if the workspace is larger than the container window and scrollbars are enabled).

TOPIC: Intersection_of_Two_Drawing_Elements.htm
========================================
Intersection of two drawing elements
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Intersection of Two Drawing Elements
 
The Intersection tool creates a polygon 
 that is equivalent to the overlapping area of two drawing elements.
 

 
 
 
How to use the intersection tool
 
 
Select the first 
 drawing element by clicking with the left mouse button.
 
Select the second 
 drawing element holding the Shift button so that both drawing elements 
 are selected.
 
Select the Intersection 
 tool from the Arrange toolbar. A new polygon is created taking the color 
 from the second shape.
 
Click and drag the 
 polygon to its location. The original drawing elements remain unchanged.

TOPIC: JSONPATH_Example.htm
========================================
JSONPATH example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
JSONPATH Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This program illustrates the use of the JSONPATH instruction.
 
Example prerequisites
 
 

 
A Json formatted file, example.json, located in the project's TP folder. The file contents is as follows.
 
 
 

 
example.json
 

 
 
 

 
{&#160;
 
 
 

 
"stores":[&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"Lambton Quay",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"Willis Street"&#160;
 
 
 

 
],&#160;
 
 
 

 
"manufacturers":[&#160;
 
 
 

 
{&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"name":"Acme Co",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"products":[&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;{&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"name":"Anvil",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"price":50&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;}&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;]&#160;
 
 
 

 
},&#160;
 
 
 

 
{&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"name":"Contoso",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"products":[&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;{&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"name":"Elbow Grease",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"price":99.95&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;},&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;{&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"name":"Headlight Fluid",&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;"price":4&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;}&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;]&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;}&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;]&#160;&#160;
 
 
 

 
}&#160;
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
&#160;
 

 
Exec_JSONPATH.svb
 

 
 
 

 
Sub Main ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM iReturn AS INTEGER;DIM i As INTEGER;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sJsonFileName AS STR, sNameSpace AS STR, sJsonPath AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sReturn AS STR;
 
 
 

 
'------ Initialise variables
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sJsonFileName = "example.json";
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sNameSpace = "MyNameSpace";
 
 
 

 
'-------- Load JSON formatted file
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;iReturn = JSONPATH("LOADFILE", sNameSpace, sJsonFileName);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Result from JSONPATH LOADFILE = ", iReturn);
 
 
 

 
'-------- Loop to extract JSON values
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;FOR(i=0; i<2; i++)
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;sJsonPath = FORMAT("manufacturers[%d].name", i);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;sReturn = JSONPATH("GETSTR", sNameSpace, sJsonPath);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Result from JSONPATH GETSTR ", sJsonPath, " is ", sReturn);
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;sJsonPath = FORMAT("manufacturers[%d].products[0].name", i);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;sReturn = JSONPATH("GETSTR", sNameSpace, sJsonPath);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Result from JSONPATH GETSTR ", sJsonPath, " is ", sReturn);
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;sJsonPath = FORMAT("manufacturers[%d].products[0].price", i);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;iReturn = JSONPATH("GETINT", sNameSpace, sJsonPath);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("Result from JSONPATH GETINT ", sJsonPath, " is ", iReturn);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;NEXT
 
 
 

 
'-------- Release resources&#160;&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;iReturn = JSONPATH("UNLOAD", sNameSpace);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("Result from JSONPATH UNLOAD = ", iReturn);
 
 
 

 
End Sub&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;

TOPIC: labels_conf.htm
========================================
How to configure Labels
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to configure Labels
 
With PcVue, the application designer has the possibility to customize a variety of text strings used in mimics and visible by runtime users. These labels can be bilingual if the application is configured to have runtime text strings in 2 languages.
 
 

 
Level labels are associated with each level of these categories: Command levels, Browsing levels, Alarm acknowledge levels, Alarm masking levels, and Alarm maintenance levels. They can be up to 255 characters.
 
 

 
Associated labels are configurable sets of strings that are used as representations for a variable's value when it is displayed and / or logged. For example, the value of a bit variable can be represented by the labels STOPPED and RUNNING (instead of values 0 and 1). There are three types of associated labels that can be configured:
 
 

 
Bit: Strings representing the values zero and one for each context in which bit associated labels can be used.
 
 

 
Alarm: Strings representing each of the possible alarm states, plus user actions associated with alarms.
 
 

 
Enumerated: This associated label comprises a list of discrete values and corresponding labels. When an enumeration label is associated with a variable, in those aspects of PcVue HMI that support it, the enumerated labels can be displayed instead of the variable's value. Depending on the intended use of the label, attached to register or text variables, the list of enumeration values can be numbers or strings.
 
The strings provided by theses labels are displayed in the Application Explorer, mimics, the Web Scheduler to display meaningful labels in place of BACnet Schedule output values, and stored in the archive units. In mimics, the strings from associated labels can be displayed in the alarm and log viewers, by the Text-Label and Send-Bit animations. Any number of bit, alarm and enumerated labels can be configured.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 Label type
 Variable type
 Application Explorer
 Mimic animations
 Web Scheduler
 Archive units - Alarm & Log viewers
 
 

 Bit
 Bit
 Yes
 Yes
 No
 Yes
 
 

 Alarm
 Alarm
 Yes
 Yes
 No
 Yes
 
 

 Enumerated
 Register
 Yes
 Yes
 Yes
 Not applicable
 
 

 Enumerated
 Text
 Yes
 Yes
 No
 Not applicable
 
 
 
 
Labels associated to user actions are also available. They are displayed in a Log Viewer that has been configured to display user actions like login and logout.
 
 
 
 

 
Extended attribute labels are customizable variable extended attribute names. Extended attributes labels are visible in the Application Explorer and can be displayed in the column header of a Grid control for instance. The labels are pre-defined and can be customized by the application developer.
 
 

 
Timestamp quality labels are a set of strings that are used when displaying the timestamp quality of variables. They are useful if data timestamps are set at the device level, for example by an OPC Server. The timestamp quality labels appear in the Application Explorer and can be displayed in the Alarm and Log Viewers by using the #x format character. The labels are pre-defined and can be customized by the application developer.
 
 
 

 
In the dialog boxes used to configure labels, the use of a dimmed italic font indicates that the label is not customized.
 
 
Configuring Level labels
 

 
Level labels are only an aid to configuration, they are displayed instead a level Id wherever a level appears as a property in a dialog. They do not appear at runtime.
 
 
 

 
Open the Application Explorer and select Labels under the General node.
 
 

 
Double-click Level labels in the content pane then a category of levels. There are five categories of levels:
 
 

 
Command levels
 
 

 
Browsing levels
 
 

 
Alarm acknowledge levels
 
 

 
Alarm masking levels
 
 

 
Alarm maintenance levels
 
 
 
If the project is configured as bilingual, you can use the Ellipsis button adjacent to each field to enter the string for the second language.
 
 

 
Click on a category then properties button in the toolbar. A dialog box appears. Show picture 
 

 
 
 
 

 
Enter level labels where appropriate. The labels are displayed as an aid to configuration wherever the level appears as a property in a dialog (instead a level Id). For example the configured labels appear in the drop-down list of Levels property in the Advanced tab of variable configuration.
 
 

 
Click Apply to save the changes, then OK to close the dialog box.
 
 
 
Configuring an associated label
 
There are three types of associated labels that can be configured, Bit, Alarm, and Enumerated. If you do not associate a specific associated label with a bit or alarm variable then strings from a default associated labels set are used. Default labels for bits and alarms exist and can be customized by the application developer.
 
 

 
Open the Application Explorer and select Labels under the General node.
 
 

 
Double-click Associated labels in the content pane then click Add an associated label button in the toolbar and select an associated label type. A dialog box appears. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 

 
Enter the name of a new associated label and an optional description. The name is used when attaching the label to a variable.
 
 

 
Click Labels tab. The configuration of this part depends on the associated label selected:
 
(Bit and Alarm associated labels) Enter the text strings. If the project is configured as bilingual, you can use the Ellipsis button adjacent to each field to enter the string for the second language.
With bits associated labels, you can define labels for the following elements:
 
 

 
Value - Displayed in mimics when using the Text-Label animation. In addition to strings for the zero and one values this label also allows the configuration of a string for display when the bit is invalid (unavailable).
 
 

 
Command to - Displayed in the dialog box that is used to confirm the forcing of a bit's state when using a Send-Bit animation.
 
 

 
Change to - Recorded in the archive units when a bit variable is configured as logged. Displayed in the log viewer when the recorded data is displayed.
 
 
 
(Enumerated associated labels) Click Add a label Item button, enter an enumeration value and the corresponding text strings. If the project is configured as bilingual, you can use the Ellipsis button adjacent to each field to enter the string for the second language. Show picture
 

 
 
 
With Enumeration associated labels, you can define labels for the following elements:
 
 

 
Value - Displayed in mimics when using the Text-Label animation. In addition to strings for values, this label also allows the configuration of a string for display when the variable is invalid (unavailable).
 
 

 
Command to - Displayed in the dialog box that is used to confirm the forcing of a variable value when using a Send-Register or Send-Text animations.
 
 

 
Change to - Recorded in the archive units when a variable is configured as logged.
 
 
 
The label for Default is used when the variable value is valid but not included in the enumerated list of meaningful discrete values.
 
 

 
Click Apply to save the changes, then OK to close the dialog box.
 
 
 

 
Deleting the text of a customized label revert it to its default value.
 
 
You can use the SCADA Basic instruction ASSOCLABEL to change the configuration of associated labels.
 
 There is only one set of user action associated labels and the strings it defines are used throughout the project. They are pre-defined and can be customized by the application developer. On saving, the new strings for User actions are displayed immediately for existing and new logs in any Log Viewer that is subsequently displayed.
 
Configuring Extended attribute labels
 
 

 
Open the Application Explorer and select Labels under the General node.
 
 

 
Select Extended attribute labels and click the Properties button in the toolbar. A dialog box appears. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 

 
Enter the text strings. The maximum length of the string is 255 characters and all characters are accepted except the double-quote ("). If the project is configured as bilingual, you can use the Ellipsis button adjacent to each field to enter the string for the second language.
 

 
The Reset button discards any change made and set labels to their default value.
 
 
 

 
Click Apply to save the changes, then OK to close the dialog box.
 
 
 

 
You can use the SCADA Basic instruction LABELS to get information on extended attribute labels.
 
 
Configuring Timestamp quality labels
 
 

 
Open the Application Explorer and select Labels under the General node.
 
 

 
Select Timestamp quality labels and click Properties button in the toolbar. A dialog box appears. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 

 
Enter the text strings. If the project is configured as bilingual, you can use the Ellipsis button adjacent to each field to enter the string for the second language.
 

 
The Reset button discards any change made and set labels to their default value.
 
 
 

 
Click Apply to save the changes, then OK to close the dialog box.


========================================
TOPIC: LAN_Example.htm
========================================
LAN example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LAN (Network Configuration) Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example exercises several modes of the LAN command.
 
The network structure is as follows:
 
 
An association of
 two servers.
 
The number and name of the of the
 association is: 1 and ASSOC01 respectively
 
The number of the local server station is: 12
 
The number of the remote server station is: 11
 
 
SUB Main()
 
langetlists();
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode CONNECT or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
&#160;
 
SUB lanconnect()
 
DIM iResult as Integer;
 
iResult = LAN("CONNECT","SERVER11S0");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SET_SERVER_MODE or Mode 5 (syntax 5)
 
&#160;
 
'A function to enable the local server station of the association (ASSOC01) 
 
'to be forced into the active state 
 
SUB Force_Active()
 
&#160;&#160;LAN("SET_SERVER_MODE", "ASSOC01", SYSTEM.STATION_NAME, "SET_SERVER_ACTIF");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'A function to enable the local server station of the association (ASSO10) 
 
'to be forced into the passive state 
 
SUB Force_Passive()
 
&#160;&#160;LAN("SET_SERVER_MODE", "ASSOC01", SYSTEM.STATION_NAME, "SET_SERVER_PASSIF");
 
END SUB
 
Example of modes 13, 14, 15 and 16
 
' StartConnections()
 
' -----------------

 
' Function for starting a connection with a remote station (passed in a parameter) 
 
' ARG1 = name of the remote station
 
'

 

 
SUB StartConnections()

 
&#160;&#160;DIM l_Ret AS INTEGER;

 
&#160;&#160;l_Ret = LAN( "START_CONNECTIONS", GETARG("ARG1"));

 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(@TIME, " StartConnections "" ", GETARG("ARG1"), " l_Ret = ", l_Ret);
 
END SUB

 

 
' StopConnections()

 
' -----------------

 
' Function for stopping a connection with a remote station (passed in a parameter) 

 
' ARG1 = name of the remote station
 
'

 

 
SUB StopConnections()

 
&#160;&#160;DIM l_Ret AS INTEGER;

 
&#160;&#160;l_Ret = LAN( "STOP_CONNECTIONS", GETARG("ARG1"));

 

 
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(@TIME, " StopConnections "" ", GETARG("ARG1"), " l_Ret = ", l_Ret);
 
END SUB
 
 
' StartConnection()

 
' -----------------

 
' Function for starting a connection with a remote station (passed in a parameter) 

 
' ARG1 = name of the remote station
 
' ARG2 = name of the remote connection

 
'

 

 
SUB StartConnection()

 
&#160;&#160;DIM l_Ret AS INTEGER;

 
&#160;&#160;l_Ret = LAN( "START_CONNECTION", GETARG("ARG1"), GETARG("ARG2"));

 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(@TIME, " StartConnection Remote station ", GETARG("ARG1"), " Remote connection ",GETARG("ARG2"), " l_Ret = ", l_Ret);
 
END SUB

 
' StopConnection()

 
' -----------------

 
' Function for stopping a connection with a remote station (passed in a parameter) 
 
' ARG1 = name of the remote station

 
' ARG2 = name of the remote connection

 
'

 

 
SUB StopConnection()

 

 
 
&#160;&#160;DIM l_Ret AS INTEGER;

 
&#160;&#160;l_Ret = LAN( "STOP_CONNECTION", GETARG("ARG1"), GETARG("ARG2"));

 

 
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(@TIME, " StopConnection Remote station ", GETARG("ARG1"), " Remote connection ",GETARG("ARG2"), " l_Ret = ", l_Ret);

 
END SUB

TOPIC: LANGUAGE_Example.htm
========================================
LANGUAGE example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LANGUAGE Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example exercises several modes of the LANGUAGE
 command. 
 

 
You must activate the bilingual mode in the menu Configure.Application Explorer.Doc settings.Languages.Enable bilingual text strings.
 
 
'Mode GET or Mode 0 (syntax 1)
 
SUB languageget()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = LANGUAGE("Get");
 
PRINT("The current language is:",intResult);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SET_L1 or Mode 1 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB languagesetL1()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = LANGUAGE("SET_L1",1);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SET_L2 or Mode 2 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB languagesetL2()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = LANGUAGE("SET_L2",1);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TOGGLE or Mode 3 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB languagetoggle()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
intResult = LANGUAGE("TOGGLE",1);
 
END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: Language_record_format.htm
========================================
Language record format
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Language Record Format
 
Lines starting with the keyword LANG identify the languages
 used. 
 
The fields of a Language record are as follows:
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Field
 
 
 
Description
 
 
 
Data type
 
 
 
Value
 
 
 

 
 
1
 
 
 
Record ID
 
 
 
Text
 
 
 
LANG.
 
 
 

 
 
2
 
 
 
Rank
 
 
 
Numeric
 
 
 
1 to n.
 
 
 

 
 
3
 
 
 
Language code name
 
 
 
String
 
 
 
The Microsoft identifier for the language and locale, separated
 by a hyphen.
 
 
 

 
 
4
 
 
 
Language locale identifier
 
 
 
Numeric
 
 
 
The Microsoft locale ID in decimal.
 
 
 

 
 
5
 
 
 
Role ID
 
 
 
Numeric 
 
 
 

 Always 0.
 
 
 
 
 
Example
 
This is an example of a record indicating the English
 (language) and USA (locale). 
 
LANG,1,ENU-USA,1033,0


========================================
TOPIC: Launching_the_Replication.htm
========================================
Launching the Replication
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Launching the Replication
 
This topic completes the description of how to use the 
 Replication Wizard. 
 
Once the configuration file has been created (at the 
 end of the Wizard), the replication process has to be sent to the SQL 
 servers.
 
 
In the Database Manager, 
 right-click on a replication node then click on Load in the context menu. 
 
 
In the Replication 
 Manager (a command line tool): see the topic Using 
 the Command Line Tool.
 
 
The launching of the replication depends on which kind 
 of replication you selected.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Option
 
 
 
Result
 
 
 

 
 
On demand
 
 
 
Replication starts, i.e. the data starts to be copied 
 immediately.
 
 
 

 
 
Scheduled
 
 
 
The replication is created as a job in the SQL server 
 with the appropriate schedule. The replication will be launched only when 
 the job schedule triggers.
 
 
 
 
Depending on the SQL Server and the replication-scheduling 
 configuration, some restrictions can apply.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Type of scheduling
 
 
 
SQL Server Express Edition
 
 
 
SQL Server 
 
 
 

 
 
&#160;
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 
(Workgroup, Standard, Enterprise)
 
 
 

 
 
On demand
 
 
 
Database Manager
 
Replication Manager (using the '/J' option)
 
 
 
Database Manager
 
Replication Manager
 
 
 

 
 
Schedule Replication
 
 
 
Does not work. 
 
The Replication Manager must be launched manually by the 
 user, using the '/J' option on any kind of replication.
 
You are advised to use SCADA Basic or the Windows Scheduler 
 to launch the Replication Manager.
 
 
 
Database Manager
 
Replication Manager
 
Only the first launch is required, then scheduling is 
 managed by the SQL Server schedule.
 
 
 
 
To adjust the settings and/or run a replication process
 
To adjust, run or re-run a replication 
 process from the Database Manager:
 
 
Open 
 the Database Manager from the Windows Start button (or from a desktop 
 icon, if you have created one).
 
In 
 the tree structure in the left-hand pane, open the Replication section 
 and select the replication process that is to be executed.
 
In 
 the general tab, you can adjust the settings for filtering and scheduling 
 the process: Show 
 picture

 
 
 
 Click 
 on the button Change Options at the foot of the window. That opens the 
 dialog Running Options.
 
 
In either case, you can follow 
 the steps in the topic Filtering 
 and Scheduling to adjust the settings and run the replication.
 
Saving the configuration
 
To save the changed configuration:
 
 
Click 
 on the Save button at the foot of the right-hand pane.

TOPIC: LGET_BUFFER.htm
========================================
LGET_BUFFER
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LGET_BUFFER
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Read a LONG located in a memory area.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
LongVal = LGET_BUFFER(handle, Offset);
 
Return type: LONG.
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Handle
 
 
 
The location of the memory buffer as returned by ALLOC_BUFFER. 
 Type LONG.
 
 
 

 
 
Offset
 
 
 
The offset in bytes at which the LONG is to be found. 
 Any numeric type.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 

 
 
Return:
 
 
 
A LONG located in a memory buffer reserved by ALLOC_BUFFER.
 
 
 
 
Example
 
i1 = LGET_BUFFER(handle, 2);
