Drawing a line
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Draw a Line
 
 
Select the Line tool 
 from the Draw toolbar.
 
Position the cursor 
 on the screen where the drawing is to start and click.
 
Move the cursor until 
 the line is of the required length.
 
Click the mouse 
 button.
 
 

 
If the Ctrl button is held down whilst a line is drawn 
 it will always be horizontal, vertical or at 45 degrees depending on which 
 is nearest to the co-ordinates.
 
 
Selecting the Line Style
 
The line style, color and width may be changed from the 
 Drawing Properties dialog. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
The line style may be selected from the following options.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Solid
 
 _________________
 
 

 
 
Dashed
 
 _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _
 
 

 
 
Dotted
 
 ............................
 
 

 
 
Dash dot
 
 _._._._._._._._._._._
 
 

 
 
Dash dot dot
 
 _.._.._.._.._.._.._.._
 
 
 
The line styles are available for all 
 line widths. The line styles may also be applied to polylines and 
 the border of solid drawing elements such as rectangles and polygons.
 

 
The pattern properties do affect the appearance of the 
 line.


========================================
TOPIC: line_print_conf.htm
========================================
How to configure Line printers
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to configure Line printers
 
There are two distinct printing mechanisms within PcVue, graphical printing and text printing.
 
Graphical printing allows you to print the contents of a window or the entire screen on the Windows' default printer. This mechanism is used when producing screenshots:
 
 

 
From a SCADA Basic script using the instruction HARDCOPY.
 
 

 
When printing a report from the replay dialog box.
 
 

 
When printing using the File.Print menu item.
 
 
 
In general, graphical printing uses the default printer as set in the operating system. You cannot change print settings from within PcVue. The only exception to this is when printing a window using File menu item then Print. You can select and print to any printer configured under the operating system. You can also change the printer properties as usual with the Windows Print dialog box.
 

 
When using any of the screen printing methods, some graphic controls may not print correctly.
 
 
Text printing allows you to print information in a line-based text format on any printer configured in the operating system, either locally or over a network. This mechanism is used when printing events from a log list, from the Alarm and Log viewers and when using the SCADA Basic instruction LPRINT and requires that the printer is also configured in PcVue. PcVue supports printing on up to 12 printers from a single station. For each printer that you configure, PcVue creates a print spooler, which manages the information being sent to the printer. You can also print to a text file. Text printing is always in portrait format and is not affected by changing the printer properties in the operating system.
 

 
When a printer is used to output events on the fly, PcVue needs an exclusive access to the printer. If you print to such a printer from another application, and depending on a variety of factors such as the printer driver behavior, you may cause PcVue's print spooler to stop. Printing can then only be resumed by user intervention.
 
 
A printer can only be used to print data from one source. If your project requires the use of both printing mechanisms, you need two printers. For example, you could use a dot matrix printer connected to one of the PC's USB ports to print alarms and events and a color ink jet printer connected to another PC's port or network to make screenshots.
 

 
Changes you make to a printer's properties at the operating system level, including changing the default printer and paper defaults (size, orientation...) are permanent.
 
Changes you make at the operating system level affect the printing of graphics with PcVue immediately. Text printing is not affected by printer properties.
 
 
 Configuring a line printer - Text printing
 
Configuring a line printer is a prerequisite to printing events from PcVue. Once a line printer is configured, you are able to:
 
 

 
Stream events to a printer via the built-in printer spooler.
 
 

 
 Print events and alarms displayed in an alarm or log viewer to a text file.
 
 

 
 Print events and alarms displayed in an alarm or log viewer to a Pdf or Xps file using Windows virtual printers.
 
 
 
 

 
Go to Configure then Application Explorer, expand the General node and select Line printers.
 
 

 
Click Add a line printer button in the toolbar. The Line printer creation dialog opens. Show picture
 

 
 
 
 

 
Select the printer Id. This number is used as a reference when selecting the printer in PcVue and enter a description for the printer (optional).
 
 

 
From the Print to drop-down, select whether to print to a File, to a Printer spooler, or to a Virtual printer and select the Enable box.
 
 

 
Depending on what you selected in the Print to drop-down, the following properties apply:
 
 

 
(For Printer spooler) To select a printer connected via another host on a network, click on the ellipses button in the Printer section and select a printer from the list displaying known hosts on the network then select or enter a name for the printer in the Printer name field.
 
 

 
(For File) Select or enter a name for the file to which the output is sent. The file must already exist, PcVue does not create it.
 
 

 
(For Virtual printer) Select a printer. Next, click on the ellipsis button in the Folder field to select the folder where to save files and enter the prefix string for file names. Files are named after a predefined pattern that includes the date and time.
 
 
 
 

 
Select the Advanced tab, depending on what you selected in the Print to drop-down, the following advanced properties apply:
 
 
 
 
 

 Properties
 Description
 
 

 Apply to all printer types
 
 

 Servers 
 If set, it defines the list of server stations on which the printer will be active. Only used in networked applications.
 
 

 Apply to Printer spooler
 
 

 Working folder
 Defines the folder in which the temporary files used by the spooler are stored. By default, the TEMP folder within the project.
 
 

 Character conversion
 Defines the character conversion to be performed before sending to the printer.
 
 

 TrueType font option
 Defines how TrueType fonts must be dealt with. If the printer does not support TrueType fonts, then leave this property at its default setting.
 
 

 Spooler timeout
 
 
Defines the period at which the spooler checks to see if it has anything to print.
 
 In some cases, it may be necessary to change this period to avoid overloading the printer buffer.
 
 
 

 Printer timeout
 
 
Defines the maximum period during which continuous messages are sent to a printer. After this period, messages are saved until the next spooler timeout. In some cases, it may be necessary to change this period to avoid overloading the printer buffer.
 

 
The properties Spooler timeout and Printer timeout are used together.
 
For example, if the Spooler timeout was set to 3 seconds, and the Printer timeout set to 2 seconds, messages are sent to the printer for a maximum of 2 seconds in any 3.
 
 
 
 

 Retry timeout
 
 
Defines the period at which the spooler retries printing to a failed printer (out of paper etc.). The default period is 900 seconds.
 
 
 

 Enable packet printing
 
 
If set, the legacy packet printing mechanism is activated instead of the built-in printer spooler. The spooler and printer timeout periods are ignored and the messages are buffered and printed in packets.
 
 

 
Lines per packet - If set, it defines the number of messages (lines) per packet.
 
 

 
Add form feed - If set, it adds a form feed after each packet.
 
 
 
 
 

 Open warning boxes
 If set, displays of the Windows' printing warning boxes, under error conditions, allowing the user to cancel or retry printing.
 
 

 Verbose
 If set, activates the reporting of all printer transactions in the print spooler message area.
 
 

 Apply to Virtual printer
 
 

 Margins
 Defines the margins for the printed page.
 
 

 Orientation
 Defines the orientation of the printed page. Either portrait or landscape.
 
 

 Page header and footer
 
 
Defines the look and feel of the header and footer of the page to be printed:
 
 

 
The font type, font size, and color.
 
 

 
The title of the page (Header 1) which could be a static text limited to 132 characters or a text variable limited to 256 characters as well as the printing timestamp format and alignment options.
 
 

 
The header 2 of the page which is an image. The image path has to be less than 260 characters. The path can be an absolute path or relative path to the project subfolder TP.
 
 

 
The footer page number.
 
 
 
 
 

 Page body
 
 
Defines the look and feel of the page to be printed:
 
 

 
The font type, font size, and border color and style.
 
 

 
The line spacing.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
Click OK then Apply to confirm the configuration and close the dialog.
 
 
 

 
Depending on the operating system and the printer driver, a spooler may be associated with each printer at the operating system level. Whilst PcVue is able to print with the print spoolers enabled at the system level, it delays the detection of printing issues as they only notify problems once they are full. To disable the Windows' print spooler, go to the Windows printer properties and select the option Print directly to the printer.
 
 

 
Tasks are available from the Application Explorer.
 
When the node Line printers is selected, you can:
 
 

 
Access Line printers settings.
 
 

 
Add a Line printer.
 
 

 
Enable and Disable all Line printers.
 
 
 
When a configured Line printer is selected, you can:
 
 

 
Remove the selected Line printer.
 
 

 
Access its properties.
 
 

 
Enable and Disable it.
 
 
 
 
&#160;PcVue print spooler - Text printing
 
&#160;PcVue creates a print spooler for each printer configured. The print spooler is started with its interface window minimized. To restore the Print spooler window click on its icon in the Windows' task bar. Each print spooler is identified by displaying the associated printer number in its title bar.
 
The print spooler window displays events related to the spooler activity and a number of fields displaying the spooler properties. Show picture
 

 
 
 

 
If you close a print spooler, all printing associated with that printer stop until PcVue is restarted.
 
 
You can perform the following actions on the print spooler while printing:
 
 

 
Stop - Temporarily suspends printing. PcVue continues to send messages to the spooler files, which grows in size until the spooler Start button is clicked. The printer status system variable is set to 0.
 
 

 
Start - Resumes printing. The spooler prints the contents of the spooler files until they are empty. The printer status system variable is set to 1.
 
 

 
Cancel - Minimizes the print spooler window.
 
 
 

 
Using the Start and Stop buttons in the print spooler window does not have the same effect as using the Start and Stop mode of the PRINTER instruction in a SCADA Basic program. If you stop the printer from the spooler window, you must start it again from the spooler window. If you stop the printer from a SCADA Basic program, you must start it again from SCADA Basic.
 
 
Line printers settings - Text printing
 
To configure general printer settings, right-click on Line printers and select Settings, the following settings can be configured:
 
 

 
Printing period - Defines the period at which packets are sent to the spooler for all printers having Packet printing enabled. The default is 1 second.
 
 

 
Number of lines per period - Defines the number of lines that are sent at once when a packet is to be printed for all printers having Packet printing enabled. Enabling this property is highly recommended.
 

 
For certain ink jet and laser printers, you must set the Add form feed property under Enable packet printing in the advanced properties of the line printer configuration to continue printing on the next page.
 
 
 
 
These settings determine the maximum rate at which PcVue prints to a line (text) printer if the Packet printing property is set in the printer configuration. For example, if the printing period is set to 5 seconds and the number of lines per period is set to 10, PcVue is able to print up to 10 lines once every 5 seconds. It is used to avoid congestion in the printing mechanism when attempting to print an excessive number of lines. For example on recovery from a communication failure.
 
It is included for compatibility only as the packet printing mechanism has been superseded by PcVue's print spooler.
 
Printing graphics
 
Using the file menu
 
Go to File in the main menu, you find here three buttons for printing: Print setup, Print preview, and Print. These provide the same functionality you find in most applications running under Windows (word processor, spreadsheet, etc.).
 
 

 
The Print setup button displays a standard Windows Print Setup dialog that allows you to select which printer to use and change its properties.
 

 
Any change in printer properties are temporary and do not affect the configuration of the operating system.
 
 
 

 
The print preview button displays a preview of what will actually be printed.
 
 

 
The print button prints the contents of the window that currently has the focus (the one that was last clicked).
 
 
 
Using a SCADA Basic script
 
You can print the entire screen or the current mimic using a Run Program animation to call a script taking advantage of the SCADA Basic instruction WINDOW.


========================================
TOPIC: Linking_An_Object.htm
========================================
Linking an object
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Linking an Object
 
Linking means inserting the location of a source of information 
 such as an Excel chart or a Word document into a window. Linked information 
 remains stored in the original source file although the window still displays 
 a graphic representation of the data as with embedding.
 
Example
 
As an example, we can consider the situation where the 
 management of a factory produces a report in an Excel spreadsheet of the 
 production targets for the following week. The report is updated each 
 day with revised targets. In one of the HMI�s windows, a link to the spreadsheet 
 object is made. The graphical view of the spreadsheet in the HMI�s window 
 is automatically updated each time the management revise the spreadsheet.
 
How to link an object
 
 
Open or select the 
 window in which the object is to be embedded.
 
From the Edit menu, select the 
 Insert OLE Object command.
 
Select the Create 
 from file option button.
 
In the File box type 
 or select the name of the object that you want to embed. You can use the 
 browse button to select a different drive or directory.
 
Tick the Link tick 
 box.
 
Click the OK button.
 
 
Changing a Linked Object
 
You can only change a linked object from within the original 
 application. To do this you can either:
 
 
Double click on the 
 object while the window is in Edit mode.
 
 
Or
 
 
Open the original 
 application and edit the original file.
 
 
While 
 a window is in Edit mode, a linked object is surrounded by a dashed border.


========================================
TOPIC: List_of_Color_and_Behavior.htm
========================================
List of color animations and behavior
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
List of Color Animations and Behavior
 
The number of colors that you can select depends on the 
 animation chosen. You do not have to use all of the colors. For example, 
 the animation Color - Bit Group allows you to select up to 16 colors 
 using 4 bits, but you could use it to select just 4 colors using 2 bits. 
 Whichever animation you choose, you must select at least 2 of the available 
 colors since transition to or from an unselected state is not recognized.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Animation
 
 
 
Behavior
 
 
 

 
 
Bit
 
 
 
Selection of 2 colors according to the status of a single 
 bit. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Bit group
 
 
 
Selection of 16 colors according to the binary combination of up to 4 bits. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Register bit
 
 
 
Selection of 2 colors using a bit masked from a register with a binary rank. For example, if you use the 3rd bit, when the binary 
 representation of the register is xxxxxxxxxxxx1xx the color selected for 
 1 will be displayed. When the binary representation of the register is 
 xxxxxxxxxxxx0xx the color selected for 0 will be displayed (x = either 
 1 or 0). Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Alarm
 
 
 
Selection of 5 colors according to the 5 states of an 
 alarm (Off, On - Nack, On - Ack, Off - Nack, Inhibited). If you select 
 the Static option, the colors used are those you specify in the animation. 
 If you select the Dynamic option the colors are taken from the preferences 
 for the appropriate alarm level. Changing the preferences will change 
 the colors displayed in the animation. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Bit & Alarm
 
 
 
Selection of 8 colors according to the binary combination 
 of the 2 states of a bit and 4 states of an alarm. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Weighted bits
 
 
 
Similar to above except that the value is calculated by 
 adding the values of 8 weighted bits. Bit 1 = 1, Bit 2 = 2, Bit 3 = 4, 
 Bit 4 = 8 etc. For example, 00000111 = 7. This animation is generally 
 used with binary encoded transducers. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Register value
 
 
 
Selection of up to 8 colors according to value of a single 
 register. Show picture

 
See the topic Understanding 
 the Thresholds Used in Color Register Value for an explanation of 
 threshold operation.
 
 
 

 
 
Gradient register
 
 
 
You can vary the element's color with gradations between 
 two selected colors, according to value of a single register. Show picture

 
See the topic Understanding 
 the Gradient Register Animation for an explanation of how the Gradient 
 Register animation works.
 
 
 

 Bargraph
 
 
Fill a shape according to the value of a register variable. Show picture
 

 
 
 See the topic Displaying a register as a bargraph.
 
 

 Legend
 
 
Create a numeric scale using a rectangle as the support element and a register variable to provide the range. Show picture
 

 
 
 See the topic The Legend Animation.
 
 

 
 
Text value
 
 
 
Selection of up to 128 colors according to the value of a text variable. The animation is case sensitive or not depending on the value of the following Mimic Options: Enable case sensitivity for animations that compare text strings. For example, if set for being case insensitive, HELLO, Hello, and hELLO would be treated as the same text value. Show picture


========================================
TOPIC: List_Of_Symbol_and_Behavior.htm
========================================
List of symbol animations and behavior
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
List Of Symbol Animations and Behavior
 

 

 &#160;PcVue loads all the symbols used in a symbol animation when the container mimic is opened. There is the possibility of a performance penalty in the case of very complex symbols or animations such as symbol on Register-value or Text-value (exchange among multiple symbols).
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Animation
 
 
 
Behavior
 
 
 

 
 
Bit
 
 
 
Selection of 2 symbols according to the status of a bit. 
 A third symbol may be selected to represent the invalid state. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Bit group
 
 
 
Selection of up to 16 symbol according to the binary combination 
 of up to 4 bits. An additional symbol may be specified that will be displayed if any of the bits are invalid. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Register bit
 
 
 
Selection of 2 symbols by a bit masked from a register using a binary rank. For example, if you use the 3rd bit, when the binary representation of the register is xxxxxxxxxxxx1xx the symbol selected 
 for 1 will be displayed. When the binary representation of the register is xxxxxxxxxxxx0xx the symbol selected for 0 will be displayed (x = either 1 or 0). A third symbol may be selected to represent the invalid state. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Alarm
 
 
 
Selection of 4 symbols according to the 4 states of an alarm (Off, On - Nack, On - Ack, Off - Nack). A further 4 symbols may be selected to represent the various invalid conditions that an alarm may take. Show picture

 
 
 

 
 
Register value
 
 
 
Selection of 8 symbols according to up to 8 thresholds applied to a register. An additional symbol may be selected to represent the invalid state. See the section on changing color for an explanation of threshold operation. Show picture

 
 
 

 Text value
 Selection of up to 32 symbols according to the equality of a text variable to 32 entered strings. A further three symbols can be selected according to the text variable being empty (null), not equal to any of the entered strings, or invalid. The animation is case sensitive or not depending on the value of the following Mimic Options: Enable case sensitivity for animations that compare text strings case sensitive. For example, if set for being case insensitive, HELLO, Hello, and hELLO would be treated as the same text value. Show picture


========================================
TOPIC: LISTBOX_Example.htm
========================================
LISTBOX example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LISTBOX Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example contains a set of functions to support a pair of List-box form controls using the LISTBOX instruction.
 
SUB Main()
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'declare variables:
 
SUB lbxSel()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM sLbxText As Str;
 
DIM sLbxText1 As Str;
 
DIM sLbxData As Str;
 
DIM sLbxData1 As Str;
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve content of selected item in 1st control:
 
lRet = LISTBOX ( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","lbx" );
 
LISTBOX ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","lbx1", lRet);
 
sLbxText = LISTBOX ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","lbx", lRet);
 
sLbxText1 = LISTBOX ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","lbx1", lRet);
 
sLbxData = LISTBOX ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","lbx", lRet);
 
sLbxData1 = LISTBOX ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","lbx1", lRet);
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
SET ( "LbxText" , sLbxText);
 
SET("LbxText1", sLbxText1);
 
SET("LbxData", sLbxData);
 
SET("LbxData1" , sLbxData1);
 
SET("LbxCount" , LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx") );
 
SET("LbxCount1" , LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
&#160;
 
'concatenate and load contents into a form control of each kind:
 
COMBOBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","cbx2", ADDSTRING("cbx",sLbxData));
 
LISTBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","lbx2", ADDSTRING("lbx",sLbxData));
 
CHECKLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","chk2", ADDSTRING("chk",sLbxData));
 
OPTIONLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","opt2", ADDSTRING("opt",sLbxData));
 
TREEVIEW ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","tvw2", ADDSTRING("tvw",sLbxData));
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve content of selected item in 2nd control:
 
SUB lbxSel1()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM sLbxText As Str;
 
DIM sLbxText1 As Str;
 
DIM sLbxData As Str;
 
DIM sLbxData1 As Str;
 
lRet = LISTBOX ( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","lbx1" );
 
LISTBOX ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","lbx", lRet);
 
sLbxText = LISTBOX ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","lbx", lRet);
 
sLbxText1 = LISTBOX ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","lbx1", lRet);
 
sLbxData = LISTBOX ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","lbx", lRet);
 
sLbxData1 = LISTBOX ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","lbx1", lRet);
 
lRet = LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx");
 
lRet = LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx1");
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
SET ( "LbxText" , sLbxText);
 
SET("LbxText1", sLbxText1);
 
SET("LbxData", sLbxData);
 
SET("LbxData1" , sLbxData1);
 
SET("LbxCount" , LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx") );
 
SET("LbxCount1" , LISTBOX ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","lbx1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'select 2nd item:
 
SUB setSellbx()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
LISTBOX ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","lbx", 2);
 
END SUB

TOPIC: Lists_of_tables_views_procedures.htm
========================================
Lists of tables, views and stored procedures
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Lists of Tables, Views and Stored Procedures
 
You can list the database tables, views and stored procedures 
 of a database from the tree structure in the Database Manager.
 

 
If a '+' icon is not shown beside a name in the tree structure, 
 click on the name. If there are any items to be listed within it, the 
 '+' icon will appear after a short pause.
 
 
Displaying a table
 
 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of the required database's name to open its sub-trees. Show picture

 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of Tables to list its tables.
 
Select a particular 
 table to display its properties in the right-hand pane.
 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of a particular table to list its columns as a sub-tree.
 
Select a column's 
 name in the sub-tree to display its properties or contents in the right-hand 
 pane.
 
 
Displaying a view
 
 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of the required database's name to open its sub-trees. Show picture

 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of Views to list its views.
 
Select a particular 
 view to display its properties in the right-hand pane.
 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of a particular view to list its columns as a sub-tree.
 
Select a column's 
 name in the sub-tree to display its properties or contents in the right-hand 
 pane.
 
 
Displaying a stored procedure
 
 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of the required database's name to open its sub-tree. Show picture

 
Click on the '+' 
 icon of Views to list its stored procedures as a sub-tree.
 
Select a particular 
 stored procedure's name in the sub-tree to display its properties in the 
 right-hand pane.
 
If a stored procedure 
 has a '+' icon, you can click on it to list its components.
 
Select a component's 
 name in the sub-tree to display its properties or contents in the right-hand 
 pane.
 
 

 
For details of how to display and modify the contents 
 a table, see the book Maintaining 
 Database Contents.


========================================
TOPIC: LoadingVerifying_programs.htm
========================================
Loading and Verifying Programs
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Loading and Verifying Programs
 
SCADA BASIC programs differ from normal BASIC in that 
 they must be loaded before they can be run. At load time, the program is 
 pre-compiled. The syntax of the instructions is verified and the program 
 is converted into a series of tokens in which each of the instructions 
 is represented by a 1-byte code. At that point, references to variables are not verified, nor are the existence of called functions, 
 as they may be loaded later.
 
When the program is executed, variable names are calculated 
 (using a branch if specified), and their existence is verified. 
 The same applies to any functions; they must be loaded before execution.
 
Where speed of execution is of primary importance, it 
 is recommended that CPU intensive functions are written in a compiled 
 language and stored in a dynamic link library (DLL). The functions may 
 then be called from a SCADA BASIC program.
 
Creating a user program
 

 
If you have not created a global program, or opened a 
 project that already contains one, you must first create one as described 
 in the Program Management topic.
 
 
To create and load a user program:
 
 
If it is not already 
 open, press function key F9 on the keyboard to open the Program Management 
 window of SCADA BASIC.
 
Type a program name 
 (e.g. 'user1') into the Programs box.
 
Click on the editing 
 button to the right of it ('...') to open the Editor window (which is 
 described in the next topic).
 
Type the code for 
 one user SUB into it, e.g.:
 
 
SUB user1 ( )
 
'declarations...
 
'instructions...
 
END SUB
 
 
Click on OK to close 
 the Editor window and return to the Program Manager window.
 
Click on the Load 
 button in the Programs section. Its name will appear in the Program area 
 to show that it is loaded.

Locating the original template or parameter
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Locating the Original Template or Parameter
 
The Locate Original task enables you to quickly navigate to the original of a template or parameter instance. The task is available from the context menu displayed under the following conditions.
 
 
From the Instances tab by right clicking on any of the following elements in the secondary configuration tree.
Template instance. Show picture

Included template.
Global parameter positioned at the top of the tree, on a topology element or template instance.
 
From the Templates tab by right clicking on any of the following elements in the composition tree (right pane).
Parameter or global parameter. Show picture

Included template.

TOPIC: Locking_and_Unlocking.htm
========================================
Locking and unlocking
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Locking and Unlocking
 
A drawing element that has been locked cannot be moved 
 or changed in any way while it is in Edit mode. When a locked drawing element 
 is selected, its handles have a different appearance to a normal drawing 
 element. In the picture below the drawing element on the right is locked 
 whilst the one on the left is unlocked. 
 

 
 
 
To lock a drawing element
 
 
Display the drawing 
 element's Properties dialog.
 
Click on the down 
 arrow button to display the Advanced properties.
 
Tick the Locked tick box
 
Click OK to confirm 
 the change. 
 
 
Or
 
 
Select the drawing 
 element.
 
Click the Lock button on the 
 Draw toolbar.
 
 
Or
 
 
Select the Lock command on the 
 Drawing context menu.


========================================
TOPIC: LOGDISPLAY_Example.htm
========================================
LOGDISPLAY example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LOGDISPLAY Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This program exercises several modes of the LOGDISPLAY 
 instruction. It assumes that in the project, there is a mimic called MENU 
 with a Log Viewer animation whose identifier is REPLOG01.
 

 
To find the Log Viewer's identifier, select Edit mode, 
 then select the menu Display.Properties List (name).
 
 
Configuration
 
' &#160;Create a domain DOM01
 
' &#160;Create a nature NAT01
 
'Variables used:
 
' &#160;@PRIORITY.MIN of type REGISTER
 
' &#160;@PRIORITY.MAX of type REGISTER
 
' &#160;@VALIDATE of type STATE
 
' &#160;@CODERETURN of type STATE
 
' &#160;@RETURN of type TEXT
 
Mode 0 - BEGIN (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplaybegin()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("BEGIN","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 1 - BEFORE (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplaybefore()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("BEFORE","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 3 - AFTER (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayafter()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("AFTER","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 4 - END(syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayend()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("END","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 9 - DOMAIN (syntax 2)
 
SUB logdisplaydomain()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("DOMAIN","MENU","","REPLOG01","DOM01");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB logdisplaydomainnofilter()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("DOMAIN","MENU","","REPLOG01","");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 5 - Nature (syntax 2)
 
SUB logdisplayNature()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("Nature","MENU","","REPLOG01","NAT01");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB logdisplayNaturenofilter()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("Nature","MENU","","REPLOG01","");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 12 - SETDATETIME (syntax 3)
 
SUB logdisplaysetdatetime()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
DIM dblStartTime as double;
 
DIM dblEndTime as double;
 
dblStartTime = DATETIMEVALUE("21/06/2002", "17:05:12:654");
 
dblEndTime = DATETIMEVALUE("21/06/2002", "17:10:12:654");
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("SETDATETIME","MENU","","REPLOG01",dblStartTime,dblEndTime) ;
 
END SUB
 
Mode 11 - PRINTALL (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayprintall()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("PRINTALL","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 13 - FILTER (syntax 4)
 
SUB logdisplayfilter()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
DIM strFilter as Str;
 
strFilter = "=(#t BEG DOM01)";
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("FILTER","MENU","","REPLOG01",strFilter);
 
END SUB
 
'Mode 14 - PRINT_SELECTED (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayprintselected()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("PRINT_SELECTED","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB;
 
Mode 15 - PRINT_DISPLAY (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayprintdisplay()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("PRINT_DISPLAY","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 16 - FIRST (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplayfirst()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("FIRST","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 17 - LAST (syntax 1)
 
SUB logdisplaylast()
 
'Declare return code
 
DIM intReturn as integer;
 
intReturn = LOGDISPLAY("LAST","MENU","","REPLOG01");
 
END SUB
 
Mode 23 - LINESELECT (syntax 9)
 
SUB OnLineSel()

 
LOGDISPLAY("LINESELECT", "log","","log1","main","","linesel" ,"");
END SUB

 
SUB LineSel()

 
DIM lHandle As Long;

 
DIM i As Integer;

 
DIM sPath As Str;

 
PRINT ("LINESELECT");

 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "lineselect/variable" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "lineselect/x" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "lineselect/y" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "lineselect/selected" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
PRINT (XMLPATH ( "GETDOUBLE", "/log/log1", "lineselect/time" ));
FOR (i=0;i<8;i=i+1)

 
 sPath = FORMAT ("lineselect.element[%d].value", i+1);

 
 lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", sPath );	
 
 PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
NEXT

 
XMLPATH ( "UNLOAD", "/log/log1");

 
END SUB

 
Mode 25 - GETSORT (syntax 11)
 
SUB GetSort()

 
DIM lHandle As Long;

 
LOGDISPLAY ( "GETSORT", "log","","log1");
 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "getsort/column" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
lHandle = XMLPATH ( "GET", "/log/log1", "getsort/sort" );

 
PRINT (CGET_BUFFER ( lHandle, 0, 255 ));

 
XMLPATH ( "UNLOAD", "/log/log1");

 
END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: Logging_in_using_a_new_user.htm
========================================
Logging in using a new User account
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Logging in When Using a New User Account
 

 
The following behavior is only exhibited when the advanced security strategy is enabled.
 
 
When you login to a user account, either after it has 
 been just created, unlocked following a failed login attempt, or reactivated 
 after a period of deactivation, you must change the password.
 
 
Open the Login dialog 
 (F2) and enter the Account Name and Password. If it is a new or unlocked account, 
 the password will have been allocated by the administrator. If it is an 
 account that has been reactivated, it will be the password that was used 
 before the account was deactivated. Click OK and the Change Password dialog 
 will be displayed.
 
Enter the current (old) 
 password again and the new password. You must enter the new password in both the New password and Confirm new fields.
 
Click OK to complete 
 the login with the new password.
 
 
Show picture


========================================
TOPIC: logging_log_monitor.htm
========================================
The Log Monitor
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Log Monitor
 
In addition to traces for diagnostic purpose, PcVue generates a log file combining all messages from its various components. The log file can be viewed at any time using the Log Monitor which is started from a shortcut in PcVue program group in the Start menu. If PcVue is running, events displayed in the Log Monitor are updated in real-time. The Log Monitor is particularly useful when running PcVue as a service, because in this case, the Event viewer is not accessible. Show picture

 

 
Open the Event details pane at the bottom of the window to display details related to the selected event.
 
 
Refer to the topic Logging and tracing for more information about the sv.log target and its configuration.


========================================
TOPIC: logging_log_trace.htm
========================================
Logging and tracing
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Logging and tracing
 
In addition to traces for diagnostic purpose for which more information is available in the Troubleshooting book, PcVue also supports logging and tracing for the purpose of activity monitoring.
Activity monitoring is an important facet of deployment in production as it permits collecting events relevant to alerting and incident management, forensic investigations and post mortem analysis. Logging is typically used in conjunction with the SNMP Agent Add-on. It also plays a major role in auditing and regulatory compliance activities.
 
Logging and tracing features support the following type of targets:
 
 

 
Storage of events in local log files and display with the Log monitor
 
 

 
Windows event logs and display with the Windows event viewer
 
 

 
Event sending for collection by external log aggregators, including Syslog servers
 
 
 
This topic describes how to customize the logging and tracing default settings and how to configure a Syslog target.
 

 
Logging and tracing in PcVue is based on the NLog open source project.
NLog is a flexible logging platform, it makes it easy to send events to several targets including files, databases and log aggregators. More information is available at https://github.com/NLog/NLog
 
Please contact your reseller if your requirements go beyond the information in this topic and need assistance to customize logging and tracing.
 
 

 
Because of its potential impact on the IT infrastructure, the configuration of logging and tracing targets shall be done according to the applicable IT policy and under the supervision of a responsible system administrator.
 
 
Default NLog settings
 
NLog is configured using an Xml file called NLog.Common.config found in the PcVue Bin folder. The configuration file provided by default as part of the installation defines two local targets, a file output called sv.log and the Windows event log. Show file
 

 

 

 
NLog.Common.config
 
 

 
 
 

 
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>
 
 
 

 
<nlog
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;xmlns="http://www.nlog-project.org/schemas/NLog.xsd"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance">
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- go to https://github.com/nlog/NLog/wiki/Configuration-file for more information -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<extensions>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<add assemblyFile="NLogAI.dll"/>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</extensions>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="logFilesDir"&#160;value="${binDir}\Log Files" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!--<variable name="logFilesDir" value="${binDir}\..\Logs" />-->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="localDateTime"&#160;value="${date:format=yyyy/MM/ddTHH\:mm\:ss.fffzzz}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="universalDateTime"&#160;value="${date:universalTime=true:
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;format=yyyy/MM/ddTHH\:mm\:ss.fffZ}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="sessionId"&#160;value="${processinfo:property=SessionId}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="userName"&#160;value="${windows-identity:userName=true:domain=false}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="userNameWithDomain"&#160;value="${windows-identity:userName=true:domain=true}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="shortProcessName"&#160;value="${processname:fullName=false}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<variable name="fullProcessName"&#160;value="${processname:fullName=true}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<default-target-parameters xsi:type="File"&#160;encoding="utf-8" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<target
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;name="sv.aiXml"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;xsi:type="File"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;fileName="${logFilesDir}\sv.log"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;archiveFileName="${logFilesDir}\sv-{#}.log"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;archiveAboveSize="52428800"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;archiveNumbering="Rolling"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;maxArchiveFiles="3"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;layout="${aiXmlEvent:indent=true:newLineOnAttributes=false:
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;includeSourceInfo=false:includeCallSite=false:includeStackTrace=false}">
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</target>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<target
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;name="sv.EventLog"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;xsi:type="EventLog"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;log="Application"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;source="SV Applications"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;layout="[${logger}]${newline}${message}${newline}${newline}
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;UserAccount: ${userNameWithDomain}${newline}SessionId:&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;${sessionId}${newline}ProcessName: ${shortProcessName}
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;${newline}ProcessId: ${processid}${newline}ThreadId: ${threadid}">
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</target>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<rules>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<logger name="*"&#160;minlevel="Debug"&#160;writeTo="sv.aiXml" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<logger name="*"&#160;minlevel="Info"&#160;writeTo="sv.EventLog" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</rules>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<include file="NLog.Syslog.config"&#160;ignoreErrors="true" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<include file="NLog.Custom.config"&#160;ignoreErrors="true" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
</nlog>
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
It is outside the scope of this help to provide a full description of every element, but the basic structure is as follows. The following are top level element with targets and rules mandatory in any configuration:
 
 

 
targets - Defines the log targets / outputs
 
 

 
rules - Defines which logger objects and log levels are active, and their output targets
 
 

 
extensions � loads NLog extensions from a *.dll file
 
 

 
include - Include external configuration file
 
 

 
variable - Defines configuration variables and their values. For example 
<variable name="logFilesDir" value="${binDir}\Log Files" /> defines the location of PcVue's log files folder
 
 
 
Each target is represented by a target element. There are two mandatory attributes for each target:
 
 

 
name - The name by which the target is known in the configuration file
 
 

 
xsi:type - The type of target - for example File or Database

 
 
 
In addition to these attributes, targets accept other attributes dependent on the target type. For example, for the file target:
 
 

 
 fileName defines the name of the actual target file
 
 

 
archiveFileName, archiveAboveSize, archiveNumbering and maxArchiveFiles control the size of the target file and how it is archived once that size is reached
 
 

 
layout - The contents and structure of the output
 
 
 
And for the Windows event log target.
 
 

 
source - The source of the messages which will appear in the Windows Event Viewer
 
 

 
layout - The contents and structure of the output
 
 
 

 
A full description of the NLog configuration elements can be found at https://github.com/nlog/NLog/wiki/Configuration-file
 
 
Log levels
 
Each log entry has a level. And each logger is configured to include or ignore certain levels. A common configuration is to specify the minimum level where that level and higher levels are included. For example, if the minimum level is Info, then Info, Warn, Error and Fatal are logged, but Debug and Trace are ignored.
 
 
 
 
 
 

 Log level
 Description
 Example
 
 

 Trace
 Most verbose level. Used during application development for debugging
 &#160;
 
 

 Debug
 Internal events of interest
 RunningAsService = false
 
 

 Info
 Information that highlights progress or application lifetime events
 Project loading steps
 
 

 Warn
 Warnings about validation issues or temporary failures that can be recovered
 Unable to find valid protection key
 
 

 Error
 Errors where functionality has failed or exceptions have been trapped
 Watchdog errors
 
 

 Fatal
 Critical level. Application is about to abort
 Winsock API version is invalid
 
 
 
 
The sv.log target and the Log Monitor
 
The default settings in the NLog configuration file includes a local log file target that generates a file called sv.log in the <InstallDir>\Bin\Log Files folder. The generated file has a maximum size of 50 Mb, after which it is automatically archived, and recording starts again. The archive files are named sv-n.log, where n is a number from 0 to 2 (sv-0.log is the most recent).
 
The contents of the sv.log file can be viewed in real-time using the Log Monitor. Show picture
 

 
 
 
The Windows event log target and the Windows event viewer
 
The default settings in the NLog configuration file includes a target that sends events to the Windows event log.
The Windows event log can be viewed using the Windows event viewer. Show picture
 

 
 
 
Settings for the Syslog target
 
&#160;PcVue's implementation of NLog includes a Syslog target which allows sending events to a Syslog aggregator. Syslog is defined by several RFCs and the default configuration requires tuning to accommodate the specific requirements of the tools in use in your system. Briefly, the RFCs are as follow and available on the IETF web site:
 
 

 
RFC 3164 - The original specification for the message format
 
 

 
RFC 5424 - Later specification originating from when Syslog became a recognized standard
 
 

 
RFC 5425 - Support for Syslog over TLS (transport layer security)
 
 

 
RFC 5426 - Support for Syslog over UDP
 
 

 
RFC 6587 - Transmission of Syslog Messages over TCP
 
 
 
The Syslog target is configured by adding a file named NLog.Targets.Syslog.config in the Bin folder. To see an example of this file click here. Show file
 

 

 

 
NLog.Syslog.config sample
 
 

 
 
 

 
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>
 
 
 

 
<nlog
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;xmlns="http://www.nlog-project.org/schemas/NLog.xsd"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;xmlns:sl="http://www.nlog-project.org/schemas/NLog.Targets.Syslog.xsd"
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance">
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;<!-- go to https://github.com/luigiberrettini/NLog.Targets.Syslog for more information -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;<extensions>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<add assemblyFile="NLog.Targets.Syslog.dll"/>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;</extensions>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;<targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<target name="sv.Syslog" xsi:type="sl:Syslog">
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:layout xsi:type="SimpleLayout"&#160;text="${message}${exception:format=ToString,StackTrace}" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:enforcement>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:splitOnNewLine>false</sl:splitOnNewLine>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:transliterate>false</sl:transliterate>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:replaceInvalidCharacters>false</sl:replaceInvalidCharacters>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:truncateFieldsToMaxLength>true</sl:truncateFieldsToMaxLength>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:truncateMessageTo>1024</sl:truncateMessageTo>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:enforcement>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:messageCreation>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- facility name: Local0, Local1, Local2, Local3, Local4, Local5, Local6 or Local7 -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:facility>Local4</sl:facility>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:perLogLevelSeverity>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:fatal>Critical</sl:fatal>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:error>Error</sl:error>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:warn>Warning</sl:warn>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:info>Notice</sl:info>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:debug>Informational</sl:debug>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:trace>Debug</sl:trace>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:perLogLevelSeverity>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- rfc: Rfc3164 or Rfc5424 -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:rfc>Rfc5424</sl:rfc>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- settings related to RFC 3164 -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:rfc3164 hostname="${machinename}"&#160;tag="${logger}: ">
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:outputPri>true</sl:outputPri>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:outputHeader>true</sl:outputHeader>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:rfc3164>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- settings related to RFC 5424 -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:rfc5424 hostname="${machinename}"&#160;appName="${logger}"&#160;procId="${processid}"&#160;msgId="-">
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:disableBom>false</sl:disableBom>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:rfc5424>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:messageCreation>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:messageSend>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- protocol: UDP or TCP -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:protocol>UDP</sl:protocol>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- settings related to UDP -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:udp>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:server>127.0.0.1</sl:server>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:port>514</sl:port>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:udp>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- settings related to TCP -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:tcp>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:server>127.0.0.1</sl:server>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:port>1468</sl:port>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- framing: nonTransparent or octetCounting -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:framing>octetCounting</sl:framing>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<!-- settings related to TLS -->
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:tls>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:enabled>false</sl:enabled>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:useClientCertificates>false</sl:useClientCertificates>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:certificateStoreLocation></sl:certificateStoreLocation>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:certificateStoreName></sl:certificateStoreName>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:certificateFilterType></sl:certificateFilterType>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<sl:certificateFilterValue></sl:certificateFilterValue>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:tls>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:tcp>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</sl:messageSend>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;</target>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;</targets>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;<rules>
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;<logger name="*"&#160;minlevel="Info"&#160;writeTo="sv.Syslog" />
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;</rules>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
</nlog>
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This sample file includes setting for both RFC 3164 or RFC 5424, both the TCP and UDP protocols, and TLS. If using this example as a basis for your configuration, make sure you set the rfc setting properly.
 
It is outside the scope of this help to provide a full description of every element, but the basic structure of the Syslog target element is as follows:
 
 

 
enforcement - Settings related to enforcing message rules. The maximum length of a message is defined by the supported RFC's and the setting TruncateMessageTo must be set accordingly.
 
 

 
messageCreation - Settings related to message creation in the format defined by either RFC 3164 or RFC 5424.
 
 

 
messageSend - Settings related to how the message is sent. This includes the protocol used, either TCP or UDP, the server and port address and transport layer security parameters. 
 
 
 

 
A full description of the Syslog configuration elements for NLog can be found at https://github.com/luigiberrettini/NLog.Targets.Syslog
 
 

 
While the base NLog and Syslog components provided as part of the installation packages are kept up-to-date on a regular basis, some configuration elements described on the GitHub pages given here as references may only be applicable to a version of the components that is not yet provided with PcVue&#160;.
 
 

 
When using TCP, the setting called framing, defined in RFC 6587 (octetCounting vs nonTransparent), often need to be adjusted to fit the Syslog receiver's expectation. Please refer to the documentation of the Syslog aggregator you use.


========================================
TOPIC: LOGICAL_Example.htm
========================================
LOGICAL example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
LOGICAL Examples
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example 1
 
This example exercises several modes of the LOGICAL command.
 
'Mode NOT or Mode 0 (syntax 1)
 
SUB logicalnot()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue As INTEGER;
 
&#160;
 
lngValue = 1; '00.....0001
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("NOT",lngValue );
 
PRINT("NOT(",lngValue,")= ",lngReturn);
 
&#160;
 
'Display NOT(1)= -2 '11.....1110
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode AND or Mode 1 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB logicaland()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
&#160;
 
lngValue1 = 1; '00.....0001
 
lngValue2 = 3; '00.....0011
 
&#160;
 
'AND= 00.....0001
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("AND",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," AND ",lngValue2," = ",lngReturn);
 
&#160;
 
'Display "1 AND 3 = 1"
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode XOR or Mode 2 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB logicalxor()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
&#160;
 
lngValue1 = 1; '00.....0001
 
lngValue2 = 3; '00.....0011
 
&#160;
 
'XOR= 00.....0010
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("XOR",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," XOR ",lngValue2," = ",lngReturn);
 
'Display "1 XOR 3 = 2"
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode OR or Mode 3 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB logicalor()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
&#160;
 
lngValue1 = 1; '00.....0001
 
lngValue2 = 3; '00.....0011
 
&#160;
 
'OR= 00.....0011
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("OR",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," OR ",lngValue2," = ",lngReturn);
 
'Display "1 OR 3 = 3"
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SHIFT_LEFT or Mode 4 (syntax 2)
 
SUB logicalshiftleft()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
&#160;
 
lngValue1 = 1; '00.....0001
 
lngValue2 = 3; 'shift by 3 digits
 
&#160;'SHIFT_LEFT = 00.....1000
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("SHIFT_LEFT",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," SHIFT_LEFT ",lngValue2," = ",lngReturn);
 
'Display "1 SHIFT_LEFT 3 = 8"
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode SHIFT_RIGHT or Mode 5 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB logicalshiftright()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
lngValue1 = 8; '00.....1000
 
lngValue2 = 3; 'shift b 3 digits
 
'SHIFT_RIGHT = 00.....0001
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("SHIFT_RIGHT",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," SHIFT_RIGHT ",lngValue2," = ",lngReturn);
 
'Display "8 SHIFT_RIGHT 3 = 1"
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode MODULO or Mode 6 (syntax 2)
 
&#160;
 
SUB logicalmodulo()
 
'Declare variables
 
DIM lngReturn As LONG;
 
DIM lngValue1 As INTEGER;
 
DIM lngValue2 As INTEGER;
 
lngValue1 = 14;
 
lngValue2 = 12;
 
'Calculate modulo: 14 = 12 + 2 
 
'decimal 14 equals 2 modulo 12
 
lngReturn = LOGICAL("MODULO",lngValue1,lngValue2);
 
PRINT(lngValue1," = ",lngReturn," (mod ",lngValue2,")");
 
'Display "14 = 2 (mod 12)"
 
&#160;
 
END SUB 
 
Example 2
 
This program shows the use of the LOGICAL instruction.
 
SUB main()
DIM x As LONG;
DIM y As LONG;
x = 622;
y = 217;
PRINT ("x = ", x);
PRINT ("y = ", y);
PRINT (" not(x) =", logical("not", x));
PRINT (" x and y =", logical("and", x, y));
PRINT (" x xor y =", logical("xor", x, y));
PRINT (" x or y =", logical("or", x, y));
PRINT (" x << 2 =", logical("shift_left", x, 2));
PRINT (" x >> 2 =", logical("shift_right", x, 2));
PRINT (" x modulus 7 =", logical("modulo", x, 7));
END SUB
 
The program displays:
 
x = 622 
y = 217 
not(x) =-623 
x and y =72 
x xor y =695 
x or y =767 
x << 2 =2488 
x >> 2 =155 
x modulus 7

TOPIC: Logical_Ops.htm
========================================
Logical Operators
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Logical Operators
 

 
 

 

 

 

 See also
 
 
The logical operators are:
 
 
 
 

 
 
Symbol
 
 
 
Operation
 
 
 

 
 
!=
 
 
 
Inequality
 
 
 

 
 
==
 
 
 
Equality
 
 
 

 
 
>
 
 
 
Greater than
 
 
 

 
 
>=
 
 
 
Greater than or equal to
 
 
 

 
 
<
 
 
 
Less than
 
 
 

 
 
<=
 
 
 
Less than or equal to
 
 
 

 
 
!
 
 
 
Negation
 
 
 

 
 
||
 
 
 
Logical Or
 
 
 

 
 
&&
 
 
 
Logical And
 
 
 
 
Example 1
 
DIM I1 As Integer, I2 As Integer;
 
If (I1 == I2) then ...
 
If (I1 >= I2) then ...
 
Example 2
 
DIM Bool1 As Integer, Bool2 As Integer;
 
Bool1 = 0;
 
Bool2 = 1;
 
If (Bool1 == Bool2) then ...
 
If (Bool1 || Bool2) then ...
 

 
String concatenations and comparisons may only be done 
 using specialized functions.
 
Logical operations on register variables are made using the LOGICAL or LOGICAL64 instructions.

TOPIC: Making_models_available_to_the_HMI.htm
========================================
Making 3D models available to the HMI
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Making 3D Models Available to the HMI
 
When the HMI is required to insert a 3D model into a 
 3D mimic, it searches for the file by name in the 3D folder. 
 
To make a particular model available:
 
 
Copy it, or the set 
 of models that includes it, from the project's folder \Shared Libraries\3DModels 
 (or an external source) to the 3D folder of the project.
 
Restart the HMI to 
 make the 3D models available in the HMI.
 
 

 
If you are starting from scratch, or from an understanding 
 of 2D techniques, please refer to the 3D Mimics chapter.


========================================
TOPIC: Management_Box.htm
========================================
Mimic management dialog
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Mimic Management Dialog
 
The Mimic Management dialog is displayed when you 
 save a mimic for the first time, save a mimic with a new name and when 
 opening a mimic using the Open tool or the File.Open command. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
It provides a list of mimics, the option of a preview 
 display of the selected mimic and tab options for selecting the mimic 
 Library and Branch. The list may be switched between a simple or detailed 
 format using the display buttons.
 
Tab Options and Command Buttons
 
 
Folder - This 
 can either be Mimics if you are saving or opening a normal mimic or Mimic 
 templates if you are creating or loading a mimic template. See the topic 
 on mimic templates for further information.
 
Library - The name 
 of the Library in which the mimic file is to be saved or opened from. 
 By default, there is one Library known as Local. Other Libraries may be 
 available depending on the configuration of the Project.
 
Name - The name of 
 the mimic to be opened or saved. You may type the name directly into this 
 field or select it by clicking in the list. The name must comply with 
 MS Windows' file naming conventions.
 
Filter - A lexical 
 filter that may be applied to reduce the number of mimic names displayed 
 in the list.
 
List display - Show a list of details of the mimics.
 
Columns display - List only the mimic names in columns.
 
Refresh - Refresh the list.
 
Preview - Show a preview image of the selected mimic in the right-hand pane.
 
Branch - The name 
 of a variable Branch to be used with the mimic. See the topic on opening 
 a mimic with a branch for further information.


========================================
TOPIC: Managing_scroll_bar_controls_from_VBA.htm
========================================
Managing scroll bar controls from VBA
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Managing scroll bar controls from VBA
 
A window displays a scroll bar when part of a mimic is hidden by the window frame. The workspace can also display scroll bars, but this topic does not apply to them.
 
You can manage window scroll bars from VBA by using these elements: 
 
 
The Mimic object event WindowScroll. It applies to the mimic associated with the window
 
The Window object's methods GetScrollPosition, SetScrollPosition and GetScrollHighPosition
 
 
Mimic and Window objects
 
The HMI exposes a Mimic/Window object model. In it, a Window object is the visual representation, a view of a Mimic object. For one Mimic object, several Window objects can be present at the same time with different scroll bar control positions.
 
Scroll bar controls
 
Whether scroll bar controls are visible or not depends on the mimic size, window size and zoom level. When the window scroll bar controls are visible, you can scroll the window contents horizontally or vertically in these four ways:
 
 
By 'line' - by increments in each direction.
 
By 'page' - to jump to a position above/below or left/right of the visible part of the window.
 
By moving the scroll box in the scroll bar.
 
By turning the mouse wheel.
 
 
Show picture
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
Limits of positioning
 
Each scroll bar control has a low limit position of 0 to represent the top or left end of the window frame, and a high limit position for the bottom or right end. These parameters are stated in logical units (by default, pixels).

TOPIC: Manual.htm
========================================
Generating an export manualy
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Generating an Export Manually
 
A User with the Export Manually user right can, on-demand, generate an entire Export, or an individual trend page or log page. The Export Management dialog can be opened independently of the Application Explorer using function key F11. Show picture

 

 
You can also open the Export Management dialog using a button in a mimic and the Macro animation including the line STANDARDACTION,11.
 
 
When you manually generate an Export, you must configure the Period Selection using one of the three methods described in the topic Understanding the Period Used by Data Export. If generating an individual trend page or log page the Period Selection properties of the Generate Export dialog are completed automatically using the default from the trend or log page configuration. If generating an entire Export the Period Selection properties must be manually entered. Show picture


========================================
TOPIC: Manually_configure_client_server_links.htm
========================================
How to manually configure the client server links
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Manually Configure Client Server Links
 
By default, all clients are automatically linked to all 
 servers. If your application requires something different you must un-tick 
 the Create 
 all links property, in the Networking Settings dialog, before configuring any nodes and then manually 
 configure the links between the clients and servers.
 
How to manually configure client server links
 
 
Open the Application Explorer and expand the configuration tree to select the Networking folder.
 
From the task list, select Settings. The Network Settings dialog opens.
 
Un-tick the Create 
 all links property and close the dialog.
 
Using the configuration tree select a station and then its node.
 
Use the Properties task to open the Node Properties dialog. Select the Links tab.
 
Expand the configuration 
 tree to display the available server nodes.
 
Tick the server nodes 
 to which the client will be linked. Show picture

 
Click the OK 
 button to confirm the configuration and close the dialog.


========================================
TOPIC: MAPDISPLAY_Example.htm
========================================
MAPDISPLAY example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
MAPDISPLAY Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises modes of the MAPDISPLAY command.
 
Dim MimicName as Str;	'Name of a mimic containing the Map Control
 
Dim MimicBranch as Str;	'Branch of the window containing the Map Control
 
Dim Id as Str; 'Id of the Map control graphic object
 
Dim LayerName as Str;	'Name of a layer
 
Dim MarkerName as Str;	'Name of a marker.
 
&#160;
 
'This sub is used to initialize program variables
 
SUB Main()
 
MimicName = "WIN_MAP"; 'Name of the mimic containing the Map graphic control
 
Id = "ID_MAP"; 'The If of the Map graphic control in the mimic
 
LayerName = "MyLayer"; 'Name of a Layer
 
MarkerName = "MyMarker";'Name of a marker
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'RESET
 
'This sub can be called to reset a Map control runtime properties
 
'and fall-back on configuration values defined at design-time
 
SUB RESET()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("LOADDEFAULT",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id);
 
Print("Mode LOADDEFAULT executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'MAP_NAV
 
'This sub gets and sets map navigation positions: Zoom level, latitude & longitude
 
SUB MAP_NAV()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim dRet as Double;
 
&#160;
 
'Get map navigation info
 
dRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETZOOM",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id);
 
Print("Mode GETZOOM executed with result: ", dRet);
 
dRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETLATITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id);
 
Print("Mode GETLATITUDE executed with result: ", dRet);
 
dRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETLONGITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id);
 
Print("Mode GETLONGITUDE executed with result: ", dRet);
 
'Set map navigation info
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETZOOM",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,4);
 
Print("Mode SETZOOM executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETLATITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,12.5);
 
Print("Mode SETLATITUDE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETLONGITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,45);
 
Print("Mode SETLONGITUDE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
'Center the map control on a marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("CENTERTOMARKER",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode CENTERTOMARKER executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'EXPORT_GPX
 
'This sub can be called to export a Map control configuration to a .Gpx file: Layers and markers
 
SUB EXPORT_GPX()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim Filename as Str; ' Name of the file to use as output - A path relative to the \GIS folder
 
&#160;
 
Filename = "ExportedMarkers.gpx";
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("EXPORT",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,Filename);
 
Print("Mode EXPORT executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'IMPORT_GPX
 
'This sub can be called to import a .Gpx file
 
'The import is asynchronous - The import completion is executed via the call-back sub IMPORT_GPX_CALLBACK
 
SUB IMPORT_GPX()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim MarkersFilename as Str; ' Name of the file to use as input - A path relative to the \GIS folder
 
Dim Asynch as Integer; 'Flag indicating whether the file should be loaded synchronously (0) or asynchronously (1)
 
&#160;
 
Asynch = 1;
 
MarkersFilename = "MarkersForImport.gpx";
 
@VarResult = 0;
 
&#160;
 
'Add an event to call the call-back function whenever VarResult changes
 
iRet = EVENT("ADDPROG", "@VarResult", "ALL>ALL", "LOADER", "", "IMPORT_GPX_CALLBACK");
 
Print("Call back event created with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("LOADMARKERS",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,MarkersFilename,Asynch,"@VarResult");
 
Print("Mode LOADMARKERS executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB IMPORT_GPX_CALLBACK()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
if (@VarResult != 1) then	'Import no longer in progress (completed with success or failure)
 
'Delete the call-back event
 
iRet = EVENT("DELPROG", "@VarResult", "ALL>ALL", "LOADER", "", "IMPORT_GPX_CALLBACK");
 
Print("Call back event deletion with result: ", iRet);
 
Print("Mode LOADMARKERS completed with result: ", @VarResult);
 
end if
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'LAYER_INFO
 
'This sub gets and sets the visibility of a layer.
 
SUB LAYER_INFO()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETLAYERVISIBILITY",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,1);
 
Print("Mode SETLAYERVISIBILITY executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("ISLAYERDISPLAYED",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName);
 
Print("Mode ISLAYERDISPLAYED executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'MARKER_INFO
 
'This sub gets and sets several properties of a marker.
 
SUB MARKER_INFO()
 
Dim dRet as Double;
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim sRet as Str;
 
Dim iChildMode as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
'Get the type of a marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERTYPE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERTYPE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
&#160;
 
'Get the latitude & longitude of a marker
 
dRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERLATITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERLATITUDE executed with result: ", dRet);
 
dRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERLONGITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERLONGITUDE executed with result: ", dRet);
 
'Set the latitude & longitude of a marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERLATITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,50);
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERLATITUDE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERLONGITUDE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,1);
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERLONGITUDE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
&#160;
 
'Get and set the tooltip of a marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERTOOLTIP",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,"A tooltip set by script");
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERTOOLTIP executed with result: ", iRet);
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERTOOLTIP",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERTOOLTIP executed with result: ", sRet);
 
&#160;
 
'Get and set the visibility of a marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERVISIBILITY",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERVISIBILITY executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERVISIBILITY",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,1);
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERVISIBILITY executed with result: ", iRet);
 
'Get and set the mimic associated to a marker
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERMIMIC",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERMIMIC executed with result: ", sRet);
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETMARKERBRANCH",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName);
 
Print("Mode GETMARKERBRANCH executed with result: ", sRet);
 
&#160;
 
iChildMode = 1;	'A pop-up mimic will be attached to the marker
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERMIMIC",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,"ChildMimic",iChildMode);
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERMIMIC executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMARKERBRANCH",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,"ChildBranch");
 
Print("Mode SETMARKERBRANCH executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'MARKER_USERDATA
 
'This sub gets and sets user data associated to a marker.
 
SUB MARKER_USERDATA()
 
Dim sRet as Str;
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim sUserData as Str;
 
&#160;
 
sUserData="MyUserData";
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETUSERDATAVARNAME",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,sUserData);
 
Print("Mode GETUSERDATAVARNAME executed with result: ", sRet);
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETUSERDATALABEL",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,sUserData);
 
Print("Mode GETUSERDATALABEL executed with result: ", sRet);
 
sRet = MAPDISPLAY("GETUSERDATAVALUE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,sUserData);
 
Print("Mode GETUSERDATAVALUE executed with result: ", sRet);
 
&#160;
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETUSERDATALABEL",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,sUserData,"A new userdata label");
 
Print("Mode SETUSERDATALABEL executed with result: ", iRet);
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETUSERDATAVALUE",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,LayerName,MarkerName,sUserData,"A new userdata value");
 
Print("Mode SETUSERDATAVALUE executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'ISLOADING
 
'This sub can be called to know if the Map control loading is in progress or completed
 
SUB ISLOADING()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("ISLOADING",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id);
 
Print("Mode ISLOADING executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'SETMAPPROVIDER
 
'This sub can be called to change the Map tile provider at runtime
 
SUB SETPROVIDER()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim sProvider as Str;
 
Dim iLocal as Integer;
 
Dim iCache as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
sProvider="MyNewProvider"; 'Name of the map provider - The map will be unusable if the value does not match a supported provider
 
iLocal=1;'Example 'Local map or online.
 
iCache=1;'Example 'Flag indicating to use the cache.
 
&#160;
 
iRet = MAPDISPLAY("SETMAPPROVIDER",MimicName,MimicBranch,Id,sProvider,iLocal,iCache);
 
Print("Mode SETMAPPROVIDER executed with result: ", iRet);
 
END SUB

TOPIC: Masking_by_Program.htm
========================================
Masking by Program
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Masking by Program
 

Applies to
 
Masking is normally a temporary operation connected 
 with the current state of the process being monitored. An alarm that 
 has any mask other than 0 continues to be scanned but its value will not 
 be propagated outside the real time database. Hence any activities connected 
 to that alarm will stop. This includes alarming and recording to disk. 
 The value is still accessible however, by a SCADA BASIC program. The mask 
 of an alarm may be used and cleared from several sources, each represented 
 by a binary weight in the mask: 
 
 
By a SCADA BASIC 
 program. Up to four levels of mask may be set in this way:
 
 
1 = Level 1 
 
2 = Level 2 
 
4 = Level 3 
 
8 = Level 4
 
 
By the operator. 
 For example, an alarm bit may be masked using an option available from 
 the Alarm Viewer. The binary weight of this option is 16.
 
 
 
By dependence. If 
 the value of a variable depends on another, for example a threshold bit, 
 the source variable going invalid will cause the dependent variable mask 
 to be set causing it also to be invalid. The binary weight of this option 
 is 32. Another example of an alarm that may appear as masked is an alarm 
 that is inhibited by another alarm or bit.
 
 
When an alarm is unmasked, its value immediately becomes 
 available.
