========================================
TOPIC: max-io-startup-aborted.htm
========================================
How to start PcVue if the number of licensed I/O variables is exceeded
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to start PcVue if the number of licensed I/O variables is exceeded
 
&#160;PcVue only checks the number of configured I/O variables against the number allowed by the license at startup. If PcVue finds more variables in its configuration than are allowed it will not start.
 
You cannot start PcVue if you have exceeded the number of variables allowed by the license. Instead, you will have to recover an earlier version of the PcVue variable configuration. If you have not taken a recent backup of the project, you can use the following procedure:
 
 

 
Locate the folder containing the project on which you are working.
 
 

 
Open the CTEMP folder and copy the file VAREXP.DAT (the variables tree definition file).
 
 

 
Open the C folder and rename the existing file from VAREXP.DAT to VAREXP.BAK.
 
 

 
Paste in the copy of VAREXP.DAT from the CTEMP folder.
 
 

 
The copy of VAREXP.DAT in CTEMP is written each time PcVue is successfully started.


========================================
TOPIC: Methodology.htm
========================================
Application Architect methodology
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Application Architect Methodology
 
There are two generally accepted approaches to modeling known as top-down and bottom-up.
 
 
Top-down - In a top-down approach an overview of the system is produced, defining first-level subsystems but not detailing base elements. Each subsystem is then refined in yet greater detail, usually by a breakdown in many additional subsystem levels, until the base elements of the entire system can be defined.
 
Bottom-up - In a bottom-up approach, the individual base elements of the system are first specified in great detail. These elements are then linked together to form larger subsystems, which then in turn are linked, sometimes in many levels, until a complete top-level system is formed. 
 
 
Whichever approach you choose, the general procedure within the Application Architect will follow these steps:
 
 
Identify templates.
High-level templates first corresponding to sub-systems if using a top-down approach.
Base elements first if using a bottom-up approach.

 
Identify parameters.
 
Identify dependencies (instantiation rules, what is child of what).
 
Define templates.
 
Define global parameters. That is those that affect a big percentage of a particular property type. Typically Station Lists, Network Name, Domain, Nature etc.
 
Associate global parameters with those templates for which they affect all instances. 
 
Define parameters. That is those that affect a smaller percentage of one or more properties. For example, the range (Max & Min) for all registers representing a temperature. Associate parameters with templates.
 
Differentiate properties by defining them by a global parameter, parameter, an expression or an input
 
Create any topology elements. In particular, in a bottom-up approach, higher level subsystems that are not yet be identified / defined as templates.

 
Instantiate templates.
 
Associate global parameters with the elements of the topology to which they are relevant (tree, topology element, template instance included templates).
 
Define parameters' and inputs' values.
 
Generate.
 
Refine by iteration.


========================================
TOPIC: mimics_and_models.htm
========================================
3D mimics and models
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3D Mimics and Models
 
The 3D features of the HMI use a new kind of mimic. In 
 general terms, it follows design and usage principles that broadly resemble 
 those of classic 2D mimics for manipulating views and animations. 
 
 
A 2D mimic only has 
 two dimensions and those coincide with the plane of the PC display's screen.
 
A 3D mimic appears 
 as if it has three dimensions, though all you see is a projection of its 
 contents onto the PC screen. 
 
 
Both 2D and 3D mimics can co-exist in a project and can 
 be displayed on the same PC screen. Although 2D and 3D drawing elements 
 have many features in common, they can only exist in their own kind of 
 mimic. 
 
You become aware of the 3D simulation in a 3D mimic through 
 lighting effects and by the movements of visual objects: by rotation, 
 change in size, movement in relation to each other and shifting your point 
 of view. You can also, as in 2D, open multiple copies of the same 3D mimic. 
 For example, with a front view and a top view of the same scene, you can 
 see where the objects are in all three dimensions.
 

 
You can only use 2D drawing elements in 2D mimics and 
 only 3D models in 3D mimics. However, many of the animation features of 
 the HMI can be applied to both.
 
 
3D and 2D drawing elements
 
A 3D drawing element is known as a 3D model. A 3D model 
 is, in some ways, similar to a symbol in a 2D mimic. A symbol is composed 
 of several discrete drawing elements each with its own properties. 
 A 3D model is composed of several discrete frames each with its own 
 properties. Unlike a 2D symbol, you cannot un-group a 3D model and return it to its component frames, although you can use the Graphic 
 Explorer to examine and change some of their properties.
 
What you can do in the HMI
 
In the HMI, you can do the following.
 
 
Insert an existing 
 3D model which could be anything from a simple 3D shape such as a sphere, 
 to a highly complex drawing such as an entire building. 
 
Use the Graphic Explorer 
 to display the hierarchy of frames in a model.
 
Use the Graphic Explorer 
 to display and change a frame's transformation and material properties.
 
Insert and configure 3D camera objects in a 3D scene so as to define custom points of view.
 
 
The above are features of 3D modeling in general. What 
 is specific to the HMI is that:
 
 
You can assign animations 
 to modify the transformations and materials of any models, frames or sub-frames. 
 
 
You can create and 
 animate symbols from 3D models. 
 
These animations 
 are connected to real-time data, just as in 2D mimics.

TOPIC: mobile_loglist.htm
========================================
How to add a Log List to the mobile server
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Add a Log List to the Mobile Server
 
To enable the viewing of logged data in TouchVue you must add one or more Log Lists to the Mobile Server. The contents of the Log List (or a subset thereof) is then available to the TouchVue user.
 
How to add a Log List to the Mobile Server
 
 
Open the Application Explorer and select the Mobile Server.
Open the Application Explorer, in the configuration tree, expand the Communication node.
Select the Servers node, and in the right pane, select the Mobile server.
 
Select and configure the Log List
Display the available Log Lists by clicking the Log List tool in the toolbar of the lower pane. Show picture

Select the appropriate Log List. 
A dialog will open displaying the Log List's configuration. If required, you can select a sub-set of the Log Lists' configuration.
Select or de-select as appropriate, the property Allow modification of filter on the client side. Selection of this property allows the TouchVue user to change the Log filter at run-time when using the TouchVue app.
Click OK to confirm the configuration and close the Log Filter dialog.
 
Repeat step 2 to add further Log Lists. The Log List in use at run-time can be selected by the user of the TouchVue app.

========================================
TOPIC: Modelling_overview.htm
========================================
Overview of 3D modeling
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Overview of 3D Modeling
 
3D modeling is a huge, rapidly developing and diversifying 
 field of activity. It is employed in many business and recreational pursuits. 
 Any experience you may have in 3D computer applications, or of navigating 
 a craft or tool within a 3D space, will assist you with the principles 
 and techniques that apply here.
 
A basic vocabulary is common across applications of 3D. 
 So are many software functions, tools, utilities and programming interfaces, 
 plus hardware components such as video (graphic) and multimedia PC cards. 
 However, there is a gradation from general capabilities up to those requiring 
 extreme performance plus specialized toolsets and the latest expertise. 
 The 3D facilities in the HMI are situated toward the basic end of that 
 scale.
 
You can insert 3D objects into the HMI and configure 
 them, but the HMI does not have a capability for creating 3D objects.
 
Kinds of '3D' object
 
The 3D objects employed here consist of software files 
 that are used to generate and display images on a 2D screen. These images 
 can look solid, fluid or vaporous in whole or in part. This has nothing directly to do with objects having 3D 
 appearances as follows:
 
 
2D buttons, backgrounds 
 and HMI drawing elements of PcVue that look as if they were sculpted and lighted. 
 The '3D' symbols that can be displayed in the HMI's 2D mimics are like 
 that.
 
Stereograms, which 
 are usually double images viewed together to give an illusion of depth.
 
Holograms, which 
 are images recorded and presented in three dimensions.
 
 
Limits of this Help book
 
This Help book is limited to the basic understanding 
 needed to configure 3D models and mimics for industrial monitoring and 
 control through the HMI.
 
 
It does not provide 
 a full description of 3D technology with DirectX.
 
The information it 
 does provide is often simplified for ease of reading.
 
 
It is structured and expressed as far as possible to 
 resemble the Help for PcVue's 2D drawing, animation and tools. 
 However, the explanations for things in the 3D world often have to cover 
 features that have no equivalent in the 2D world. For those, a distinct 
 set of concepts is necessary, with terminology to suit.
 

 
If you are starting from scratch, or from an understanding 
 of 2D techniques, please refer to the Getting Started Manual for 3D Mimics.

It is possible to have more than one view of the same

 be independently moved, zoomed and switched between Design and Run modes. 
 For example, you could have:
 
 
One view in Run mode 
 and the other in Design mode.
 
One view zoomed in 
 to change the fine detail and another view at full size to see the effect 
 of those changes.

Select the mimic 
 by clicking anywhere in it.
 
Select the command Window.New 
 Window.
 
 
Or
 
 
Right-click on the 
 window title bar and select the command New Window from the 
 context menu.

TOPIC: Multi-station_projects.htm
========================================
Using network-dependent profiles
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Using Network-dependent Profiles
 
On networked (multi-station) projects, you can configure user accounts so that the profile that is active when the user logs on, is dependent on which station he/she is using. To do this you attach more than one profile to each user. Each profile is associated with a station list that determines on which stations it is active. Station lists are created when defining the station network communication. The user will not be available on any station that is not in at least one of the station lists as there will be no active profile for that station.Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Enabling network dependent profiles
 
To use network dependent profiles you must first tick the option Enable network dependent profile association in the User Accounts settings.
 
How to add a profile and station list to a user
 
 
Select the Users folder in the Application Explorer configuration tree and select the user to which the profile is to be added.
 
From the task list, select Add a user profile. A pop-up dialog is displayed containing a list of available profiles
 
Select the profile using the drop-down list box.
 
Select the station list using the drop-down list box and click OK.
 
 
Distributing the user accounts configuration File
 
The user accounts are not automatically distributed on 
 a multi-station system. To distribute the user accounts you must first shutdown PcVue on all stations and then copy the file User.dat (from the 
 project's C directory) to all the stations in the project.
 
Alternatively, you can use a small SCADA Basic program 
 to distribute the file.
 
Show example
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
DIM fbuf1 as long;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'handle of the buffer returned by FSTAT
 
 
 

 
DIM user1 as str;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'location of the file user.dat
 
 
 

 
DIM date as STR;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'Modification date of the file
 
 
 

 
CONST TAILLE =&#160;0;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'offset for the file size
 
 
 

 
CONST MODIF =&#160;4;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'offset for the file date
 
 
 

 
CONST ALLOC =&#160;64;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;'22 is the minimum buffer size
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
sub main()
 
 
 

 
user1=&#160;"..\\CTEMP\\user.dat";
 
 
 

 
fbuf1 =&#160;ALLOC_BUFFER&#160;(ALLOC);
 
 
 

 
FSTAT(user1,fbuf1);
 
 
 

 
date =&#160;CGET_BUFFER(fbuf1,MODIF,20);
 
 
 

 
CYCLIC("ADDPROG",5,"DIFFUSION","","lastmodif");
 
 
 

 
end sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
sub lastmodif()&#160;
 
 
 

 
dim date_modif as STR;
 
 
 

 
FSTAT(user1,fbuf1);
 
 
 

 
date_modif =&#160;CGET_BUFFER(fbuf1,MODIF,20);
 
 
 

 
IF&#160;(CMPSTRING(date,date_modif)&#160;<>&#160;0) THEN
 
 
 

 
diff();
 
 
 

 
END IF
 
 
 

 
date = date_modif;
 
 
 

 
end sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
sub diff()
 
 
 

 
'This function has to be completed by launching a batch file to copy user.dat file
 
 
 

 
to other stationsprint("Distribution of user.dat on all stations (directory C and CTEMP)");
 
 
 

 
end sub

TOPIC: MULTIMEDIA_Example.htm
========================================
MULTIMEDIA example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
MULTIMEDIA Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This example exercises modes of the MULTIMEDIA command.
 
It uses:
 
 
A mimic: MENU
 
A wave file Alarm.wav 
 in the TP folder
 
An avi file demo.avi 
 in the TP folder
 
 
It returns "true" or "false" according 
 to the CD drive's capabilities.
 
SUB capabilityeject()
 
PRINT(MULTIMEDIA("SEND","cdaudio function can eject"));
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Begins recording. Print for debugging.
 
SUB startrec()
 
PRINT(MULTIMEDIA("SEND","open new type waveaudio alias mysnd"));
 
PRINT(MULTIMEDIA("SEND","record mysnd"));
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'To play a wave sound
 
'The .wav file must be present in the TP folder of this project 
 
SUB playwav()
 
MULTIMEDIA ("SEND","Play Alarm.wav");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Opening an animation sequence with video
 
SUB play()
 
IF (Window("IS_OPEN","MENU","")) THEN 
 
&#160;&#160;MULTIMEDIA ("OPENW","movie","demo.avi","MENU", "",70,10,320,240);
 
ELSE
 
&#160;&#160;MULTIMEDIA ("OPENW","movie","demo.avi","Projection");
 
END IF
 
&#160;&#160;MULTIMEDIA ("SEND","play movie");
 
&#160;&#160;MULTIMEDIA ("SEND","set movie time format frames");
 
END SUB

TOPIC: Multiple.htm
========================================
Making Multiple Copies of a Configuration Object Using Copy and Paste
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Making Multiple Copies of a Configuration Object Using Copy and Paste
 
Depending on the object that has been copied, it is possible to paste multiple copies using the Multiple Paste command. The Multiple paste command is available from the context menu only when a supported object has been copied.
 
How to paste multiple copies of an object
 
 
Copy the object in the normal manner using Ctrl+C or the Copy command.
 
Right click the location in the configuration tree where the object is to be pasted and, from the context menu, select Multiple paste. The Multiple Copy dialog opens. Show picture

 
Configure the multiple copies. The following rules are used if the name of the object to be copied ends in a number.
The number is removed to generate the Name prefix. 
The number plus one is used as the Start value.
The number of numeric characters digits is used as the number of Fixed digits (else set to two).
 
Click Paste to generate the objects.
 
 
Objects supported by the Multiple Paste command
 
 
Template instance
 
Topology element
 
All configuration objects
 
Mimic
 
Symbol
 
Embedded template
 
All configuration sub objects except those that can only be configured once.
 
 
Objects not supported by the Multiple Paste command
 
In general any configuration object that can only be configured once on a given parent is not supported my multiple paste. This includes the following variable sub objects.
 
 
Expression
 
Chronometer
 
Counter
 
Alarm masking
 
Discrepancy check
 
Alarm acknowledge trace
 
Alarm program associated action
 
Alarm window associated action
 
Alarm synthesis
 
Threshold


========================================
TOPIC: Multiple_Active_Server_Architecture.htm
========================================
Multiple active server architecture
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Multiple Active Server Architecture
 
Within Multiple Active Server Architecture, all servers 
 communicate simultaneously with the industrial equipment. This architecture 
 requires the use of either a multi-master industrial network or the capability 
 of multiple porting the industrial equipment. Normally this architecture 
 will use two servers, but three or more servers can be used. The entire 
 application will be identical on all servers.
 
To configure this type of architecture you must select the option Multiple 
 active servers when configuring the association.
 
Normal operation
 
Under normal operation, all servers communicate with the industrial 
 equipment. Each client connects to the first server that answers. As 
 each client connects to the first server that answers, it is possible that 
 different clients will connect to different servers. The server to which 
 a client is connected can also be controlled using a SCADA Basic program 
 by using the LAN instruction.
 
Server failure
 
Clients registered on a server that fails will automatically switch 
 to the next working server of the association.
 
Server restart
 
A server automatically restarts in the active state. Client stations 
 will only subscribe to it if they are not already subscribed to an operational 
 server.


========================================
TOPIC: Naming_rule.htm
========================================
Using the naming rule property
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Using the Naming Rule Property
 
To ensure uniqueness of the names of generated elements, by default, the name includes branch segments from all levels of the instantiation tree.
 
However, for some elements, this is not essential or desirable and the naming rule is relaxed allowing the possibility to define shorter names, or even names generated using an expression. The way in which the naming is implemented is defined by the Naming Rule property, which has the following options. Show picture

 
 
Full branch - Generate the full name including all branch segments. This is the default.
 
Short branch - Generate the name using the primary instance. That is the branch segment of the instance containing the element that will be generated.
 
Leaf only - Generate the name using only the name of the element as defined in the template.
 
 
Alternatively, you can enter an expression in the naming rule that will be used to generate the name.
 

 
If using a naming rule other than Full Branch is used, it is the user that must ensure the uniqueness of the generated name.
 
 
The Naming Rule property is available for the following configuration elements.
 
 
Cyclic
 
Expressions
 
Alarm synthesis
 
Associated labels
 
All communication objects
 
Mimics

About daylight saving and recording data
 
If you enable the option in Windows to automatically adjust the clock for daylight saving changes it means that once a year the affected PC loses a time slice (when the clocks go forward) and it gains another(when the clocks go back). In Europe for example, this is typically an hour. Whilst this does not have any detrimental effect on PcVue, it has the following effect on the historical data recording.
 
 
Clocks go forward - There will be no historic data for the time slice.
 
Clocks go back - There will be two sets of data with the same timestamps during the time slice.
 
 
The Trend Viewer in particular is not designed to deal with two sets of data with the same timestamp. As a consequence, displaying data for such a time period presents an undefined behavior. 
 

 
If you need to archive data timestamped at the device level, it is recommended to use Database archiving (HDS). In general, the native recording system (proprietary archive unit) does not provide a reliable data sequence.


========================================
TOPIC: Native_data_recdg_overview.htm
========================================
Overview of recording in proprietary archive units
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Overview of Recording in Proprietary Archive Units
 
Native data recording records in, and display from, PcVue own built-in data storage system.
 
Where is the data recorded?
 
The data is recorded in one or more archive units that 
 are created as part of configuration. There are a number 
 of different types of archive units. The choice of which type of archive 
 unit is used mainly depends on how the recorded data is to be retrieved. 
 See the topic About the different 
 types of archive unit for more information.
 
What types of data can I record?
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Type
 
 
 
Data recorded
 
 
 
Retrieval of recorded data
 
 
 

 
 
Trend data
 
 
 
The values of register and bit variables.
 
 
 
Through the HMI using a historic Trend Viewer.
Programmatically using SCADA Basic.
3rd party products, word processor, spreadsheet, database etc. - depending 
 on the chosen recording format.
 
 
 

 
 
Log data
 
 
 
The values of bit and alarm variables 
 plus the following operator actions:
 
Send command, send program, alarm acknowledge, logon and 
 logoff.
 
 
 
Through the HMI using a Log Viewer.
Programmatically using SCADA Basic.
3rd party products, word processor, spreadsheet, database etc. - depending 
 on the chosen recording format.
 
 
 

 
 
Report data 
 
 
 
The values of register, bit, alarm and text variables.
 
 
 
Through the HMI using mimics configured as reports.
Printed to a graphic printer.
 
 
 

 
 
VCR data
 
 
 
The values of register, bit, alarm and text variables.
 
 
 
Through the HMI using a specially configured PcVue
 station. The data is replayed in pseudo real time controlled using a VCR 
 style (Video Cassette Recorder) dialog.
 
 
 
 
Simplified schematic of native data recording


========================================
TOPIC: Navigating.htm
========================================
Navigating the program file
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Navigating the Program File
 
Going directly to a line using the line number
 
Programming errors are generally reported with a line number and when debugging it is useful to be able to go directly to that line. 
 
 
You can open a dialog in which you can enter the line number using either the keystroke Ctrl+G or by double clicking on the line number in the status bar.
 
 

 
To display line numbers in the editor, use the keystroke F11 or the Line Numbers tool on the toolbar.
 
 
Going directly to a function
 
 
You can navigate directly to a specific function using the Goto Function dialog opened using the keystroke Ctrl+Shift+F. 
 
 
The dialog contains a list of functions within the program being edited and clicking on the function name causes the editor to scroll directly to that function.
 
Searching and replacing
 
The editor supports standard Windows search and replace functions activated either using the keystrokes Ctrl+F and Ctrl+H or the toolbar. The search string combo box remembers up to 20 previous search strings.


========================================
TOPIC: Network_in_the_ae.htm
========================================
Managing networking in the Application Explorer
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Managing Networking in the Application Explorer
 

 
 
 
A project's networking configuration is displayed under the Communication.Networking node. It includes Stations, Nodes, Lists and Associations. The Application Explorer is used for network configuration and diagnostics. It displays run-time status information and offers commands for the configured network items which can be useful for troubleshooting.
 

 
State information is "as-seen" by the local station, that is the one on which the Application explorer is running:
 
 

 
All information about the local station are retrieved locally, independently of any connection to other stations.
 
 

 
All information about remote stations are retrieved through the Networking exchanges, and may or may not be available depending on the connection state with the selected remote station.
 
 
 
In particular, state information is for the connection between the local station, that is the one on which the Application explorer is running, and the selected station or node.
 
The maximum number of stations in a single networked application is 500.
 
 
Configuration tasks when the Networking folder is selected
 
 
 
 
 
 

 Icon
 Task
 Description
 
 

 
 
 
 Settings
 Display the general Network Settings dialog.
 
 

 
 
 
 Remove all configuration
 Remove all network configuration. 
 
 

 
 
 
 Networking wizard
 Run the networking wizard.
 
 
 
 
Configuration tasks and run-time status information when the Stations folder is selected
 
 
 
 
 
 

 Icon
 Task
 Description
 
 

 
 
 
 Add a station
 Add a network station and the first connection node.
 
 

 
 
 
 Paste
 Paste a station. Only available after a cut or copy.
 
 
 
 
For each configured station, the following run-time information is displayed:
 
 
State - The state of the connection between the selected station and the local station. See the table below for more information.
 
License number - The Id of the license used on the selected station, or 'Trial' if the station is running in trial mode.
 
License role - The role of the station with regards to licensing. For example, the station can have the role of handling a local license, be a license owner, a license renter...
 
Project version - When using Central Project Management, the version of the project.
 
Library version - When using Central Project Management, the version of the library.
 
Product version - The product software version.

 Add a connection node to a station.
 
 

 
 
 
 Open and enable connections
 If the local station has a link configured with a node of the selected station, it connects to that station. Equivalent to the SCADA Basic instruction LAN mode START_CONNECTIONS.
 
 

 
 
 
 Close and disable connections
 If the local station has a link configured with a node of the selected station, it disconnects from that station. Equivalent to the SCADA Basic instruction LAN mode STOP_CONNECTIONS.
 
 

 
 
 
 Ping
 Opens a test dialog from where the path to the station can be checked using ping. Only available if the selected station has a single node configured.
 
 

 
 
 
 Remove
 Remove the selected station.
 
 

 
 
 
 Properties
 Display the Station Properties dialog.
 
 

 
 
 
 Copy
 Copy the selected station.
 
 

 
 
 
 Cut
 Cut the selected station.
 
 

 
 
 
 Paste
 Paste a node. Only available after a cut or copy.

Client connection state - The state of the client connection (if any). See the table below for more information.

About the Standby Active and Standby Passive subscription modes
 
Consider the following architecture:
 
 
DAS01, DAS02 and DAS03 are Data Acquisition Servers, 
 
DAS_ASSOC01 is a single active Data Acquisition Association including DAS01, DAS02 and DAS03,
 
Client01 is a client of the association.
 
 
If DAS01, DAS02, DAS03 are running, and the Availability Rates within the association DAS_ASSOC01 are DAS01 = 100, DAS02 = 80, DAS03 = 60, then, in the Application Explorer running on the client station the following subscription modes are seen.
 
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS01 = Main
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS02 = Standby active
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS03 = Standby passive
 
 
If DAS01, DAS02, DAS03 are running, and the Availability Rates within the association DAS_ASSOC01 are DAS01 = 70, DAS02 = 80, DAS03 = 60, then, in the Application Explorer running on the client station the following subscription modes are seen.
 
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS01 = Standby active
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS02 = Main
 
The subscription mode with the station DAS03 = Standby passive

TOPIC: Network_Mode_of_SVBATCH.htm
========================================
Network Mode of SVBATCH
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Network Mode of SVBATCH
 
 

Applies to
 
Description
 
It is possible to define lists of stations and to carry
 out the following operations on this list:
 
 
SELECT to define
 a set of log entries.
 
UPDATE to update
 a recording.
 
The search criteria
 for UPDATE mode identify the batch and the start date. If
 a recording satisfies these criteria on different stations, it will be
 updated.
 
DELETE works in there
 same way as UPDATE mode.
 
CANCEL enables you
 to cancel a request for a selection (by SELECT or ARCHIVELIST). With
 this request, it is not necessary to carry out a GETNEXTBUFFER operation.
 
ARCHIVE.
 
BATCHLIST.
 
ARCHIVELIST.
 
GETNEXTBUFFER enables
 you to continue recording a selection when the SCADA BASIC buffer is too
 small to contain all the data.
 
 
The CREATE mode operates locally on the station.
 
Structure
 
All the requests are event-driven. The result of the
 request is obtained on transition to 1 of the configured event bit (in
 NETWORKBROADCAST).
 
The status buffer enables retrieval of the mode of the
 request in progress and to process it.
 
Once the process is completed, the NEXTCOMMAND mode enables
 you to reactivate the execution of requests.
 

 
The processing of all stored requests is held up until
 the NEXTCOMMAND mode is run.


========================================
TOPIC: New.htm
========================================
How to add and configure a data export
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Add and Configure a Data Export
 
 
Open the Application Explorer and expand the configuration tree to select the Data Analysis.
 
Select the Data Exports folder and, from the task list, select Add export. The Export configuration dialog opens. 
 
 Show picture

 
Configure the export.
Enter the Name for the export. The name is the unique identifier of the export within PcVue and can also be used in the expression used to generate the workbook or folder name.
Enter the optional Description.
Select or enter the name of the optional Status variable. The status variable provides an indication of the current status of an export. See the topic Export status codes.
Select the export File Type from either Excel or Csv.
 
If you chose Excel:
Select the Excel tab.

 Show picture

Enter the Workbook name. The name can either be a string, an expression (see the topic Using expressions in the folder, workbook or template names) or a variable (see below).
The file extension is .xlsx by default. You can change this using the Workbook Extension field. 
If you are using a workbook template enter its name in the Workbook Template field. The name can either be a string, an expression (see the topic Using Expressions in the Folder, Workbook, Template or Sheet Names) or a variable (see below). 
 
If you chose Csv: 
Select the Csv tab.
 
 
 Show picture

Enter the Folder name. The name can either be a string, an expression (see the topic Using expressions in the folder, workbook or template names) or a variable (see below). The folder is relative to the Default exports directory and can be used as a container for all Csv files that will be created from a given data export.
The character used to separate fields in the output file is a comma by default. This can be changed using the Delimiter field.
The file extension is .csv by default. You can change this using the File Extension field.
 
Click Apply to create the export configuration or OK to create the export configuration and close the dialog if you do not intend to change any of the advanced properties.
 
Select the advanced properties. See the topic Data export advanced properties.
 
Add trend and/or log pages. See the topics How to add a trend page and How to add a log page.
 
 

 
The Workbook and Workbook Template support the following file extensions, xlsx (workbook), xlsm (macro-enabled workbook), xltx (template), xltm (macro-enabled template).
 
 
Using a variable for the folder, workbook or template name
 
You can use a text variable to provide the folder, workbook or template name. The variable's name, preceded by an @ character, is entered in the corresponding field. The variable's value can provide either a relative or an absolute path and can include an expression. 
 
 
 
 
 

 Examples of absolute path
 Examples of relative path
 
 

 C:\Directory\MyExport.xlsx
 MyExportFolder\MyExport.xlsx
 
 

 &#160;
 =Export.Name+"\\MyTemplate.xltx"


========================================
TOPIC: Node_advanced_tab.htm
========================================
The node Advanced tab
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The Node Advanced Tab
 
The Node Advanced tab contains properties that, for most 
 applications, will not need to be changed from the default. Show picture

 
 
LanA number - The network LanA number. Only required for a NetBios network.
 
Enable watchdog - The watchdog period is the interval in seconds at which 
 a 'keep alive' message is sent from the client to the server. The purpose of this message is to check if the server is 
 still available on the network (some network drivers do not provide this 
 information directly). If you do not want to use the watchdog (for example 
 if you are using an RAS network connection) it can be disabled.
 
Reconnect period - This is the period at which a client will attempt to 
 re-establish connection with a disconnected server. The default value can be reduced where re-establishing a broken connection 
 is a high priority.
 
Send timeout - When a message is sent from one station to another the 
 sending station expects an acknowledge from the recipient. If acknowledgment 
 is not received within the send timeout period, the connection is assumed 
 to have failed.
 
Connection timeout - On startup the client attempts to connect to its servers. 
 If there is no reply after the call timeout period, the connection is assumed 
 to have failed.
 
Buffer size and Wait time - A message is sent between stations when either the number 
 of bytes exceeds the message size, or the message is older than the wait 
 time. The default values are 30 KB and 100 ms. For example, a single value change generates about 100 
 bytes of data. If there were only one value change in a 100 ms period, it 
 would be transmitted at the end of that period. If there were many value 
 changes in a sub 100 ms period, the data would be sent as soon as the buffer 
 reached 30 KB.


========================================
TOPIC: Numeric_formatting_characters.htm
========================================
Numeric formatting characters
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Numeric Formatting Characters
 
 
I - Used for input 
 formatting only.
 
O - Used for output 
 formatting only.
 
I/O - Used for both 
 input and output formatting.
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
Character
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#
 
 
 
Digit placeholder. Display a significant digit or nothing. If the 
 expression has a digit in the position where the # appears in the format 
 string, display it; otherwise, display nothing in that position.
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
0
 
 
 
Digit placeholder. Display a significant digit or a zero. If the expression 
 has a digit in the position where the 0 appears in the format string, 
 display it; otherwise, display a zero in that position. If the number 
 has fewer digits than there are zeros (on either side of the decimal) 
 in the format expression, display leading or trailing zeros.
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#e or #E
 
 
 
Exponential format in either lower or upper case. You 
 can use placeholders either side to specify the format of the mantissa 
 and exponent. Example 0.00#e00 would display 45.6789 as 4.57e+01.
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#b
 
 
 
Binary format, bit base. You can use placeholders specify 
 the number of bits. Example 000000#b would display 45 as 101101. 
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#B
 
 
 
Binary format, byte base. You can use placeholders to 
 specify the minimum number of bytes. Example 0#b would display 45 as 00101101.
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#bb or #BB
 
 
 
Binary format. The number of digits displayed depends 
 on the number of bytes of the source variable. Example #bb would display 
 45 from a 2 byte unsigned integer variable as 0000000000101101
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#x or #X
 
 
 
Hexadecimal format. You can use placeholders to specify 
 the number of characters. Example 0000#X would display 45.6789 as 002D
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
#x or #XX
 
 
 
Hexadecimal format. The number of characters displayed 
 depends on the number of bytes of the source variable. Example #xx would 
 display 45 from a 4 byte unsigned integer variable as 0000002d
 
 
 

 
 
I/O
 
 
 
.
 
 
 
Decimal placeholder. The decimal placeholder determines 
 how many digits are displayed to the left and right of the decimal separator.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
%
 
 
 
Percentage placeholder. The expression is multiplied by 
 100. The percent character % is inserted in the position where it appears 
 in the format string.
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
~
 
 
 
Display the absolute value (unsigned).
 
 
 

 
 
O
 
 
 
,
 
 
 
Thousand separator. The thousand separator separates thousands 
 from hundreds within a number that has four or more places to the left 
 of the decimal separator.
 
 
 
 
Important notes about numeric formatting
 
If a decimal input format is chosen (for example #.###), the 
 User will not be able to enter a value in exponent format (for example 
 1.5e2). The maximum number of digits that can be entered depends on the 
 number of placeholders in the format.
 
If an exponent input format is chosen, the user can enter the 
 value as a normal number or as an exponent (for example 1.5e2 or 150). 
 The maximum number of digits that can be entered depends on the number 
 of placeholders in the format. When no decimal point placeholder is defined, 
 a default format of 6 digits is used.
 
For binary, byte and hexadecimal formats, the maximum number 
 of digits that the user can enter depends on the variable type. For example, 
 a two byte unsigned integer would allow a maximum of four hexadecimal 
 digits.

TOPIC: Numeric_Value.htm
========================================
Displaying a register as a numeric value
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Displaying a Register as a Numeric Value
 
There are two animations that display a register as a numeric value called Register and Register & Bits. The animation 
 Register & Bits is provided for compatibility with earlier versions of the software. Both animations use a text field as the support. The text field provides the font, color and the style (inverse, button etc.) properties.
 
Selecting the display format
 
The display format is selected using the format field 
 in the Display Register tab.
 
 
 
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Selects either the auto or the custom formats.
 
 
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Allows the format to be supplied by a variable in the 
 server.
 
 
 
 
Compatibility note - Register & Bits
 
The animation Value - Register & Bits, which allows 
 you to display a register and change its color using a combination of 
 up to 4 bits (2 for the text color & 2 for the background color), 
 is provided for compatibility with earlier versions of the software. The 
 functionality it provides is similar, but not identical, to using the 
 Text - Register plus Color � Four Bits animations on the same drawing.

TOPIC: Object_Properties_dialog.htm
========================================
OLE object properties dialog
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The OLE Object Properties dialog
 
The OLE Object Properties dialog is displayed by clicking 
 on the object with the right mouse button and then selecting the Properties 
 command from the displayed context menu. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
The appearance of the dialog may vary depending on the 
 type of object. All objects will have the General and View tabs. Linked 
 objects will have the additional Link tab.
 
 
The General tab displays 
 the type of object, its size and location. For a linked object, the location 
 will be that of the original file. For an embedded object, the location 
 will be the file of the host window.
 
The View tab allows 
 you to select the display of the object as editable information or as 
 an icon. Displaying the object as an icon has little use in the HMI, as 
 you cannot double click on it to reveal the information at run time.
 
 
The Link Tab
 
The Link tab is only displayed for linked objects. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Command Buttons
 
 
Change source - Displays 
 a standard Windows file selection dialog to enable you to choose a new source 
 for the object.
 
Open Source - Opens 
 the source file for editing using the original application.
 
Update now - Updates 
 the display of the object in the HMI�s window with any changes that have 
 been made.
 
Break link - Breaks 
 the link between the display in the HMI�s window and the source file. 
 The display of the object within the window retains its appearance but the object
 becomes a non-editable Windows Metafile.

TOPIC: ofdlgsecuritydialogbox.htm
========================================
Security Dialog Box
 
	

Security Dialog Box

	

	

	

	

	

Use this dialog box to determine the level of security used when opening documents, or to remove a certificate from the list of trusted sources.

Security Level Tab

The settings on this tab indicate the level of security used when opening documents or loading add-ins.

High

You can run code only in VBA projects that have been digitally signed and that are on your list of trusted sources (described below). If the certificate for a signed project is already on your list of trusted sources, it is automatically enabled and no warning is displayed. If the certificate for a signed project is not currently on your list of trusted sources, a warning is displayed and you can choose whether to enable or disable VBA code. If you choose to enable VBA code, you can choose to add the developer to the list of trusted sources. Before trusting a source, you should confirm that the source is responsible and uses a virus scanner before signing macros. Unsigned VBA projects are automatically disabled, and no warning is displayed. You cannot enable unsigned VBA projects at this security level.

Medium

A warning is displayed whenever a VBA project from a source that is not on your list of trusted sources is loaded. You can choose whether to enable or disable both digitally signed and unsigned VBA projects. If the project might contain a virus, you should choose to disable the add-in. If the project has been signed, you can choose to add the developer to the list of trusted sources. Before trusting a source, you should confirm that the source is responsible and uses a virus scanner before signing macros.

Low

If you are sure that all the VBA projects you load are safe, you can select this option�it turns off all virus protection. At this security level, VBA projects are always enabled.

Trusted Sources Tab

This tab lists the currently trusted certificates that can be used by developers to sign documents and add-ins. When you open a digitally signed document, the digital signature appears on your computer as a certificate. The certificate names the VBA project�s source, plus additional information about the identity and integrity of that source. A digital signature does not necessarily guarantee the safety of a project, and you must decide whether you trust a project that has been digitally signed. If you know you can always trust macros from a particular source, you can add that macro developer to the list of trusted sources when you open the project.

Remove Button

If you added a certificate to your list of trusted sources when you first opened VBA project signed with that certificate, and later choose not to trust that source, you can use the Remove button to remove the certificate from your list of trusted sources. The next time a project signed with that certificate is opened, the virus protection behavior corresponding to the setting on the Security Level tab will occur.


========================================
TOPIC: One_for_many_architecture.htm
========================================
One for many architecture
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
One for Many Architecture
 
One for Many architecture uses a single server station to provide redundant operation for two or more associations. For example using three stations, Station1, Station2 and Station3.
 
 
Station3 is included in an association with Station1 to provide redundant operation for Station1.
 
Station3 is ALSO included in an association with Station2 to provide redundant operation for Station2.

 
 
 This architecture is a way to minimize the number of stations to deploy and maintain, while ensuring a certain level of redundancy. 
 
Instead of the entire variables tree in Station3 being active (producing the value of variables) or passive (consuming the value of variables) some variables can be passive and others can be active depending on which association produces them.
 
For an example of this type of architecture and more information, see the topic 1 for many redundant servers and 2 clients in the Architecture Examples book.


========================================
TOPIC: OPC_Example.htm
========================================
OPC example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
OPC Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This program illustrates the use of the OPC instruction.
 
Example prerequisites
 
 

 
An OPC Server configured with the name Server01
 
 

 
An OPC Group configured with the name Group01 under the server Server01.
 
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
DIM sServerAlias AS STR, sGroupName AS STR, sServerAlias.GroupName As Str;
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Intitialize program wide variables
 
 
 

 
SUB Main ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sServerAlias = "Server01";
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sGroupName = "Group01";
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sServerAlias.GroupName = ADDSTRING(sServerAlias, ".", sGroupName);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;PRINT("ServerAlias.GroupName is ",sServerAlias.GroupName);
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Force a read of an OPC group
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_OPC_READ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("READ", sServerAlias.GroupName, "DEVICE" );
 
 
 

 
End Sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Start an OPC Group
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_OPC_STARTGROUP()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("STARTGROUP", sServerAlias.GroupName);
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Stop an OPC Group
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_OPC_STOPGROUP()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("STOPGROUP", sServerAlias.GroupName);
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Check OPC group status
 
 
 

 
SUB CHECKSTATUS()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sVarName AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM iValue As INTEGER;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Construct status var name and use deferred addressing to get value
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sVarName = ADDSTRING("System.OPC.", sServerAlias,".",sGroupName, ".On");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;iValue = ?sVarName;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;IF(iValue == 1) Then
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("OPC Group ", sServerAlias, ".",sGroupName, " is running");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;ELSE
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("OPC Group ", sServerAlias, ".",sGroupName, " is stopped");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;END IF
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Temporarily set specific trace bits. The state of other trace bits is unaffected. The effect is immediate.
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_SETTRACE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sHexValue AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sHexValue = "00300000"; 'Hex equivalent of trace 21 and trace 22 set.
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("SETTRACE", sServerAlias.GroupName, sHexValue);'
 
 
 

 
End Sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Temporarily reset specific trace bits. The state of other trace bits is unaffected. The effect is immediate.
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_RESETTRACE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sHexValue AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sHexValue = "00300000"; 'Hex equivalent of trace 21 and trace 22 set.
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("RESETTRACE", sServerAlias.GroupName, sHexValue);'
 
 
 

 
End Sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Temporarily change (overwrite) all the trace bits. The effect is immediate.
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_PUTMASKTRACE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sHexValue AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sHexValue = "00300000"; 'Hex equivalent of trace 21 and trace 22 set.
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("PUTMASKTRACE", sServerAlias.GroupName, sHexValue);
 
 
 

 
End Sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Permanently change (overwrite) all the trace bits. Taken into account after restart.
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_SAVEMASKTRACE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sHexValue AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sHexValue = "00300000"; 'Hex equivalent of trace 21 and trace 22 set.
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("SAVEMASKTRACE", sServerAlias.GroupName, sHexValue);
 
 
 

 
End Sub
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Permanently reset (overwrite) all the trace bits. Taken into account after restart.
 
 
 

 
Sub Exec_RESETSAVEMASKTRACE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM sHexValue AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sHexValue = "00000000";
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;OPC("SAVEMASKTRACE", sServerAlias.GroupName, sHexValue);
 
 
 

 
End Sub

TOPIC: OPTIONLIST_Example.htm
========================================
OPTIONLIST example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
OPTIONLIST Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example contains a set of functions to support a pair of Option-button list form controls using the OPTIONLIST instruction.
 
SUB Main()
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'declare variables:
 
SUB optSel()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM soptText As Str;
 
DIM soptText1 As Str;
 
DIM soptData As Str;
 
DIM soptData1 As Str;
 
DIM lRet2 As Long;
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve content of selected item in 1st control:
 
lRet = OPTIONLIST ( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","opt" );
 
OPTIONLIST ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet);
 
soptText = OPTIONLIST ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
soptText1 = OPTIONLIST ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet);
 
soptData = OPTIONLIST ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
soptData1 = OPTIONLIST ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet);
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
SET ( "optText" , soptText);
 
SET("optText1", soptText1);
 
SET("optData", soptData);
 
SET("optData1" , soptData1);
 
SET("optCount" , OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt") );
 
SET("optCount1" , OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
&#160;
 
'check the latest change
 
' 1 Selection has changed; 2 Option has changed
 
PRINT ( OPTIONLIST ( "GETEVENTTYPE", "ctrl","","opt") );
 
If ( OPTIONLIST ( "GETEVENTTYPE", "ctrl","","opt") == 2 )
 
Then
 
&#160;
 
lRet2 = OPTIONLIST ( "GETSTATE", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
OPTIONLIST ( "SETSTATE", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet, lRet2);
 
End If
 
&#160;
 
'concatenate and load contents into a form control of each kind:
 
COMBOBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","cbx2", ADDSTRING("cbx",soptData));
 
LISTBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","lbx2", ADDSTRING("lbx",soptData));
 
CHECKLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","chk2", ADDSTRING("chk",soptData));
 
OPTIONLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","opt2", ADDSTRING("opt",soptData));
 
TREEVIEW ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","tvw2", ADDSTRING("tvw",soptData));
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve content of selected item in 2nd control:
 
SUB optSel1()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM soptText As Str;
 
DIM soptText1 As Str;
 
DIM soptData As Str;
 
DIM soptData1 As Str;
 
lRet = OPTIONLIST ( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","opt1" );
 
&#160;
 
OPTIONLIST ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
soptText = OPTIONLIST ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
soptText1 = OPTIONLIST ( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet);
 
soptData = OPTIONLIST ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","opt", lRet);
 
soptData1 = OPTIONLIST ( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","opt1", lRet);
 
lRet = OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt");
 
lRet = OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt1");
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
SET ( "optText" , soptText);
 
SET("optText1", soptText1);
 
SET("optData", soptData);
 
SET("optData1" , soptData1);
 
SET("optCount" , OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt") );
 
SET("optCount1" , OPTIONLIST ( "COUNT", "ctrl","","opt1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'select 2nd item:
 
SUB setSelopt()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
OPTIONLIST ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","opt", 2);
 
END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: Ordering_models.htm
========================================
Ordering 3D models
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Ordering 3D Models
 
When you use the ordering tools, models are moved along
 the 3D world axis that is perpendicular to the window. That is, the axis
 that is parallel to the direction in which the camera is aimed. For example,
 when using a Top view, if you select one model and apply Move To Front,
 it will move to a position above the rest.
 
The sides of a model
 
Because they are displayed in a window using the camera
 in an orthographic mode, the front and rear sides of a 3D model are defined
 as follows:
 
 
 
 

 
 
Side
 
 
 
 
Position 
 
 
 

 
 
Front side
 
 
 
Nearest to the camera.
 
 
 

 
 
Rear side
 
 
 
Farthest from the camera.
 
 
 
 
The model's sides used for ordering are those of a virtual
 rectangle (which is not displayed) just big enough to encompass the 2D
 projection of the model's bounding box onto the screen.
 
It can be difficult to distinguish the front side from
 the back side of a model. Hence, it is easier to visualize a scene using
 a second window (onto the same mimic) with a different orthographic camera
 mode. For example, here are two windows of the same mimic, using the Top
 View and Front View orthographic camera modes respectively: Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
While you use the ordering tools in the first window
 (Mimic1:1), the front and back sides of a model can be visualized in
 the second window (Mimic1:2). In the first window (Mimic1:1), the 3D world
 Y axis is the one perpendicular to the window. 
 
The ordering tools
 
The four ordering tools are as follows.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Tool
 
 
 
 
Icon
 
 
 
 
Effect of clicking
 
 
 
 

 
 
Move To Front
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Move the selected model in front of
 all other models. 
 
 
 

 
 
Move To Back
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Move the selected model behind all other
 models. 
 
 
 

 
 
Move Forward
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Move the selected model in front of the
 model immediately in front of it. 
 
If several models
 are selected, the tool moves the other models in
 front of the reference model.
 
 
 

 
 
Move Backward
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Move the selected model behind the model
 immediately behind it. 
 
If several models
 are selected, the tool moves the other models behind the reference model.
 
 
 
 
To order models using these tools:
 
 
Holding down the
 Shift key, select the models to be ordered. A green bounding box is shown
 around each one. The last one you select is the reference model - its
 Gizmo is visible.
 
Click on the button
 for the kind of ordering you require. The models are then arranged into
 that order.
 
 
When a model is moved in front of another model, it is
 moved closer to the camera. Conversely when a model moved behind another
 model, it is moved farther from the camera.
 

 
'Move To Front' and 'Move To Back' take all models into
 account whether selected or not. 
 
 
Example
 
The following example shows how you can order models
 in a window (Mimic1:1) using the Top View orthographic camera mode, while
 visualizing the ordering effect in a second window (Mimic1:2) using a
 Front View orthographic camera mode. In each case, several
 models may be selected and moved at the same time: Show picture.


========================================
TOPIC: Other_actions.htm
========================================
Action parameters reference
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Action Parameters Reference
 
This topic explains the parameters specific to each type of action. 
 
Send command
 
Allows the user to set a bit, register or text variable to a value. For text or register variables, a specific value is entered in the configuration. For bit variables, the bit is toggled. 
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the configured variable name. If the variable name starts with an @, any context branch is ignored and the specific variable is used in all zones. The left panel displays a hierarchy of zones. Selecting a zone shows its context branch and any variables matching the filters.
In the picture below, the variable name is CMD_LIGHT, the context for the zone is BMS.Floor00.Room00, and hence, the variable when the user is in that zone is BMS.Floor00.Room00.CMD_LIGHT.
 
 
If the variable is a bit, you can select Send to 1, Send to 0, or Reverse. The send mode can be Direct, Confirmation or Label (display the bit's associated label).
 
If the variable is a register, you can configure a specific value or an increment. The send mode can be Direct or Confirmation.
 
If the variable is a text, you can only configure a specific value.
 
 
Only commandable variables are displayed. If multiple variables match the filter, the first one available will be set. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Variable details
 
Displays detailed attributes of a variable to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and configured variable name. If the variable name starts with an @, any context branch is ignored and the specific variable is used in all zones. The left panel displays a hierarchy of zones. Selecting a zone shows its context branch and any variables matching the filters.
In the picture below, the variable name is OCCUPANCY and the context branch for the zone is BMS.Floor00.Room00, and hence, the variable when the user is in that zone is BMS.Floor00.Room00.OCCUPANCY. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
List of variables
 
Displays a list of variables and their values to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the local branch. If the local branch starts with an @, any zone context branch is ignored. The left panel displays a hierarchy of zones. Selecting a zone shows its context branch and any variables matching the filter. The action will display a set of all matching variables. The number of variables that can be displayed is limited to 100. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
In addition to the zone context and action branches, a filter using an Sql expression can be entered. For example Domain = "MyDomain". See the topic Using an Sql Expression to Filter the List of Variables in the Grid Control book. The Sql expression and branch are combined using a logical AND.
 
Trends
 
 Displays a trend view of up to 16 pre-selected variables to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and configured variable names. If a variable name starts with an @, any context branch is ignored and the specific variable is used in all zones. For each trace, you can select color, thickness, line style, marker and type in a similar manner to the Trend Viewer in the desktop application. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
List of alarms
 
Displays a list of alarms to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the local branch. If the local branch starts with an @, any zone context branch is ignored. The alarms that are displayed can be filtered in the same manner as the Alarm Viewer in the desktop application, using Domain, Nature, Alarm Level and Event Type. The action will display all alarms matching the filter. The number of alarms that can be displayed is limited to 100. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
List of logged events
 
 Displays a list of events from a selected Log List to the user.
 
There is no context for this action. The events that are displayed are from the selected Log List and can be filtered, in the same manner as the Log Viewer in the desktop application, using Domain, Nature, Alarm Level and Event Type. The number of events that can be displayed is limited to 100.Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Symbol
 
 Displays a selected symbol to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the local branch. If the local branch starts with an @, any zone context branch is ignored. Any variables displayed by the symbol take into account the action context unless the name of the variable within the symbol itself is directly referenced (preceded by an @) in which case the specific variable is resolved irrespective of the context. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Mimic and symbol rendering in mobile apps is similar to that of WebVue except for the following restrictions.
 
 
Mimics are displayed full size and cannot be scaled to fit the device screen. However, you can scroll a mimic using touch and drag.
 
The Link-open animations are not executed, other than to open a single pop-up mimic.
 
The Link-close animations are not available to the user.
 
 

 
For detailed information on the available mimic functionality, see the book Configuring a project for WebVue.
 
 
Mimic
 
 Displays a selected mimic to the user.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the local branch. If the local branch starts with an @, any zone context branch is ignored. Any variables displayed by the mimic take into account the action context unless the name of the variable within the mimic itself is directly referenced (preceded by an @) in which case the specific variable is resolved irrespective of the context. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Mimic and symbol rendering in mobile apps is similar to that of WebVue except for the following restrictions.
 
 
Mimics are displayed full size and cannot be scaled to fit the device screen. However, you can scroll a mimic using touch and drag.
 
The Link-open animations are not executed, other than to open a single pop-up mimic.
 
The Link-close animations are not available to the user.
 
 

 
For detailed information on the available mimic functionality, see the book Configuring a project for WebVue.
 
 
Execute program
 
Allow the user to execute a selected SCADA Basic script on the server.
 
The context used in the action is a combination of the zone context branch and the local branch. If the local branch starts with an @, any zone context branch is ignored. Any variables referenced in the script take into account the action context unless the name of the variable within the script itself is directly referenced (preceded by an @) in which case the specific variable is used irrespective of the context. A program function and arguments can be specified in a similar manner to the Send program animation in the desktop application. If a function is not specified, the Main function will be executed. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 

 
The program does not have to be loaded with the given branch for such an action to be effective. The script can be generic, and you can retrieve the execution context by using the verb GETARG mode BRANCH.
 
 
Open Url
 
Allow the user to browse to a specific Url in an embedded web browser. There is no context for this action. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Open chat
 
Allow the user to open an existing chat, or create a new one. Show picture
 
Choose from Open chat if exists or Open chat if exists, create a new chat otherwise to configure the main behavior. If create a new chat is allowed then the chat properties, Chat description, Chat color, Storage time of messages and Chat users must be configured in a similar manner to the Messaging Control in mimics.


========================================
TOPIC: Other_log_files.htm
========================================
Other log files
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Other log files
 
MacroTrace.log file
 
The MacroTrace.log file contains a record of PcVue main events. New events are appended to the file providing a complete summary of operations. The following events are recorded.
 
 
Start and stop of PcVue&#160;
 
Load, start, stop and unload of each manager
 
Connection and disconnection of a networked stations
 
Start and stop of communication for data acquisition
 
Manager watchdog messages
 
 

 
The maximum file size is configured by the Max size for MacroTrace log file setting in the Audit diagnostics settings. In the event that the max size is reached, a backup MacroTrace.bak.log is created and a new empty file started.
 
HDS.log file
 
The HDS.log file contains a record of events produced by the Historical Data Server. Each time the HDS is started the previous HDS.log is renamed to HDS1.log. If HDS1.log already exists, it is renamed to HDS2.log etc. The number of rotating file is nine.
 
Writing to the file takes place on each new event and there is no limit on the size of the file.
 
OPCsrv.log file
 
The OPCsrv.log file contains a record of events, produced by the PcVue main program, dedicated to OPC server activities. Each time PcVue is started, the previous OPCsvr.log is renamed to OPCsvr1.log. If OPCsvr1.log already exists, it is renamed to OPCsvr2.log etc. The number of rotating file is nine.
 
Writing to the file takes place on each new event and there is no limit on the size of the file.
 
SV32.Exceptions.log file
 
The SV32.Exceptions.log file is generated in the unlikely event of a fatal error of the PcVue main program. It contains important diagnostic information when an exception occurs or a manager watchdog is triggered. The information it contains can only be interpreted by
your technical support contact and you may be asked to provide such files when reporting a problem.
 
This file is not renamed on the start of PcVue unlike Trace.log. It will contain information about all exceptions with the most recent at the end of the file.
The maximum size is 10 Mb.
If this maximum size is reached, the file is saved as SV32.Exception.bak.log and a new SV32.Exception.log is created.
