TEXTBOX example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
TEXTBOX Example
 
 

Applies to
 
Example of the use of the TEXTBOX instruction.
 
Sub Search()
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;Dim iFind As Integer;
 
&#160;
 
'-------- Search for first occurrence of VCX
 
 &#160;&#160;iFind = TextBox("INDEXOF", "SearchEngine", "", "TextBox1", "VCX");
 
&#160;
 
'-------- If found then select text to highlight it.
 
&#160; If (iFind >= 0) Then 
 
 &#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;TextBox("SELECTTEXT", "SearchEngine", "", "TextBox1", iFind, 3);
 
&#160;&#160;End if
 
&#160;
 
End Sub()


========================================
TOPIC: TEXTVAR_Example.htm
========================================
TEXTVAR example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
TEXTVAR Examples
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example 1
 
This example exercises several modes of the TEXTVAR command.
 
The program uses short delays, so there will be pauses before results are displayed.
 
It uses a file called "ReadMe.txt" stored in 
 the TP folder of your current project (created in the Main Sub).
 
It requires these variables:
 
 
TEXT01 of type TEXT,
 
TEXT02 of type TEXT,
 
TEXT03 of type TEXT.
 
 
SUB Main()
 
'prepare the file ReadMe.txt
 
PRINT("Open file result: ", FOPEN("ReadMe.txt", "w+"));
 
PRINT("Put to file result: ", FPUTS("ReadMe.txt", "ReadMe.txt file content"));
 
PRINT("Close file result: ", FCLOSE("ReadMe.txt"));
 
&#160;
 
@TEXT01 = "";
 
@TEXT02 = "";
 
@TEXT03 = "";
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
&#160;
 
TextvarBuftotext();
 
TextvarTexttobuf();
 
TextvarFiletotext();
 
TextvarTexttofile();
 
TextvarTextcompare();
 
TextvarTextcopy();
 
TextvarTextlen();
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode BUFTOTEXT or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
SUB TextvarBuftotext()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
DIM lngBuffer as long;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode BUFTOTEXT or Mode 1 (syntax 1)");
 
lngBuffer = FILETOBUF("ReadMe.txt");
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("BUFTOTEXT", "TEXT01", lngBuffer, 0, 0, 15);
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- The number of characters read is: ", intResult);
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT01: ", @TEXT01);
 
FREE_BUFFER(lngBuffer);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TEXTTOBUF or Mode 2 (syntax 1)
 
SUB TextvarTexttobuf()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
DIM strResult as str;
 
DIM lngBuffer as long;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode TEXTTOBUF or Mode 2 (syntax 1)");
 
lngBuffer = ALLOC_BUFFER(250);
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("TEXTTOBUF", "TEXT01", lngBuffer, 0, 0, 15);
 
strResult = CGET_BUFFER(lngBuffer, 0, 15, 0);
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- The number of characters copied is: ", intResult);
 
PRINT("- The string in the buffer is: ", strResult);
 
FREE_BUFFER(lngBuffer);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode FILETOTEXT or Mode 3 (syntax 2)
 
SUB TextvarFiletotext()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode FILETOTEXT or Mode 3 (syntax 2)");
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("FILETOTEXT", "TEXT02", "ReadMe.txt", 0, 0, 15);
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- The number of characters read is: ", intResult);
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TEXTTOFILE or Mode 4 (syntax 3)
 
SUB TextvarTexttofile()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode TEXTTOFILE or Mode 4 (syntax 3)");
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("TEXTTOFILE", "TEXT01", "Text01.txt", 0, 0, 15);
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- The number of characters written is: ", intResult);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TEXTCOMPARE or Mode 5 (syntax 4)
 
SUB TextvarTextcompare()
 
'Compare the first 4 characters
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode TEXTCOMPARE or Mode 5 (syntax 4)");
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("TEXTCOMPARE", "TEXT01", "TEXT02", 4);
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, " ; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
PRINT("- The comparison result is (0 means equal): ", intResult);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TEXTCOPY or Mode 6 (syntax 5)
 
SUB TextvarTextcopy()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode TEXTCOPY or Mode 6 (syntax 5)");
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, " ; TEXT03: ", @TEXT03);
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("TEXTCOPY", "TEXT03", "TEXT01");
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- Copied TEXT01 to TEXT03 with result: ", intResult);
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, " ; TEXT03: ", @TEXT03);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'Mode TEXTLEN or Mode 7 (syntax 6)
 
SUB TextvarTextlen()
 
DIM intResult as integer;
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("Mode TEXTLEN or Mode 7 (syntax 6)");
 
intResult = TEXTVAR("TEXTLEN", "TEXT01");
 
&#160;
 
PRINT("- Variable TEXT01: ", @TEXT01);
 
PRINT("- Length of the character string in TEXT01: ", intResult);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
Example 2
 
This example sets and displays text variables.
 
It assumes that a mimic contains these text variables:
 
�	TEXT01,
 
�	TEXT02.
 
The program uses short delays, so there will be pauses before results are displayed.
 
A file test.txt in project folder TP contains between 4 and 50 characters (here: "abc,123"). The program allocates a memory buffer and loads it from the text file.
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM hbuf as long;
 
&#160;
 
'prepare the file test.txt
 
PRINT("Open file result: ", FOPEN("test.txt", "w+"));
 
PRINT("Put to file result: ", FPUTS("test.txt", "abc,123"));
 
PRINT("Close file result: ", FCLOSE("test.txt"));
 
&#160;
 
'load file content into a buffer
 
hbuf = FILETOBUF("test.txt");
 
PRINT("Buffer contains: ", CGET_BUFFER(hbuf, 0, 50, 0));
 
&#160;
 
'clear the text variables
 
@TEXT01 = "";
 
@TEXT02 = "";
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
PRINT("Text variables TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, "; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
&#160;
 
'copy buffer contents to variable TEXT01
 
PRINT("BufToText all: ", TEXTVAR("BUFTOTEXT", "TEXT01", hbuf, 0, 0, 0), " chars.");
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
PRINT("Text variables TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, "; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
&#160;
 
'copy from variable TEXT01 (4 chars. from offset 3) to end of file
 
PRINT("TextToFile: ", TEXTVAR("TEXTTOFILE", "TEXT01", "test.txt", 3, 1, 4), " chars.");
 
&#160;
 
'copy 3 characters from buffer at offset 4 to variable TEXT02
 
PRINT("BufToText: ", TEXTVAR("BUFTOTEXT", "TEXT02", hbuf, 0, 4, 3), " chars.");
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
PRINT("Text variables TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, "; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
&#160;
 
'compare the first 4 characters of the variables
 
PRINT("Compare result (0 means equal): ", TEXTVAR("TEXTCOMPARE", "TEXT01", "TEXT02", 4));
 
&#160;
 
'copy 3 characters from file to start of variable TEXT02
 
PRINT("FileToText: ", TEXTVAR("FILETOTEXT", "TEXT02", "test.txt", 0, 8, 3), " chars.");
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
PRINT("Text variables TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, "; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
&#160;
 
'copy variable TEXT02 to TEXT01
 
PRINT("TextCopy result: ", TEXTVAR("TEXTCOPY", "TEXT01", "TEXT02"));
 
DELAY(0.1); 'to apply value to variable
 
PRINT("Text variables TEXT01: ", @TEXT01, "; TEXT02: ", @TEXT02);
 
PRINT("Compare result (0 means equal): ", TEXTVAR("TEXTCOMPARE", "TEXT01", "TEXT02", 4));
 
&#160;
 
'release the buffer
 
FREE_BUFFER(hbuf);
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
Results
 
The program displays these results in the Program Management window (F9, Show Results):
 
Open file result: 1
 
Put to file result: 1
 
Close file result: 1
 
Mode BUFTOTEXT or Mode 1 (syntax 1)
 
- The number of characters read is: 15
 
- Variable TEXT01: ReadMe.txt file
 
Mode TEXTTOBUF or Mode 2 (syntax 1)
 
- The number of characters copied is: 15
 
- The string in the buffer is: ReadMe.txt file
 
Mode FILETOTEXT or Mode 3 (syntax 2)
 
- The number of characters read is: 15
 
- Variable TEXT02: ReadMe.txt file
 
Mode TEXTTOFILE or Mode 4 (syntax 3)
 
- The number of characters written is: 15
 
Mode TEXTCOMPARE or Mode 5 (syntax 4)
 
- Variable TEXT01: ReadMe.txt file ; TEXT02: ReadMe.txt file
 
- The comparison result is (0 means equal): 0
 
Mode TEXTCOPY or Mode 6 (syntax 5)
 
- Variable TEXT01: ReadMe.txt file ; TEXT03:
 
- Copied TEXT01 to TEXT03 with result: 1
 
- Variable TEXT01: ReadMe.txt file ; TEXT03: ReadMe.txt file
 
Mode TEXTLEN or Mode 7 (syntax 6)
 
- Variable TEXT01: ReadMe.txt file
 
- Length of the character string in TEXT01: 15


========================================
TOPIC: The_built-in_objects.htm
========================================
The PcVue objects
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The PcVue Objects
 
The properties, methods and events of the PcVue objects allow application designers to implement any customized behavior needed for the supervision and control of the process. Some object instances are always available as part of the core functionality, for example ThisProject, whereas others are created as part of the application, for example an opened mimic.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Class
 
 
 
Represents
 
 
 
Instance in VBA
 
 
 

 
 
Application
 
 
 
The HMI application.
 
 
 
Application
 
 
 

 
 
AVI
 
 
 
An AVI object.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Bitmap
 
 
 
A bitmap object. This class supports BMP, JPG, and PNG image formats.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
fvProject
 
 
 
The currently opened project.
 
 
 
ThisProject
 
 
 

 
 
GIF
 
 
 
A GIF object. This class supports GIF image formats.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Graphics
 
 
 
A collection representing all graphic elements within a mimic. The following object classes are included. 
 
AVI
Bitmap
GIF
Group
Metafile
OLE
Poly
Shape
Symbol
Text
 
 
 
Graphics
 
 
 

 
 
Group
 
 
 
A group object. A number of drawing elements that have been grouped together (not a symbol).
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Metafile
 
 
 
A metafile object. This class supports WMF and EMF image formats.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Mimic
 
 
 
An opened mimic.
 
 
 
ThisMimic
 
 
 

 
 
Mimics
 
 
 
A collection representing all opened Mimic objects.
 
 
 
TheseMimics, Mimics
 
 
 

 
 
OLE
 
 
 
A third party OLE object. Its properties, methods, functions and events are exposed once it is inserted into a mimic. For information on the behavior of the object, see the documentation provided by the vendor.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Poly
 
 
 
A polygon/polyline object. This class includes the Line, Polyline, Polygon and B�zier curve drawing elements.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Recipe
 
 
 
A list of ingredients and variables configured as a recipe.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Shape
 
 
 
A shape object. This class includes the Rectangle, Rounded Rectangle, Ellipse, Arc and Half-arc drawing elements.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Symbol
 
 
 
A symbol object.
 
 
 
ThisSymbol
 
 
 

 
 
System
 
 
 
A set of properties that describes the system on which the software is running.
 
 
 
ThisSystem, System
 
 
 

 
 
TemplateGraphics
 
 
 
A collection representing all graphic elements within a mimic template. Identical to the Graphics collection except that it points to the graphics of a mimic template. If the mimic has no template then TemplateGraphics is redirected to Graphics.
 
 
 
TemplateGraphics
 
 
 

 
 
Text
 
 
 
A text object. This class includes the Text drawing element.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
UIAlm
 
 
 
An alarm display object.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
UILog
 
 
 
A log display object.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
UITrd
 
 
 
A trend display object.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Variable
 
 
 
A currently subscribed variable. It provides properties, methods and events for variables of all types.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Variables
 
 
 
A collection representing all currently subscribed variables.
 
 
 
TheseVariables, Variables
 
 
 

 
 
Window
 
 
 
A view of an opened mimic. A mimic can be opened in multiple windows (views) simultaneously. For example, one in design mode and the other in run mode.
 
 
 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
 
Windows
 
 
 
A collection representing all opened Window objects.
 
 
 
Windows


========================================
TOPIC: Timetable_animation_at_run_time.htm
========================================
Using the Timetable animation at run time
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Using the Timetable Animation at Run Time
 
A Timetable is based on a standard week which contains 
 a number of periods, defined by start and end times, for each of the 7 
 days. The action with which the Timetable is associated will be triggered 
 at the start and end of each period. So for example, a Timetable could 
 be used to trigger a report on Monday through to Friday, but not on Saturday 
 and Sunday.
 
The schedule defined by the Standard Week is repeated 
 indefinitely, except for any Exception Periods which have been created. 
 Exception periods override the standard weekly settings and may be used 
 to change the times at that the action is triggered over holidays or 
 shut down periods.
 
Displaying a timetable
 
When a Timetable is displayed at run-time, it allows the 
 user to view the time periods that have been configured for each day. 
 Show picture
 

 
 
 
The command buttons on the right of the Timetable may 
 be used to change which days are viewed. The command buttons marked Prev 
 and Next at the top and bottom of each column may be used to scroll 
 each column vertically if there are more than 6 periods in a day.
 
Adding and changing exception periods
 
At run-time, the periods defined by the standard week 
 may not be changed, but they may be overridden by adding and changing 
 exception periods. The ability to add or change exception periods is determined 
 by the options enabled when the control zone was created. An exception 
 period is added or changed by clicking the command button marked EXC at 
 the bottom of the column for the day concerned. If the control zone was 
 created without the ability to change exception periods these buttons 
 are not displayed.
 
Each exception allows the definition of different periods 
 for 3 dates. The first date defines the change in operation on the first 
 day of the period, the second date (which always follows the first), defines 
 the operation on all subsequent days until the third date, which defines 
 the operation on the last day of the period.
 
Exception 
 periods of less than three days may be created by leaving the periods 
 in the first and or last date empty. The Scheduler then assumes that the 
 exception period is only for those dates with period definitions.
 
Saving a timetable
 
Once a Timetable has been changed it may be saved to 
 disk by selecting the Save command button. If it is not saved, any changes 
 made will be temporary and will be lost when PcVue is shut down. 
 The right to save a Timetable is given by the configuration of the control 
 zone. 
 

 
The WebScheduler offers a web-based alternative for operators to change time intervals of standard weeks and exceptions.
 
It supports built-in timetable as well as BACnet Schedule and Calendars.

TOPIC: Tips.htm
========================================
Drawing tips
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Drawing Tips
 
Interrupting the drawing process
 

 
When using the following drawing tools you can use the Esc key at any time to return to the selector (pointer) tool.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Line
 
 
 
Rectangle
 
 
 
Rounded rectangle
 
 
 

 
 
Ellipse
 
 
 
Half ellipse
 
 
 
Quarter ellipse
 
 
 

 
 
Arc
 
 
 
Pie
 
 
 
Polygon
 
 
 

 
 
Polyline
 
 
 
Bezier shape
 
 
 
Bezier curve
 
 
 

 
 
Text
 
 
 
Color picker
 
 
 
Color Dropper
 
 
 
 
 
The current drawing process (if any) will be completed before returning to the selector tool. For example, if you were drawing a polygon the final node would appear at the position of the cursor when the Esc key was pressed. Show picture
 

 
 
 
Automatic scrolling
 
A mimic window shows horizontal and vertical scrollbars when it is zoomed in and/or when a mimic is configured to be larger than the window. If you scale or anchor a drawing element outside of the current area of the mimic, the window will track the movement so as to keep visible the object drawing handle that you are manipulating.
 
For example, as you draw a line you can move its end point toward the right-hand edge of the window area (a). Show picture 
 
The window will scroll to the right to keep it visible (b). Show picture

TOPIC: Trace_log_files.htm
========================================
Trace.log files
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Trace.log files
 
The Trace.log files contains all events produced by the PcVue main program as seen in the Event Viewer window. The maximum file size is configured by the Max size for Trace log file setting in the Audit diagnostics settings. 
 
If the Trace.log reaches the maximum size, traces are written to a new log file Trace1.log. When Trace1.log reaches the maximum size, traces are written to a new log file Trace2.log etc. The number of new files is limited to nine and so when Trace9.log is full, Trace1.log is emptied and recording continues there.
 
The file Trace.log always contains traces generated since PcVue was last started.
Upon startup, the previous Trace.log is renamed to LastSession1_Trace.log. If LastSession1_Trace.log already exists, it is renamed to LastSession2_Trace.log etc. The number of rotating sessions is nine.
 
Below is an example listing of the trace files you might find in the Log Files folder.
 
Trace.log
Trace1.log
Trace2.log
Trace3.log
Trace4.log
Trace5.log
Trace6.log
Trace7.log
Trace8.log
Trace9.log
LastSession1_Trace.log
LastSession1_Trace1.log
LastSession1_Trace2.log
LastSession1_Trace3.log
LastSession1_Trace4.log
LastSession1_Trace5.log
LastSession1_Trace6.log
LastSession1_Trace7.log
LastSession1_Trace8.log
LastSession1_Trace9.log
�.
LastSession9_Trace.log
LastSession9_Trace1.log
LastSession9_Trace2.log
LastSession9_Trace3.log
LastSession9_Trace4.log
LastSession9_Trace5.log
LastSession9_Trace6.log
LastSession9_Trace7.log
LastSession9_Trace8.log
LastSession9_Trace9.log
 
Traces are buffered and written to the files every ten seconds or if the size of buffered messages exceed 32 Kb.

TOPIC: TRACEON_TRACEOFF.htm
========================================
TRACEON/TRACEOFF
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
TRACEON/TRACEOFF
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Enable tracing of function calls.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
TRACEON; 
 
or 
 
TRACEOFF;
 
There is no return type.
 
Execution
 
Trace allows the name of the calling program, the name 
 of the called program, and the name of the called function to be known. 
 The trace appears in the results area of the program dialog box.
 
The TRACEON instruction is active for all functions of 
 the same program until the TRACEOFF instruction is encountered.
 

 
If executed in a WebVue session context this instruction is processed by the computer that hosts the web back end and the output is created on that computer.
 
 
Example
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM intResult as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
TRACEON;
 
procedure1();
 
procedure2("Parameter1");
 
intResult = procedure4();
 
PRINT("Return from procedure = ",intResult);
 
TRACEOFF;
 
END SUB
 

 

 
Any nested function calls will also be traced.

TOPIC: Traces.htm
========================================
Data export traces
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Data Export Traces
 
The Traces tab enables trace messages associated with Data Export. Enabling a trace generates a corresponding message, which is logged in the Trace Files and displayed in the Event Viewer dialog (F7) when the condition occurs. The following screenshot was taken with the default configuration. Show picture
 

 

 
 

 
 
 
Trace 1: General - Basic events. Example: Generation started
 
Trace 2: Configuration loading - Loading of the main export configuration. Example: Number of trend pages in export is 1
 
Trace 3: Expression resolution - Messages related to the resolution of any expressions used in the configuration properties. Example: Expression successfully resolved 'Export.Name'='Export01'
 
Trace 4: Historical communication - Communication with the historical server. Example: Send get trend to histo about TrendPage01.Unit01.Register01...'
 
Trace 5: Generation steps - Messages related to progress through the generation process. Example: 1 trend page found to export


========================================
TOPIC: Transformations.htm
========================================
3D Transformations
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3D Transformations
 
Geometrical transformation is expressed in terms of 3 
 kinds of motion - Translation, Rotation and Scaling.
 
Translation
 
A translation is a transformation that changes the position 
 of an object without changing its orientation or size. It is described 
 by a 3D vector (a direction in space), that can be measured along the 
 3 axes: X, Y and Z. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Rotation
 
Rotation is a transformation that changes an objects 
 orientation. Rotation can be described by a number of methods, two of 
 the most common of which are supported by the HMI.
 
 
Euler XYZ angles 
 - This method is the easiest to understand but has major limitations and 
 is not contiguous. It is described by three angle one for each of the 
 X, Y and Z axis. The axis are sometimes referred to as yaw, pitch and 
 roll. Show picture

X-axis rotation (Pitch)
Y-axis rotation (Yaw)
Z-axis rotation (Roll)

 
Rotational vectors - This 
 is the most universal and best representation, although not the easiest 
 to understand. The axis is a 3D vector and the angle is given about that 
 axis. Rotational vectors are described using X, Y and Z values (for the 
 vector) and a single angle.
 
 
Scaling
 
Scaling is a transformation that resizes (scales) the 
 object. The HMI uses 3 coefficients (or ratios) to describe the scaling 
 along the local X, Y and Z axes of the objects. Local scaling is used 
 to avoid any skewing. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
How transformation is specified
 
Transformation may be specified in two ways.
 
 
World transformation 
 - The change in an objects origin is with respect to the world space origin 
 that is common to all objects in a scene. In the case of the HMI, the 
 world space is an entire 3D mimic.
 
Local transformation 
 - The change in an objects origin is with respect to the parent objects 
 origin.
 
 
The following diagram illustrates the relationship between 
 the world coordinate system and an object's local coordinate system. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
In a parent-child hierarchy, the transformation of the 
 parent is always applied to the objects it contains. For a top-level object 
 (no parent), its world transformation is identical to its local transformation. 
 When editing a child object, you will see its local transformation information 
 (relative to its parent).
 
In terms of the HMI, a 3D model is normally a top-level 
 object and hence uses world transformation. The one exception is when 
 a model has been incorporated in a symbol in which case the symbol is 
 the top level object and the parent of the 3D model. A frame within a 
 model is a child of either the model or another frame and hence uses local 
 transformation.


========================================
TOPIC: Transforming.htm
========================================
Transforming objects in the instances tree
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Transforming Objects in the Instances Tree
 
How to transform a topology element into an instance of a template
 
A topology element can be replaced by an instance of a template. The main properties (Description, Name and Branch) of the topology element are retained.
 
 
In the right pane of the Application Architect, select the Instances tab.
 
Select a topology element and select Transform into a template instance.
 
Expand the menu of available templates and select the template to replace the topology element.
 
 
How to transform a topology element into an instance of a new template
 
A topology element can be converted into an instance of a new template. The main properties (Description, Name and Branch) of the original topology element are retained.
 
 
In the right pane of the Application Architect, select the Instances tab.
 
Select a topology element and select Transform into a template instance.
 
Expand the menu and select the New template... command. The Templates Selector dialog is displayed
 
Select the parent for the new template.
 
Enter the template name and confirm with the OK command.
 
 

 
A topology element will need to be converted to an instance of a template if the application changes so that configuration elements, graphics or parameters are required at that level.
 
 
Transforming the tree into template instance
 
It is possible to transform the top level of the instances tree, representing the project, into a template instance. This makes it possible to place objects, such as File and File Item, at the highest level.
 
Transforming a template instance into a topology level
 
A template instance can be transformed into a topology level.
 
 
In the right pane of the Application Architect, select the Instances tab.
 
Select a template instance and select Transform into a topology level.
 
 

 
The entire contents of the template instance will be lost (objects, parameters, etc.).

TOPIC: Transforming_a_Bezier_Curve_into_a_Polygon.htm
========================================
Transforming a B�zier Curve into a Polygon
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Transforming a B�zier Curve into a Polygon
 
You can convert a B�zier curve into a Polygon. This can 
 be useful when you want to use the path animation.
 
To transform B�zier curve into a Polygon:
 
 
Draw a B�zier curve 
 and adjust it to the final shape.
 
Display the B�zier 
 curve's Drawing Properties dialog and select the Aspect tab.
 
In the shape drop 
 down list box, select Polygon.
 
Click OK to confirm 
 the transformation.

TOPIC: TREEVIEW_Example.htm
========================================
TREEVIEW example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
TREEVIEW Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example contains a set of functions to support a pair of Tree-view form controls using the TREEVIEW instruction.
 
SUB Main()
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'declare variables:
 
SUB tvwSel()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM stvwText As Str;
 
DIM stvwText1 As Str;
 
DIM stvwData As Str;
 
DIM stvwData1 As Str;
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve contents of selected item in 1st control:
 
lRet = TREEVIEW ( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","tvw" );
 
TREEVIEW ( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","tvw1", lRet);
 
stvwText = TREEVIEW( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","tvw", lRet);
 
stvwText1 = TREEVIEW( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","tvw1", lRet);
 
stvwData = TREEVIEW( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","tvw", lRet);
 
stvwData1 = TREEVIEW( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","tvw1", lRet);
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
SET ( "tvwText" , stvwText);
 
SET("tvwText1", stvwText1);
 
SET("tvwData", stvwData);
 
SET("tvwData1" , stvwData1);
 
SET("tvwCount" , TREEVIEW( "COUNT", "ctrl","","tvw") );
 
SET("tvwCount1" , TREEVIEW( "COUNT", "ctrl","","tvw1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
&#160;
 
'concatenate and load contents into a form control of each kind:
 
COMBOBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","cbx2", ADDSTRING("cbx",stvwData));
 
LISTBOX ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","lbx2", ADDSTRING("lbx",stvwData));
 
CHECKLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","chk2", ADDSTRING("chk",stvwData));
 
OPTIONLIST ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","opt2", ADDSTRING("opt",stvwData));
 
TREEVIEW ( "LOAD", "ctrl","","tvw2", ADDSTRING("tvw",stvwData));
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'retrieve content of selected item in 2nd control:
 
SUB tvwSel1()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
DIM stvwText As Str;
 
DIM stvwText1 As Str;
 
DIM stvwData As Str;
 
DIM stvwData1 As Str;
 
lRet = TREEVIEW( "GETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","tvw1" );
 
TREEVIEW( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","tvw", lRet);
 
stvwText = TREEVIEW( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","tvw", lRet);
 
stvwText1 = TREEVIEW( "GETTEXT", "ctrl","","tvw1", lRet);
 
stvwData = TREEVIEW( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","tvw", lRet);
 
stvwData1 = TREEVIEW( "GETUSERDATA", "ctrl","","tvw1", lRet);
 
&#160;
 
'put its contents in a frame:
 
lRet = TREEVIEW( "COUNT", "ctrl","","tvw1");
 
SET ( "tvwText" , stvwText);
 
SET("tvwText1", stvwText1);
 
SET("tvwData", stvwData);
 
SET("tvwData1" , stvwData1);
 
SET("tvwCount" , TREEVIEW( "COUNT", "ctrl","","tvw") );
 
SET("tvwCount1" , TREEVIEW( "COUNT", "ctrl","","tvw1"));
 
SENDLIST ("BLOC");
 
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
'select 2nd item:
 
SUB setSeltvw()
 
DIM lRet As Long;
 
TREEVIEW( "SETSELECTEDINDEX", "ctrl","","tvw", 2);
 
END SUB


========================================
TOPIC: TREND_Example.htm
========================================
Trend Viewer example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
TREND Examples
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
The following example exercises a few modes of the TREND 
 command.
 
It assumes that there is a window called BigTrend with 
 a Trend Viewer identified as TRN1.
 
SUB Checkmode () ' Check current mode for trend
DIM res AS INTEGER;
res = TREND("GETTYPE", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", 1);
IF (res == 2) THEN
trend.type =REAL ();
ELSE
trend.type = HIST ();
END IF
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB REAL () ' Change trend to real time
TREND("SETTYPE", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", 0, 1);
RETURN ("RealTime");
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB HIST () ' Change trend to historic
TREND("SETTYPE", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", 0, 2);
RETURN ("Historic");
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB PERIOD2 () ' Double the trend period
DIM TrendPeriod AS DOUBLE;
TrendPeriod = TREND("GETPERIOD", "BigTrend", "", "trn1");
TREND("SETPERIOD", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", (TrendPeriod*2));
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB PERIOD1 () ' Half the trend period
DIM TrendPeriod AS DOUBLE;
TrendPeriod = TREND(7, "BigTrend", "", "trn1");
TREND("SETPERIOD", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", (TrendPeriod/2));
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB SCROLLPLUS100 () 'Scroll trend forward 100%
TREND ("SCROLLPERCENT", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", 100);
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB SCROLLMINUS100 () 'Scroll trend back 100%
TREND ("SCROLLPERCENT", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", -100);
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB SCROLLPLUS10 () 'Scroll trend forward 10%
TREND ("SCROLLPERCENT", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", 10);
END SUB
 
&#160;
 
SUB SCROLLMINUS10 () 'Scroll trend back 100%
TREND ("SCROLLPERCENT", "BigTrend", "", "trn1", -10);
END SUB
 
Example of using GETCONFIG_ASYNCH
 
' Example 1. No specific callback function is defined by the user, the default callback function onTrend_GetConfig is used:
 
Sub onTrend_GetConfig()
 
&#160;&#160;Print("callback function is called");
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub asyncGetConfig()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim ret as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;ret = Trend("GETCONFIG_ASYNC", " MimicTrend1", "", "trend1","TrendConfig1.xml");
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
' Example 2. Callback function is defined by the user in the program �p� with name of �onTrendGetXmlConf�:
 
' The onTrend_GetConfig can get the argument which is passed in caller function.
 
Sub onTrendGetXmlConf()
 
 &#160;PRINT(getArg("ARG1"));
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
' The caller function passes an argument �TrendConfig1�, the XML will be saved to the absolute path �F:\\TrendConfig2.xml�:
 
Sub asyncGetConfig()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim ret as integer;
 
&#160;&#160;ret = Trend("GETCONFIG_ASYNC", " MimicTrend1", "", "trend1","F:\\TrendConfig2.xml","p","","onTrendGetXmlConf", "TrendConfig1");
 
End Sub
 
Example of the file generated by either mode GETCONFIG or GETCONFIG_ASYNCH

TOPIC: Type_Conversion.htm
========================================
Type conversion instructions
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Data Type Conversion Instructions
 
 
 
 

 
 
Instruction
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
BIN
 
 
 
 
Return a string representing, in binary, a number passed 
 in base 10.
 
 
 

 
 
CONVERT
 
 
 
 
Convert alphanumeric string to & from Hexadecimal, 
 Octal, Binary and BCD.
 
 
 

 
 
DVAL
 
 
 
 
Return the numeric value of a character string as a DOUBLE.
 
 
 

 
 
HEX
 
 
 
 
Return a string representing, in hex, a number passed 
 in base 10.
 
 
 

 
 
IVAL
 
 
 
 
Return the numeric value of a character string as an INTEGER.
 
 
 

 
 
LVAL
 
 
 
 
Return the numeric value of a character string as a LONG.
 
 
 

 
 
OCT
 
 
 
 
Return a string representing, in octal, a number passed 
 in base 10.
 
 
 

 
 
SVAL
 
 
 
 
Return the numeric value of a character string as a SINGLE. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
TOC
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to a STR (character string).
 
 
 

 
 
TOD
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to a DOUBLE.
 
 
 

 
 
TODOUBLE
 
 
 
 
Convert a variable to a DOUBLE value.
 
 
 

 
 
TOI
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to an INTEGER by truncation.
 
 
 

 
 
TOL
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to a LONG.
 
 
 

 
 
TOLL
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to a LONGLONG.
 
 
 

 
 
TOS
 
 
 
 
Convert a number to a SINGLE.

TOPIC: Union_of_two_drawing_elements.htm
========================================
Union of two drawing elements
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Union of two drawing elements
 
The Union tool creates a polygon that 
 is the sum of the areas of two drawing elements. 
 

 
 
 
How to use the union tool
 
 
Select the first 
 drawing element by clicking with the left mouse button.
 
Select the second 
 drawing element holding the Shift button so that both drawing elements 
 are selected.
 
Select the Union 
 tool from the Arrange toolbar. A new polygon is created taking the color 
 from the second shape.
 
Click and drag the 
 polygon to its location. The original drawing elements remain unchanged.


========================================
TOPIC: Use_of_third_party_editors.htm
========================================
Use of third party editors on translation files
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Use of Third Party Editors
 
In a tool such as Microsoft Excel, the format in which the import/export files are generated 
 (character separated value) is displayed as a spreadsheet. 
 That is the most suitable tool for editing as it puts each line in a separate 
 row and each text string in a separate cell. Show picture
 

 

 
 
 
 
Hints for using Excel
 
 
When you have finished editing, 
 save the import/export file in CSV format using comma as the delimiter.
 
You can use Style.Conditional Formatting to 
 shade the rows so as to distinguish the kinds of element to which 
 they relate. Show picture. 
 

 
 
In the latter case you should save the file in both the 
 native spreadsheet format (XLS) to preserve the formatting and in CSV 
 format for the Import/Export Tool.


========================================
TOPIC: Use_of_Unicode_.htm
========================================
Important note about the use of unicode for character encoding
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Important Note About the Use of Unicode for Character 
 Encoding
 

 &#160;PcVue uses a mechanism called DBCS (double byte 
 character sets) for encoding the characters used in its strings. The 
 Import/Export Tool converts the DBCS characters to/from Unicode during 
 the import/export process, unless the Create Unicode Files tick box is 
 not ticked. If it is not ticked, single byte (ANSI) encoding is used.
 
Wherever possible Unicode should be used in order to 
 support the widest range of languages (particularly those with an Asian 
 origin).
 
When Unicode should be used
 
Unicode encoding should only be used with a third party 
 editor that supports it.
 
About converting Unicode to DBCS
 
The precise conversion process depends on the system's 
 native language and/or Regional Setting configuration. Some combinations 
 of these parameters work for particular languages but not for others. 
 Therefore, it is strongly recommended that, if after an import the character 
 rendering in PcVue produces unexpected results, you do not use 
 the Import/Export Tool for that particular language.


========================================
TOPIC: User_Choosing_a_strong_password.htm
========================================
Choosing a strong password
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Choosing a Strong Password
 
A strong password is one that is very difficult to guess. In PcVue, if you select the option Impose strong password in the User Accounts Preferences, you must then enter a strong password for all User Accounts.
 
What is a strong password?
 

 &#160;PcVue uses the following criteria to determine if a strong password has been configured.
 
 
It must contain between 8 and 16 characters.
 
It cannot contain the user name.
 
It must contain characters from at least three of the four following types of character:
Lower case alphabetic: a-z
Upper case alphabetic: A-Z
Numeric: 0-9
Special characters: ` ~ ! @ # $ % ^ * ( ) _ + - = { } | [ ] \ ? . /
you cannot use non-printable control characters.
 
 

 
If you have not selected Impose strong password then you cannot use special characters in the password.
 
If you have selected Impose strong password then you can use special characters in the password.
 
In both cases, the user name is not case sensitive.
 
 
As you enter the password its strength is indicated by a colored bar. Show picture


========================================
TOPIC: User_conf.htm
========================================
How to configure a user
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
How to Configure a User
 
 
Configuring a user
Select the Users folder in the Application Explorer configuration tree and, from the task list, select Add a user. A dialog to configure a new user will open. Show picture

Enter a name for the user. The name is used when logging on using the User Account. The name is limited to 20 characters.
Enter a description of the user. The description appears in the contents pane when the Users folder is selected. The description is optional.
Enter the password and password confirmation. For information on choosing a password, see the topic Choosing a strong password.
 
 Entering optional user details. 
 
Select the Details tab. The following properties, all of which are optional, are available.
First and last name. The first and last name are concatenated to form the read only property Display name.
Title - The User's title, for example Professor. The Title replaces the Role property, which was part of the user's configuration prior to version 9.0. For compatibility with Windows domain user groups, the Role property is now part of the Profile configuration. 
E-mail address - The e-mail address can be used when sending a message using an event.
Mobile phone number - The mobile phone number can be used when sending a message using an event.
 
Click Apply to create the user and leave the dialog open or OK to create the user and close the dialog.
 
 
Many of the properties from the Details tab are also available in System variables. The Display Name and Title are also displayed in the dialog opened by the Security - User Information animation.

Rules and behavior of cut, copy and paste
 
The following table describes the rules and behavior when using cut and paste, or copy and paste, on configuration objects in the User Accounts folder.
 
 
 
 
 

 User accounts
 Behavior
 
 

 Profile
 The pasted Profile must be given a unique name.
Any Users that were associated with the original Profile will also be associated with the new one.
 
 

 User
 The pasted User must be given a unique name. 
If a Profile was associated with the original User, it will also be associated with the new one.

TOPIC: VIDEODISPLAY_Example.htm
========================================
VIDEODISPLAY example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
VIDEODISPLAY Example
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
This program illustrates the use of the VIDEODISPLAY instruction.
 
Example prerequisites
 
 

 
A mimic containing a Video control must be open.
 
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
'-------- Program wide variables
 
 
 

 
DIM sWindow AS Str, sBranch AS STR, sIdentity AS STR, sURL AS STR;
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
SUB Main ()
 
 
 

 
'-------- Intialize program wide variables
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sWindow = "mimic5"; 'Mimic name
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sBranch = ""; ' Branch
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sIdentity = "VideoCtrl1"; 'Video control Id
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;sURL = "rtsp://[username:password@]ip_address[:rtsp_port]/server_URL[[?param1=val1[?param2=val2]�[?paramN=valN]]"; 'Video stream URL. Customize value to suite your specific setup (credentials, port...)
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Play the specific video stream
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_PLAY ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM lReturn AS LONG;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;lReturn = VIDEODISPLAY("PLAY", sWindow, sBranch, sIdentity, sURL);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;IF (lReturn == 1) THEN
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode PLAY successful");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;ELSE
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode PLAY failed");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;END IF
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Stop the specific video stream
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_STOP ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM lReturn AS LONG;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;lReturn = VIDEODISPLAY("STOP", sWindow, sBranch, sIdentity);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;IF (lReturn == 1) THEN
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode STOP successful");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;ELSE
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode STOP failed");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;END IF
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Pause video stream. Note that pause has a toggle action.
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_PAUSE ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM lReturn AS LONG;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;lReturn = VIDEODISPLAY("PAUSE", sWindow, sBranch, sIdentity);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;IF (lReturn == 1) THEN
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode PAUSE successful");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;ELSE
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode PAUSE failed");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;END IF
 
 
 

 
END SUB
 
 
 

 
&#160;
 
 
 

 
'-------- Take a snapshot of the video stream
 
 
 

 
SUB Exec_SNAPSHOT ()
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;DIM lReturn AS LONG;
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;lReturn = VIDEODISPLAY("SNAPSHOT", sWindow, sBranch, sIdentity);
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;IF (lReturn == 1) THEN
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode SNAPSHOT successful - check project's TP folder!");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;ELSE
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;PRINT("VIDEODISPLAY mode SNAPSHOT failed");
 
 
 

 
&#160;&#160;END IF
 
 
 

 
END SUB

========================================
WEBBROWSER Example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 WEBBROWSER Example
 
 

Applies to
 
This example contains a set of functions to script the Web browser control using the WEBBROWSER instruction.
 
Sub NavigateTo()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lRet as Long;
 
&#160;&#160;Dim sUri as Str;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;'ARG1 is a valid Uri such as https://example.com or file://C:\MyFolder\MyFile.pdf
 
&#160;&#160;'ARG1 can also be an absolute filename such as C:/MyFolder/MyFile.pdf
 
&#160;&#160;sUri = GETARG("ARG1");
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lRet = WebBrowser("NAVIGATE", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl", sUri);
 
&#160;&#160;Print("Navigate to ", sUri, " executed with result: ",TOC(lRet));
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub NavigateBack()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lRet as Long;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lRet = WebBrowser("BACK", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl");
 
&#160;&#160;Print("Navigate back executed with result: ",TOC(lRet));
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub NavigateForward()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lRet as Long;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lRet = WebBrowser("FORWARD", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl");
 
&#160;&#160;Print("Navigate forward executed with result: ",TOC(lRet));
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub GetZoomFactor()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lRet as Long;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lRet = WebBrowser("GETZOOMFACTOR", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl");
 
&#160;&#160;Print("Zoom factor is: ",TOC(lRet));
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub GetInfo()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim sRet as Str;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;sRet = WebBrowser("GETSOURCEINFO", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl",0);
 
&#160;&#160;Print("The page Url is: ",sRet);
 
&#160;&#160;sRet = WebBrowser("GETSOURCEINFO", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl",1);
 
&#160;&#160;Print("The document title is: ",sRet);
 
&#160;&#160;sRet = WebBrowser("GETSOURCEINFO", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WrongCtrlId",1);
 
&#160;&#160;Print("The document title if the Web ctrl Id is wrong is: ",sRet);
 
End Sub
 
&#160;
 
Sub PrintToPdf()
 
&#160;&#160;Dim lRet as Long;
 
&#160;
 
&#160;&#160;lRet = WebBrowser("PRINTTOPDF", "WebBrowserMimic", "", "WebBrowserCtrl", "PrintToPdf.pdf",1);
 
&#160;&#160;Print("PrintToPdf executed with result: ",TOC(lRet));
 
End Sub

TOPIC: Webvue.htm
========================================
WEBVUE
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
WEBVUE
 

 
 

 

 

 

 See also 

Example
 
Manage the connections to WebVue clients.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Syntax
 
 
 

 1
 LIST
 1, 6
 
 

 4
 CONNECTURL
 2
 
 
 

 6
 USERNAME
 3
 
 
 

 7
 WEBVUE_CONTEXT
 5
 
 
 

 8
 MULTIMEDIA
 4
 
 
 

 9
 CONTEXT
 3
 
 
 

 10
 HYPERLINK
 7
 
 
 
 

 

 
With version 12 of PcVue, the following modes are no longer supported because they are no longer applicable:
 
 

 
SETWINDOW - Used to select the start-up window for the Web client. 
 
 

 
RESTOREWINDOW - Used to restore the configuration of the initial mimic based on the profile configuration.
 
 

 
RESTOREURL - Used to provide the user name for connection to a Url address.

 
 
 
These modes were originally designed for integration of the legacy WebVue applet into 3rd party web portals. Such integration is now realized differently, based on the REST API.
 
 The following modes are deprecated and replacement instructions are available. They are still supported at runtime for compatibility but will be removed in a later version:
 
 

 
CONNECTURL - Select the start window of the Web client.
It is replaced by the instruction WEBVUE("HYPERLINK").

 
 

 
USERNAME - Get the name of the user that started the WebVue session.
It is replaced by the instruction SYSTEM("USER").

 
 

 
WEBVUE_CONTEXT - Return the context in which the program is executed.
It is replaced by the instruction SESSION("GETTYPE").
 
 

 
CONTEXT - Return the name of the context variable when the program is run from a context window.
It is replaced by the system variable @SYSTEM.CONTEXT.NAME.
 
 
 
 
Syntax 1
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode, Handle);
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Handle
 
 
 
Identifies the list of user-station pairs of connected
 web client. Type LONG.
 
The list's format is:
 
Username1,,
 
Username2,,
 
...
 
The second field used to include information about the client host name, IP address and router address. Starting with version 12, it is always empty.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
1
 
 
 
LIST
 
 
 
Retrieve the list of web client sessions.
 
Return: Number of connected clients, or
 
-1 if the allocation for Handle is not large enough. See ALLOC_BUFFER.
 
 
 
 
Syntax 2
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode, UserName,
HtmlPage , BitVariableName, Reconnect, NewBrowser); 
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
UserName
 
 
 
Leave empty (""). Type
 STR.
 
 
 

 
 
HtmlPage 
 
 
 
The Url of the web page where to redirect the web client upon connection under the form "https://...". Type STR.
 
 
 

 
 
BitVariableName
 
 
 
A database BIT variable that is set to 1 when the connection
 is made. Type STR.
 
 
 

 
 
Reconnect
 
 
 
After disconnection: a delay before reconnection is attempted.
 Type INTEGER.
 
0: Re-connect immediately. 
1: Delay time (in seconds) after inactivity
 of mouse and keyboard.
 
 
 

 
 
NewBrowser
 
 
 
Whether to open a new web browser instance or not. Type INTEGER.
 
0: New tab in the current web browser instance.
1: New web browser instance.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
4
 
 
 
CONNECTURL
 
 
 
This mode is deprecated, use WEBVUE("HYPERLINK") instead. Select the start window of the Web client.
 
Return: 1 if successful, else 0 (variable cannot be found or incorrect type).
 
 
 
 

 
If the Url does not start with the protocol (http:// or https://), then it is considered relative by the web browser and it will redirect to the web resource relative to the /WebClient virtual directory on the WebVue web server.
 
 
Syntax 3
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode, WebSession,
UserHandle);
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
WebSession
 
 
 
Web session type. Type INTEGER. For details, see GETARG mode 29: WEB.
 
 
 

 
 
UserHandle 
 
 
 
The handle of a buffer in which the result of the instruction is returned. Type
 LONG.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
6
 
 
 
USERNAME
 
 
 
This mode is deprecated, use SYSTEM("USER") instead.
 
Get the name of the user that started the WebVue session.
 
Return: 0 if successful, else:
 
-1: WebSessionis not an INTEGER.
 
-2: WebSession is empty.
 
-3: UserHandle is not a LONG.
 
-4: UserHandle has a size of 0 (i.e. no buffer was allocated).
 
-5: UserHandle is empty. Normally this is impossible.
 
-6: UserHandle's length
 is larger than the size allocated by ALLOC_BUFFER. 
 
 
 

 9
 CONTEXT
 
 
This mode is deprecated, use the system variable @SYSTEM.CONTEXT.NAME instead. Get the name of the context variable when the program is run from a context window.
 
Return: 1 if successful, else 0.
 
 
 
 

 
Both modes return a null string in the buffer when run
 from the PcVue desktop client. You can get a list of active WebVue sessions by using mode 1 (LIST).
 
 
Syntax 4 
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode, Sub-mode);
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Sub-mode
 
 
 
The sub-mode. Type STR.
 
 
BEEP - Play an audio file.
 
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
8
 
 
 
MULTIMEDIA
 
 
 
Play a multimedia file on the WebVue client device.
 
Return: 1 if successful, else 0.
 
 
 
 

 
The operation of mode MULTIMEDIA sub-mode BEEP depends on the context of execution of the caller program
 
 

 
The audio file is played on all current WebVue clients if the caller program IS NOT a web context; for example if the caller program is running on the Web back end server, triggered by an event on a variable of scope Shared.
 
 

 
If the caller program is executed in the context of a particular WebVue session, then the audio file is played only on this web client; for example, if the program is triggered by a click on a button in a mimic displayed at the web client.
 
 
 
The ability of a WebVue client to play a multimedia file depends on the setting of the property Allow multimedia operation in the profile of the User that started the WebVue session. See the topic Adding a WebVue User in the main help.
 
It is not possible to configure the sound file played by mode MULTIMEDIA, sub-mode BEEP. However, it is possible to change the sound file manually.
 
The sound file played is called beep.wav and is located in the following folder:
 
<Installation folder>\usr\<MyProject>\WEB\audios\beep.wav
 

Changing the sound played by WEBVUE
 
 
Syntax 5
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode);
 
 
Return type: INTEGER.
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
7
 
 
 
WEBVUE_CONTEXT
 
 
 
This mode is deprecated, use SESSION("GETTYPE") instead. Return the context in which the program was executed.
 
Return: 0 when the program is executed in the context of the PcVue desktop client, else !=0 when executed on a web client.
 
 
 
 
Syntax 6
 
 IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode);
 
Return type: is INTEGER.
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
1
 
 
 
LIST
 
 
 
Retrieve the list of web client sessions in an XML fragment.
 
This information includes:
 
 

 
clientip:	The IP address of the client. If the client is not on the same network as the server it will return the IP of the router or NAT device nearest to the server.
 
 

 
clienthost: On logon a reverse DNS lookup is performed to get the host name of the client. The same limitation as for the ip applies, if the client is on a different network the host of the nearest router is shown.
 
 

 
useragent:	The full user agent string of the client. This is used to fill the data for the other nodes.
 
 

 
browser: The name of the browser (e.g., Chrome, Edge, etc).
 
 

 
os: The name of the client operating system (e.g., Windows, Mac, etc.)
 
 

 
devicetype: The type of device, either desktop or mobile.
 
 
 
 For more information, see the example below and XMLPATH.
 
 
 
 
Syntax 7
 
IntVal = WEBVUE(Mode, Url, NewTab);
 
 
Return type: is INTEGER.
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Url
 
 
 
A Url under the form "https://...". Type STR.
 
 
 

 
 
NewTab
 
 
 
Whether to open a new tab in the web browser or not. Type INTEGER.
 
0: Open in the current tab of the web browser.
1: Open in a new tab of the web browser.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Mode
 
 
 
Mnemonic
 
 
 
Action
 
 
 

 
 
10
 
 
 
HYPERLINK
 
 
 
Redirect the calling web client to the web page defined by Url.
 
Return: 1 if successful, else 0.
 
 
 
 

 
If the Url does not start with the protocol (http:// or https://), then it is considered relative by the web browser and it will open the web resource relative to the /WebClient virtual directory of the WebVue web server.
 
 
Example
 
For an example, select the Example link above.


========================================
TOPIC: WEBVUE_Example.htm
========================================
WEBVUE example
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
WEBVUE Examples
 

 
 

 

 

 Applies to
 
 
Example 1 - Data returned by WEBVUE mode LIST - Syntax 1
 
The following example uses the mode LIST (syntax 1 text-based) to list all users connected via a Web
 client.
 
Sub ListSyntax1()
 
Dim iRet as Integer;
 
Dim strClientList as Str;
 
Dim hBuff as Long;
 
&#160;
 
hBuff = ALLOC_BUFFER(5000); 'Allocate a buffer of 5 kb
 
iRet = Webvue("LIST", hBuff);	'Retrieve the list of sessions
 
Print("Mode LIST Syntax 1 executed with result: ", iRet);
 
&#160;
 
if (iRet == -1) then	'List retrieval failed
 
Print("List retrieval failed - Memory buffer is too small");
 
else	'List retrieval is successful
 
strClientList = CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255);
 
Print("Number of web clients: ", iRet);
 
Print("List of web clients: ", strClientList);
 
end if
 
&#160;
 
FREE_BUFFER(hBuff);
 
End Sub
 
Example 2 - Data returned by WEBVUE mode LIST - Syntax 6
 
The following example using the mode LIST (syntax 6 XML-based) to list all users connected via a Web
 client.
 
Typical XML fragment returned by mode LIST - Syntax 6
 
<list myid="767565456788">
 
<session>
 
id="535625422312"
 
creationtime=xxxxxxx
 
username="User01"
 
clienttype="webvue"
 
clientip:xxx.xxx.x.xxx
 
clienthost: MM-MBP.local
 
useragent: Mozilla/5(Maintosh; intel Mac OS X 10_15_7)AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/15.3 Safari/
 
browser: Safari
 
os: Mac
 
devicetype: Desktop
 
</session>
 
</list>
 
Script to analyze the returned data
 
SUB LISTWEBVUESESSIONS()
 
DIM iRet as Integer;
 
DIM hBuff as Long;
 
DIM i As Integer;
 
DIM iSessionCount As Integer;
 
DIM sPath As Str;
 
&#160;
 
Webvue("LIST"); 'Retrieve the list of sessions
 
Print("Mode LIST Syntax 6 executed");
 
&#160;
 
iSessionCount = XMLPATH("COUNT","webvue/list","list/session"); 'Retrieve the list of sessions in the XML fragment
 
Print(iSessionCount, " Web session(s) currently active.");
 
&#160;
 
hBuff = XMLPATH("GET","webvue/list","list.myid");
 
If (hBuff != 0 ) Then
 
Print("myid - The session Id of the script caller is: ",CGET_BUFFER ( hBuff, 0, 255 ));
 
End If
 
&#160;
 
If (iSessionCount>0) then
 
For (i=1; i<=iSessionCount;i++) 'Iterate the collection of session elements in the XML fragment
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].id", i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH( "GET", "webvue/list", sPath );
 
If (hBuff != 0) Then
 
Print("Web session number ",i);
 
Print("\tid: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].creationtime", i);
 
Print("\tcreationtime: ",DATETIMESTRING(XMLPATH("GETDOUBLE","webvue/list",sPath)));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].username",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH("GET","webvue/list",sPath );
 
Print("\tusername: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].clienttype",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tclienttype: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].clientip",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tclientip: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].clienthost",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tclienthost: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].useragent",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tuseragent: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].browser",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tbrowser: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].os",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tos: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
&#160;
 
sPath = FORMAT("list.session[%d].devicetype",i);
 
hBuff = XMLPATH ("GET","webvue/list",sPath);
 
Print("\tdevicetype: ",CGET_BUFFER(hBuff,0,255));
 
End If
 
Next
 
End If
 
End Sub

TOPIC: Welcome.htm
========================================
About this documentation
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
About this documentationThis documentation is for PcVue 16.3.
There is more to the documentation than meets the eye. This topic gives an overview of the conventions used in this documentation as well as the powerful search tools you can use to find the information you need.
The documentation contains information that helps you navigate the product installation, configuration, deployment and troubleshooting. It includes explanation about the HMI workspace, the Application Explorer, the Application Architect, the Smart Generators, SCADA Basic scripting and deployment tools. 
It also contains detailed information about the Add-ons and the reference for those VBA instructions that are specific to PcVue.
The Microsoft VBA reference help is integrated in the VBA environment and can be displayed using function key F1 when the VBA Editor is open.
The technical resources for PcVue SDK & API are available separately when you choose to install them.
Style conventions of this documentation
A bulleted list is used for a list of items that has no particular order, and for lists of tab options.
A numbered list is used for a list that has a particular order, such as how to do something.
Text in a mono-spaced font and with a background color is used for 
file fragments and data lists.
Show picture 
 indicates a place where you can click to display a picture about the topic.

The following icons are used to signify:

An essential note.

A warning.

A tip.
Help workspace
The contents tab
The help Contents tab offers a hierarchical view of sub-books and topics. Each sub-book relates to either a workflow such as Installation or to a product feature such as the Application Explorer.
Feel free to drill down to find the sub-book and topics relevant to what you are looking for.
The Index tab
The help Index contains a list of all the dialog boxes, tabs, toolbars and files mentioned in the help. If you want help about a particular tab, dialog, toolbar of file just type in the first few characters of the title in the search box at the top of the Index tab.
Basic search features
You can use the search tab to find information in the help system. The search string can use letters and numbers. The letters are not case-sensitive. Punctuation marks are ignored except when within quotations. However, you cannot search for quotation marks.
Using the drop-down arrow adjacent to the search field, you can re-run or adapt previous searches.
You can search for words using a simple wildcard, question mark, and asterisk, mixed with other characters.
To search forExampleTopics containing
A single wordvisibleThe word 'visible' (also its grammatical variants such as 'visibility').
A phrase"register variable"The phrase 'register variable' and its grammatical variants such as "register variables".
Wildcard expressionsgen*Terms starting with 'gen' such as 'genuine', 'general', 'generic', 'generate', 'generator' etc.
&#160;gener??Terms with any character in that position such as 'general' and 'generic'.
The Search tab includes some additional controls known as the Advanced Search Controls to help you refine your search. Show picture

ControlActionExample
Search previous resultsLimit the search to the results of the previous search&#160;
Match similar wordsFind words that match a keyword or only differ in having a commonly used suffix'add' will match 'added' or 'adding' but not 'additive'.
Search titles onlyOnly the titles of the topics will be searched&#160;
Advanced search features
Using logical search expressions
The logical operators AND, OR, NOT and NEAR enable you to define your search precisely by relating several search terms. You can type them directly or insert them using the right arrow button adjacent to the search field.
Logical search expressions are evaluated left to right unless they contain parentheses (see below). The following table shows how you can use each of these operators.
To search forExampleTopics containing
Both terms in the same topicalarm AND #tBoth words 'alarm' and '#t'.
Either term in a topicalarm OR #tThe word 'alarm' or the word '#t' or both.
The first term without the second term.alarm NOT #tThe word 'alarm' but not the word '#t'.
Both terms in the same topic, close together.alarm NEAR #tThe word 'alarm' and the word '#t' within 8 words of each other.
 If no operator is specified, AND is implied. For example, the query 'alarm display window' is equivalent to 'alarm AND display AND window'.
Using nested expressions
Nested expressions use parentheses '(...)' to construct complex searches.
The basic rules for searching help topics using nested expressions are as follows:
The expressions in parentheses are evaluated before the rest of the query.
The query is then evaluated from left to right.
You cannot nest expressions more than five levels deep, e.g. '(level_1...(level_2...(level_3...(level_4...(level_5...)))))'.
As you add levels and logical operators, a query quickly becomes unmanageable. It is simpler to employ the option to search previous results, as described above in the Basic search feature.
ExampleOperatorsTopics containing
alarm AND ((display OR window) NEAR select)'AND', 'OR', 'NEAR', '(...)'The word 'alarm' along with the words 'display' and 'select' close together; or 'alarm' along with 'window' and 'select' close together.
alarm NOT display OR window'NOT', 'OR'; left-to-right ruleThe word 'alarm' without the word 'display'; or the word 'window'.
alarm NOT (display OR window)'NOT', 'OR', '(...)'The word 'alarm' without either of the words 'display' or 'window'.
For further information
This summary was adapted from Microsoft's Help for Help Viewer. The latest version is available from the HTML Help Downloads page of the Microsoft Developer Network (MSDN) website: https://msdn.microsoft.com/library/ms669985.aspx.
Readers looking for further information should download HelpDocs.zip, which contains Viewhlp.chm, the help for Help Viewer. This covers accessibility features, shortcut keys, keeping track of your favorite topics, customizing a Help window and using an input method editor (IME) for text entry in East Asian languages.


========================================
TOPIC: What_is_a_DirectX_model_.htm
========================================
What is a 3D DirectX model?
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
What is a 3D DirectX Model?
 
A 3D DirectX model is a file that contains information 
 to render a 3D drawing. It can represent anything from a simple 3D shape 
 such as a sphere to a highly complex drawing such as an entire building. 
 
 
Whatever it represents, a 3D model contains the following 
 information.
 
 
Frames
 
Pivot
 
Meshes
 
Materials
 
 
Frames
 
A frame is a container that applies geometric transformation 
 (position, rotation and scaling) to its contents. A frame contains a pivot 
 about which rotation takes places, and either a single mesh, other frames 
 (known as child or sub frames) or both.
 
A frame becomes a child frame when it is linked to a 
 (parent) frame during the model design. A child frame does not have to 
 be contained with the bounds of the parent frame. As well as its own transformation, 
 a child frame inherits any transformation of its parent frame, moving 
 relative to the parent frames' pivot.
 
Pivot
 
The pivot is the point about which rotation takes place. 
 The position of the pivot determines the model or frames location within 
 a 3D mimic. Each model has a pivot point and each frame and child frame 
 within a model has a pivot point.
 
Meshes
 
A mesh provides the surface geometry and is made up of 
 a collection of faces (polygons) bound together. A mesh is always contained 
 within a frame. A mesh references one or more materials. 
 
Materials
 
A material provides the visual representation of the 
 surface created by a mesh. The definition of a material may be contained 
 within the mesh it is used or within the model. A material that is defined 
 within the model can be used by several meshes.
 
Lighting
 
A major difference from other 3D applications is that 
 there are no explicit light sources, or reflections, in the HMI. Even 
 though any object can be configured with a glowing color, it cannot illuminate 
 other objects, nor can it reflect them in a surface of its material.


========================================
TOPIC: What_is_a_material.htm
========================================
What is a 3D material?
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
What is a 3D Material?
 
A material is a collection of properties that define 
 how the surface of a mesh is represented visually. A material is composed 
 of the following properties. The HMI column indicates whether the property 
 is available in the HMI's 3D Properties dialog.
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
Property
 
 
 
HMI
 
 
 
Description
 
 
 

 
 
Diffuse color
 
 
 
Yes
 
 
 
The diffuse color is the color of the light reflected 
 from a surface under pure white light, but it is usually defined as the 
 color of the object itself.
 
 
 

 
 
Specular color
 
 
 
Yes
 
 
 
The specular color is used for the highlights. Together 
 with the specular level, it controls the shininess of a surface. The specular 
 color is usually configured with a brighter version of the diffuse color. 
 A black specular color will make a surface appear matt.
 
 
 

 
 
Specular level
 
 
 
Yes
 
 
 
The specular level controls the sharpness of the highlights. 
 A larger value will contract the highlight areas and render the surface 
 shinier. Smaller values stretch the highlight areas and render the surface 
 duller. If the specular color is black, the specular level is unused.
 
 
 

 
 
Ambient color
 
 
 
No
 
 
 
The color of the light reflected from a surface that 
 is lighted.
 
 
 

 
 
Emissive color
 
 
 
Yes
 
 
 
The emissive color is similar to the ambient color except 
 that the surface does not require any light. In a scene with no light, 
 a surface defined with an emissive color other than black can be seen. 
 In a scene with light, it is usually applied so as to adjust the overall brightness 
 of an object surface.
 
 
 

 
 
Opacity
 
 
 
Yes
 
 
 
Controls the transparency of the surface. Also known as 
 Alpha.
 
 
 

 
 
Texture
 
 
 
No
 
 
 
A texture is an image mapped onto the surface. The final 
 visual representation depends on the surface material attributes (colors 
 and opacity) and the lighting. 
 
 
 
 

 
Diffuse, specular and emissive colors are defined in 
 the HMI as RGB colors (red-green-blue). 
 
 
Local and Indexed materials
 
In a 3D model, there can be two types of material, according 
 to where its properties are declared.
 
 
Local - The properties 
 of the material are configured with, and only available to, a particular 
 mesh.
 
Indexed - The properties 
 of the material are configured as part of the model and are available 
 to any mesh in the model.
 
 
A mesh can use a local or indexed material, or both. 
 When using an indexed material, the mesh references the material's name.


========================================
TOPIC: WHILE_WEND.htm
========================================
WHILE...WEND
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
WHILE...WEND
 

 
 

 

 

 See also
 
 
Execute a series of instructions in a loop while a given 
 condition remains true.
 
WebVue support - Yes.
 
Syntax
 
WHILE(Expression)
 
[block of instructions]
 
WEND
 
 
 
 

 
 
Argument
 
 
 
Meaning
 
 
 

 
 
Expression
 
 
 
A boolean condition.
 
 
 
 
Execution
 
 
If the Expression 
 is true, all instructions included between WHILE and WEND are executed, 
 then the Expression is evaluated again.
 
If the Expression 
 is still true, the process is repeated.
 
If the Expression 
 is false, execution continues at the instruction following WEND.
 
 

 
Nesting is possible within the limits of the available resources.
 
 
Example
 
SUB Main()
 
DIM i as Integer;
 
&#160;
 
i=0;
 
WHILE (i<3) 'so long as i<3
 
&#160;&#160;i++; 'increment i by 1
 
&#160;&#160;PRINT(i);
 
WEND
 
END SUB
